scenarios.title: Brute Force Against RDP

briefing.subject: Lockout alerts on a finance workstation
briefing.body: Service desk is seeing repeated logon failures targeting the local Administrator account on a finance machine exposed through a misconfigured firewall rule. Confirm whether this is a brute force attempt, decide if it succeeded, and escalate if needed.
objectives: Validate the alert as true or false positive
Determine if any logon succeeded
Recommend an escalation decision

iocs.note: Source of brute force, foreign ASN

steps.prompt: What confirms this is a brute force and not a typo storm?
options: High volume of 4625 from one external IP at machine speed
A single 4625 event
User opened an email
steps.rationale: Brute force shows a high rate of failures from one source, faster than human typing.

steps.prompt: First containment action?
options: Block the source IP and remove the public RDP exposure
Reimage the workstation
Reset every user password
steps.rationale: Stop the exposure and the source first. RDP should never face the internet.

questions.prompt: Did the attacker eventually authenticate?
options: Yes, a 4624 success followed the failures
No, all attempts failed
questions.explain: Event 4624 from the same external IP after the 4625 storm means the brute force succeeded.

questions.prompt: Which control would have prevented internet exposure?
options: RDP behind VPN or gateway with MFA
A longer password only
Antivirus
questions.explain: Network exposure is the root issue. RDP belongs behind a gateway with MFA.

hints: Sort the SIEM by source IP and watch the failure rate.
Look for a 4624 success near the end of the failure run.
The attacker IP appears in Threat Intel flagged malicious.

answerKey.rootCause: Internet-exposed RDP brute forced to a successful Administrator logon.

scenarios.debrief: A strong Tier 1 analyst confirms the pattern, checks for success, and escalates the moment a 4624 appears. The lesson: failures matter less than the one success.

scenarios.title: User-Reported Phishing Email

briefing.subject: Reported phish in the billing department
briefing.body: A billing employee forwarded a suspicious mailbox-verification email. Determine if it is malicious, whether anyone clicked, and what indicators to add to the blocklist.
objectives: Assess the email and its link
Check for any click or follow-on activity
Extract indicators for blocking

steps.prompt: Best indicator that this is phishing?
options: Look-alike sender domain with a credential-harvest link
It mentions the company name
It has an attachment icon
steps.rationale: A look-alike domain plus a login link is the classic credential phish signature.

steps.prompt: What protects other users fastest?
options: Block the domain and URL, then purge the message tenant-wide
Email everyone to be careful
Nothing, only one person got it
steps.rationale: Block and purge stops the campaign across all mailboxes.

questions.prompt: Did the SIEM show evidence of a successful click leading to compromise?
options: No confirmed post-click compromise in scope
Yes, ransomware detonated
questions.explain: This scenario stops at delivery. The skill is recognizing a phish before it escalates.

questions.prompt: Which two indicators belong on the blocklist?
options: Phishing domain
Phishing URL
The reporter's mailbox
outlook.office365.com
questions.explain: Block the adversary domain and URL. Never block legitimate infrastructure.

hints: Filter SIEM source to syslog and read the mail gateway lines.
Compare the sender domain to bvhn.org carefully.
The domain and URL are both in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: Credential phishing email delivered, no confirmed compromise.

scenarios.debrief: Good triage separates delivery from compromise. Reporting a phish before anyone clicks is a win, not an incident.

scenarios.title: Outbound Port Scan Detected

briefing.subject: Firewall noise from the server VLAN
briefing.body: The perimeter firewall is logging a burst of denied connections fanning across the server subnet. Decide whether this is reconnaissance, where it originates, and how to respond.
objectives: Classify the activity
Identify the source
Choose a proportionate response

steps.prompt: What pattern marks a horizontal scan?
options: One source touching many hosts on the same ports
One host on one port repeatedly
Normal web browsing
steps.rationale: Horizontal scanning sweeps many destinations on the same ports.

questions.prompt: Is the scan internal or external in origin?
options: External public IP
Internal workstation
questions.explain: The source is a public IP hitting the server VLAN from outside.

questions.prompt: Were the scanned ports allowed or denied?
options: Denied by the firewall
Allowed through
questions.explain: All scan attempts show DENY, so the perimeter held. Monitor and block the source.

hints: Filter to the firewall source and group by destination.
Count how many distinct destinations one source touched.
Check the action field: ALLOW or DENY.

answerKey.rootCause: External reconnaissance scan, blocked at perimeter.

scenarios.debrief: Not every alert is an incident. A blocked scan is reconnaissance to log and block, not a crisis. Proportionate response matters.

scenarios.title: Ransomware Detonation and Containment

briefing.subject: URGENT: files encrypting on the file server
briefing.body: A finance user opened a macro document. We now see encryption on the main file share. You own this incident end to end. Contain it, identify the chain, and deliver a post-mortem. Speed matters, but so does evidence.
objectives: Reconstruct the attack chain
Contain before full encryption
Document IOCs and a recovery path

steps.prompt: First containment action?
options: Network-isolate the file server and patient-zero host
Pay the ransom
Reboot the file server
steps.rationale: Isolation stops spread and preserves evidence. Rebooting can destroy volatile data.

steps.prompt: Next, to stop the foothold?
options: Block the C2 IP and domain at the firewall and proxy
Delete the ransom note
Email the staff
steps.rationale: Cut command and control so the operator loses live access.

steps.prompt: How do you remove persistence?
options: Identify and remove malicious processes, services, and scheduled tasks on affected hosts
Run antivirus once and assume done
Change the wallpaper
steps.rationale: Hunt persistence on every touched host, not just the loud one.

steps.prompt: Safest restore approach?
options: Restore from clean offline backups after confirming eradication
Decrypt with the attacker tool
Restore immediately, eradicate later
steps.rationale: Restore only after eradication, and only from backups the attacker could not reach.

questions.prompt: What was the initial access vector?
options: Macro-enabled phishing document
Exposed RDP
USB drop
Drive-by download
questions.explain: The chain starts at a phishing email and a Word macro spawning PowerShell.

questions.prompt: Which ATT&CK technique covers the encryption stage?
options: T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact
T1046 Service Discovery
T1566 Phishing
questions.explain: Mass encryption maps to T1486, the Impact tactic.

questions.prompt: Select every confirmed IOC to block.
options: C2 IP
C2 domain
Ransomware hash
outlook.office365.com
questions.explain: Block the C2 and the malware hash. Never the legitimate mail service.

hints: Read the SIEM top to bottom: email, macro, PowerShell, beacon, SMB, encryption.
The C2 beacon repeats on a fixed interval. That periodicity is the tell.
Cross-check the C2 IP and malware hash in Threat Intel for confirmation.

answerKey.rootCause: Phishing macro to PowerShell loader to C2 to SMB lateral movement to mass encryption.
containment: Isolate file server and patient zero

scenarios.debrief: The flagship incident. Strong responders isolate fast, cut C2, hunt persistence on every host, and restore only from clean backups. The post-mortem should read as a clear timeline a CISO can brief to leadership.

scenarios.title: Credential Phishing to Account Takeover

briefing.subject: Suspicious sign-in and inbox rule
briefing.body: A finance account signed in from an unusual location, then an inbox rule appeared that forwards and hides messages. This looks like account takeover after a phish. Confirm the chain and lock the attacker out without losing evidence.
objectives: Confirm the takeover
Identify attacker persistence in the mailbox
Revoke access and contain

steps.prompt: Most urgent action on the account?
options: Revoke active sessions and force re-auth, then reset the password
Delete the mailbox
Only reset the password
steps.rationale: Revoking tokens kills the live session. A password reset alone leaves stolen tokens valid.

steps.prompt: What attacker persistence must be removed?
options: The malicious inbox rule and any consented OAuth app
The user's signature
Nothing
steps.rationale: Inbox rules and OAuth grants survive a password reset. Remove both.

questions.prompt: Why is a password reset alone insufficient here?
options: Stolen session tokens and OAuth grants stay valid
Passwords never matter
The user likes the password
questions.explain: Modern takeover persists via tokens and app consent. Revoke sessions and remove grants.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the attacker inbox rule?
options: Hide replies and harvest finance threads quietly
Improve productivity
Forward spam only
questions.explain: The rule forwards and deletes finance messages so the victim never sees the fraud.

hints: Trace the order: phish, foreign login, inbox rule, OAuth consent.
Note the sign-in geo against the user's normal location.
Persistence here is the inbox rule plus the consented app.

answerKey.rootCause: Phishing led to account takeover with token theft, inbox rule, and OAuth persistence.

scenarios.debrief: Identity is the new perimeter. The lesson is that resetting a password is not containment. You must kill tokens and remove cloud persistence.

scenarios.title: Data Exfiltration over HTTPS

briefing.subject: Large outbound transfer overnight
briefing.body: A clinical workstation pushed an unusually large volume of data to an unfamiliar host overnight. Given we hold patient records, treat possible PHI loss seriously. Confirm exfiltration, scope it, and contain.
objectives: Confirm and size the exfiltration
Identify the destination
Contain and assess data-loss impact

steps.prompt: Fastest way to stop ongoing loss?
options: Block the destination and isolate the source host
Wait for morning
Email the user
steps.rationale: Stop the transfer immediately by blocking the destination and isolating the host.

steps.prompt: Why does the data type matter here?
options: PHI loss triggers HIPAA breach assessment and notification duties
It does not
Only credit cards matter
steps.rationale: Healthcare data loss carries legal notification obligations. Scope what left.

questions.prompt: Roughly how much data left the network?
options: Hundreds of megabytes and climbing
A few kilobytes
Nothing measurable
questions.explain: Summing the transfers shows hundreds of MB, a serious volume for record data.

questions.prompt: What makes HTTPS exfiltration hard to spot?
options: The payload is encrypted, so volume and destination are the main signals
It is always blocked
It uses port 23
questions.explain: Content is hidden by TLS. Analysts rely on volume, destination, and timing.

hints: Filter firewall logs by bytes and sort descending.
Add up the transfer sizes to one destination.
The destination domain and IP are flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: Staged clinical data exfiltrated over HTTPS to an external dropzone.

scenarios.debrief: Encrypted exfiltration hides content but not behavior. Volume, destination reputation, and timing are your signals. In healthcare, scoping the data is as important as stopping it.

scenarios.title: Hunt: C2 Beacons by Periodicity

briefing.subject: Hunt request: find the quiet beacon
briefing.body: No alert fired, but I want you to hunt. Somewhere in the egress traffic a host is calling home on a regular interval, hiding in normal-looking TLS. Find the beacon by its rhythm, not by a signature.
objectives: Form a hypothesis about beaconing
Find the periodic callback
Confirm the C2 and the beaconing host

steps.prompt: What signal best reveals a beacon?
options: Regular interval between connections to one destination
A single large download
A 404 error
steps.rationale: Beaconing shows time regularity. The interval is the hunt signal, not the payload.

steps.prompt: Which second channel confirms covert C2?
options: High-entropy DNS TXT queries to the same actor domain
Normal web browsing
A printer log
steps.rationale: DNS tunneling alongside the TLS beacon strengthens the C2 finding.

questions.prompt: What is the approximate beacon interval?
options: About every 10 to 12 seconds in sim time
Once a day
Random with no pattern
questions.explain: The repeat fires on a fixed cadence. Spotting that cadence is the hunt skill.

questions.prompt: Why is a hunt different from alert triage?
options: It is hypothesis-driven with no prior alert
It only happens after a breach
It needs no evidence
questions.explain: Hunting starts from a hypothesis and searches proactively, before any alert.

hints: There is no alert. Start from the hypothesis that something beacons.
Group outbound TLS by destination and look at time gaps.
A second covert channel uses DNS TXT records.

answerKey.rootCause: Undetected host beaconing over TLS with a DNS tunneling backup channel.

scenarios.debrief: Threat hunting rewards patience and pattern thinking. The beacon hides in plain sight. Its rhythm gives it away, which is why time analysis beats signature matching.

scenarios.title: Hunt: Living off the Land Binary Abuse

briefing.subject: Hunt request: trusted tools doing untrusted things
briefing.body: Attackers increasingly avoid malware by abusing built-in Windows binaries. Hunt for trusted tools used in untrusted ways, such as certutil or mshta reaching out to the internet. Find the abuse and the operator behind it.
objectives: Hunt for LOLBin abuse
Tie the activity to a host and user
Confirm the remote payload source

steps.prompt: Why are LOLBins effective for attackers?
options: They are trusted and signed, so they evade naive allowlists
They are rare
They need admin always
steps.rationale: Built-in signed tools blend in and bypass simple application controls.

steps.prompt: What turns a benign tool into a finding?
options: Unusual command line and outbound network from the tool
The tool existing on disk
A normal user opening Word
steps.rationale: Context matters. certutil fetching a remote file is the abuse signal.

questions.prompt: Which behavior flagged certutil as malicious?
options: It downloaded a remote file via -urlcache
It signed a certificate
It printed help text
questions.explain: certutil -urlcache pulling a remote payload is a classic LOLBin download cradle.

questions.prompt: Which detection approach catches this best?
options: Behavioral process and command-line monitoring
File hash blocklists only
Disabling logging
questions.explain: Hashes will not catch trusted binaries. Behavior and command lines will.

hints: Hunt EventID 4688 for trusted tools with odd command lines.
Look for certutil, mshta, or bitsadmin reaching the internet.
The payload domain matches the C2 in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: Adversary used signed Windows binaries to fetch payloads and run encoded PowerShell.

scenarios.debrief: Modern intrusions hide inside trusted tools. The hunt skill is reading intent from command lines and network behavior, not from file reputation.

scenarios.title: S3 Bucket Public Exposure and Data Leak

briefing.subject: Patient export bucket may be public
briefing.body: A change in our AWS account set a patient-export S3 bucket to public-read, and we see external access from a foreign IP. This is potentially a major PHI breach. Confirm the exposure, stop it, and start the breach assessment.
objectives: Confirm the public exposure
Identify who changed the ACL and from where
Contain and begin HIPAA breach scoping

steps.prompt: First action on the bucket?
options: Revert ACL to private and enable account-wide public-access block
Delete the bucket
Leave it and investigate slowly
steps.rationale: Make it private now and turn on the public-access block so it cannot recur.

steps.prompt: What about the abused credentials?
options: Disable the access key and rotate credentials
Nothing
Email the vendor
steps.rationale: Disable and rotate the key used to make the change.

steps.prompt: Why escalate to compliance immediately?
options: Exposed PHI triggers HIPAA breach notification timelines
It is only IT's problem
No need
steps.rationale: PHI exposure starts a regulated clock. Legal and compliance must engage early.

questions.prompt: What single change created the exposure?
options: PutBucketAcl set public-read
A user opened email
A firewall rule
questions.explain: The CloudTrail PutBucketAcl event set the bucket public-read.

questions.prompt: Which discipline must be engaged beyond IT?
options: Legal and compliance for HIPAA
Marketing
Facilities
questions.explain: PHI breaches are legal events. Compliance and legal own notification.

hints: Read CloudTrail for ACL and login events.
Find the PutBucketAcl with public-read.
Tie the foreign login and the ACL change to one access key.

answerKey.rootCause: Compromised cloud credential set a PHI bucket public, enabling external data access.

scenarios.debrief: Cloud incidents move at API speed. The lesson blends technical containment with the reality that healthcare data exposure is a legal and compliance event from minute one.

scenarios.title: AiTM Token Theft and Session Hijack

briefing.subject: MFA was satisfied but the login looks wrong
briefing.body: We have a sign-in that passed MFA yet originated from a hostile location moments after a normal session here. This pattern fits adversary-in-the-middle token theft, where the attacker replays a stolen session. MFA alone did not stop it. Investigate and contain.
objectives: Recognize token replay despite MFA
Confirm the hijacked session
Contain identity and remove persistence

steps.prompt: Why did MFA not prevent this?
options: The attacker proxied the login and stole the post-MFA session token
MFA was off
The user shared a code by accident only
steps.rationale: AiTM relays the whole auth flow and captures the session token after MFA succeeds.

steps.prompt: Strongest containment for token theft?
options: Revoke all sessions and require re-auth, then remove added persistence
Reset password only
Do nothing, MFA covered it
steps.rationale: Revoke tokens to invalidate the stolen session, then strip rules and OAuth grants.

questions.prompt: What defense most reduces AiTM risk?
options: Phishing-resistant MFA such as FIDO2 passkeys
Longer passwords
Email banners only
questions.explain: Hardware-bound, phishing-resistant MFA cannot be relayed by a proxy page.

questions.prompt: Which artifacts show post-takeover persistence?
options: Malicious inbox rule
OAuth app consent
A normal calendar event
outlook.office365.com traffic
questions.explain: The inbox rule and OAuth grant are the attacker's persistence.

hints: Note that MFA shows satisfied yet the geo is hostile.
Token replay means session revocation, not just a password reset.
Persistence again is the inbox rule plus the OAuth app.

answerKey.rootCause: Adversary-in-the-middle phishing stole a session token, bypassing MFA, then added mailbox and OAuth persistence.

scenarios.debrief: This is the modern identity attack students must understand. MFA is necessary but not sufficient. Phishing-resistant factors and fast token revocation are the real defenses.

scenarios.title: Malware Beacon on a Workstation

briefing.subject: EDR flagged a document spawning PowerShell
briefing.body: Endpoint protection raised an alert that Word launched PowerShell on a clinician workstation, and the firewall now shows steady outbound TLS. Confirm whether this is a real infection, trace the chain, and decide on isolation.
objectives: Confirm the malicious process chain
Identify the C2 destination
Decide on host isolation

steps.prompt: Which evidence confirms a real infection, not a false positive?
options: winword.exe spawning powershell, then encoded download, then periodic beaconing
A single antivirus popup
One DNS query to office365
steps.rationale: The full chain from document to beacon is the signal. One alert alone is not.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Network-isolate the host and block the C2, preserving memory
Power the machine off immediately
Email the user to reboot
steps.rationale: Isolation cuts the attacker while preserving volatile evidence. Powering off destroys memory.

questions.prompt: What is the beacon interval to the C2?
options: About 60 seconds
About 1 hour
Random with no pattern
questions.explain: The firewall lines to the C2 are evenly spaced about 60 seconds apart, the classic beacon tell.

questions.prompt: What started the infection?
options: A macro-enabled document executed by the user
A brute force on RDP
A public S3 bucket
questions.explain: The chain begins with winword.exe spawning powershell, which is macro-driven user execution.

hints: Look at the parent and child process in the 4688 events.
Group firewall connections to the C2 and measure the gaps.
The C2 domain and hash are both in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: Macro-enabled document executed a PowerShell download cradle that established a periodic C2 beacon.

scenarios.debrief: Tier 1 confirms the chain rather than reacting to a single popup. The macro-to-beacon pattern is the most common malware path students will see.

scenarios.title: SQL Injection on the Patient Portal

briefing.subject: Odd query strings hitting the patient portal
briefing.body: The web logs for the patient portal show automated requests with SQL fragments in the query string, and one response was far larger than normal. Determine if an injection succeeded, what was targeted, and how to remediate.
objectives: Confirm the attack type
Determine if any request succeeded
Recommend a permanent fix

steps.prompt: What is the attack and the target?
options: SQL injection against the patients table
A port scan of the web VLAN
A phishing email
steps.rationale: The UNION SELECT payload reading from the patients table is textbook SQL injection.

steps.prompt: Which fix is permanent, not just a stopgap?
options: Parameterized queries in the application code
A WAF rule only
Rebooting the server
steps.rationale: A WAF rule is a temporary virtual patch. Parameterized queries remove the vulnerability.

questions.prompt: Did any injection attempt succeed?
options: Yes, one request returned a 200 with a large body
No, every attempt was blocked
questions.explain: The 200 response with an unusually large size means rows were returned to the attacker.

questions.prompt: Which two responses are the right remediation?
options: Parameterized queries
Block the source and add a WAF virtual patch
Disable logging
Email the developers a warning only
questions.explain: Block and virtual-patch to stop the bleeding, then fix the code with parameterized queries.

hints: Read the query string after q=. Look for UNION SELECT.
Compare the response sizes. The big 200 is the success.
The source User-Agent names the tool.

answerKey.rootCause: Unparameterized query in the patient portal allowed UNION-based SQL injection that read the patients table.

scenarios.debrief: This builds the Vulnerability Management muscle. Students learn to separate temporary mitigations from permanent fixes and to scope what data an injection touched.

scenarios.title: Impossible Travel Sign-in

briefing.subject: A user signed in from two places at once
briefing.body: Identity protection flagged a user with two successful sign-ins minutes apart from locations too far to travel between. Decide whether this is impossible travel, and choose a first containment step.
objectives: Confirm impossible travel
Rule out a benign explanation
Choose a first response

steps.prompt: What makes this impossible travel?
options: Two successful sign-ins from distant locations within minutes
Two failed logons
A single login from the office
steps.rationale: Two successes too far apart in time to be the same person traveling is the definition.

steps.prompt: Best first containment?
options: Revoke the user sessions and require re-authentication
Send the user a reminder email
Ignore it, MFA passed
steps.rationale: Revoke sessions first. MFA satisfied does not rule out a stolen token.

questions.prompt: Could a VPN fully explain this as benign?
options: No, the second location is a known hostile geo and the timing is impossible
Yes, always assume VPN
questions.explain: A corporate VPN would not place the user in a hostile foreign geo seconds later. Treat as suspicious.

questions.prompt: What should you check next for persistence?
options: New inbox rules and OAuth grants on the account
The printer queue
CPU temperature
questions.explain: Account takeovers commonly add inbox rules and OAuth grants to maintain access.

hints: Compare the two sign-in geos and their timestamps.
MFA satisfied does not equal safe.
Revoke sessions, then hunt for persistence.

answerKey.rootCause: Possible account takeover indicated by impossible travel between the office and a hostile geo.

scenarios.debrief: A clean Tier 1 identity triage. The skill is rejecting the easy benign story when the geo and timing do not support it.

scenarios.title: Web Shell and Server Persistence

briefing.subject: Our web server is running shell commands
briefing.body: The public web server is showing its application pool process launching command shells. That points to a web shell dropped after an exploit. Trace the entry, find the shell, and remove the foothold.
objectives: Confirm the web shell
Locate the persistence
Contain and eradicate

steps.prompt: What single event most indicates a web shell?
options: w3wp.exe spawning cmd.exe
A user opening Excel
A successful health check
steps.rationale: The web server application pool should never spawn an interactive shell. That is a web shell.

steps.prompt: What removes the foothold properly?
options: Delete the uploaded shell, patch the entry vulnerability, and rotate server secrets
Restart the web service only
Block one IP and move on
steps.rationale: The shell, the entry hole, and any stolen secrets must all be addressed or the attacker returns.

questions.prompt: Where is the web shell located?
options: An uploaded file such as uploads/cmd.aspx
In the DNS server
In a printer driver
questions.explain: The spawning of cmd from w3wp traces to an attacker-uploaded script in the web root.

questions.prompt: Why is isolating the server not enough by itself?
options: The entry vulnerability and any stolen secrets remain unless remediated
Isolation always fixes everything
The server cannot be reinfected
questions.explain: Without patching the entry and rotating secrets, the attacker can return through the same hole.

hints: Look for a server process spawning cmd.exe.
The exploit request shows how they got in.
Eradication means shell plus entry plus secrets.

answerKey.rootCause: Exploited public-facing application let the attacker upload a web shell and run commands for persistence.

scenarios.debrief: Web shells are a top persistence technique. The lesson is that containment is not eradication. The hole and the secrets matter as much as the shell.

scenarios.title: Insider Data Theft

briefing.subject: A departing employee is touching a lot of PHI
briefing.body: An employee on their notice period bulk-accessed patient files and the firewall shows a large upload to a personal cloud service. Determine intent, contain the data loss, and preserve evidence for HR and legal.
objectives: Confirm the insider activity
Identify where the data went
Contain and preserve evidence

steps.prompt: What distinguishes this from an external breach?
options: A valid internal account is doing the access and upload
An external brute force
A public bucket
steps.rationale: The actor is a legitimate user abusing authorized access. That changes the response and the stakeholders.

steps.prompt: Best containment that also protects the case?
options: Disable the account, block the upload destination, and preserve logs for HR and legal
Confront the employee directly
Delete the logs to avoid panic
steps.rationale: Stop the loss and preserve evidence. HR and legal own the personnel side. Never destroy evidence.

questions.prompt: Where did the data go?
options: A personal cloud storage service
Back to the EHR server
A printer
questions.explain: The firewall shows a large upload to a personal cloud domain, not a business system.

questions.prompt: Which stakeholders must be engaged early?
options: HR
Legal
The attacker's family
Marketing
questions.explain: Insider cases are personnel and legal matters. Engage HR and legal alongside containment.

hints: The actor is an internal account, not an outside IP.
Follow the large outbound upload to its destination domain.
Preserve, do not delete. This may become a legal case.

answerKey.rootCause: A departing insider abused authorized access to bulk-collect PHI and upload it to personal cloud storage.

scenarios.debrief: Insider threat flips the usual model. The credential is valid and the response is as much HR and legal as technical. Evidence preservation is paramount.

scenarios.title: Business Email Compromise and Wire Fraud

briefing.subject: Finance got an urgent vendor banking change
briefing.body: A compromised mailbox sent finance an urgent request to change vendor banking details, and an inbox rule is hiding the replies. This is business email compromise heading toward wire fraud. Contain the account and stop the payment.
objectives: Confirm the BEC pattern
Stop the fraudulent payment
Contain the account

steps.prompt: What is the fraud mechanism here?
options: A compromised mailbox requests a banking change while an inbox rule hides replies
A ransomware note
A port scan
steps.rationale: BEC abuses a trusted mailbox to redirect payment, and the rule conceals the conversation from the real owner.

steps.prompt: What stops the actual loss fastest?
options: Alert finance to halt the wire and verify any change out of band
Reset the password next week
Reply to the attacker
steps.rationale: The money is the impact. Stop the wire and verify by phone before any account cleanup.

questions.prompt: How should finance verify a banking change request?
options: An out-of-band call to a known vendor contact
By replying to the email
By trusting the urgency
questions.explain: Out-of-band verification defeats BEC. Never verify a change using the same channel that requested it.

questions.prompt: Which artifact is the attacker hiding behind?
options: An inbox rule that diverts and deletes replies
A calendar invite
A printer job
questions.explain: The malicious inbox rule keeps the real mailbox owner from seeing the fraud unfold.

hints: Tie the wire-change email to the compromised mailbox.
The inbox rule is the concealment.
Stop the money first, then clean the account.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised mailbox launched business email compromise, using an inbox rule to hide a fraudulent vendor wire-change request.

scenarios.debrief: BEC is among the costliest attacks by dollars lost. The winning move is process: out-of-band verification of any payment change, plus fast account containment.

scenarios.title: Cryptojacking on a Server

briefing.subject: An app server is pinned at 98 percent CPU
briefing.body: Monitoring shows an application server maxed out on CPU, and the firewall logs connections to a mining pool on an unusual port. This looks like cryptojacking. Confirm it, find how it got there, and remove it.
objectives: Confirm resource hijacking
Identify the mining destination
Contain and eradicate

steps.prompt: What signals cryptojacking rather than a busy app?
options: Sustained max CPU plus stratum protocol to a mining pool
Normal web traffic
A single failed login
steps.rationale: Stratum on an odd port paired with pinned CPU is mining, not normal load.

steps.prompt: What is the right cleanup?
options: Kill the miner, block the pool, and find and close the entry path
Add more CPU
Ignore it, no data was stolen
steps.rationale: Resource theft still means a compromise. Remove the miner and fix how it got in.

questions.prompt: What protocol and port indicate mining here?
options: Stratum on port 3333
HTTPS on 443
SSH on 22
questions.explain: The firewall shows stratum mining traffic on port 3333 to a pool.

questions.prompt: Why does cryptojacking still matter even without data theft?
options: It proves a foothold and steals compute and money
It never matters
It improves performance
questions.explain: The miner is evidence of compromise and a path the attacker can reuse for worse.

hints: Look for stratum and the odd destination port.
Pinned CPU plus mining traffic is the pattern.
Treat it as a real compromise, not just a performance issue.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker gained execution on the app server and deployed a cryptominer connecting to a mining pool.

scenarios.debrief: Cryptojacking is easy to dismiss as a performance blip. The lesson is that stolen compute is still a compromise and a marker of a foothold.

scenarios.title: Hunt: Lateral Movement by Pass-the-Hash

briefing.subject: Hunt for lateral movement to the domain controller
briefing.body: Threat intel suggests an actor is moving laterally with stolen hashes rather than passwords. Hunt the authentication and SMB logs for pass-the-hash patterns reaching the domain controller and scope the spread.
objectives: Identify pass-the-hash logons
Trace the pivot host
Recommend containment

steps.prompt: What pattern marks pass-the-hash?
options: NTLM network logons to systems with no prior interactive logon by that account
Normal Kerberos sign-ins
A single failed password
steps.rationale: Pass-the-hash shows as type 3 NTLM logons appearing without the interactive logon that should precede legitimate use.

steps.prompt: Strongest containment for hash reuse?
options: Isolate the pivot, reset the abused account, and rotate exposed credentials
Change one firewall rule
Wait and watch
steps.rationale: Cut the pivot and invalidate the stolen hash by resetting the account and exposed secrets.

questions.prompt: Which authentication detail signals pass-the-hash?
options: Type 3 NTLM logon with no preceding interactive logon
A normal type 2 interactive logon
A failed Kerberos request
questions.explain: Reused hashes produce network NTLM logons that lack the expected interactive logon trail.

questions.prompt: What is the pivot in this hunt?
options: The internal host originating the NTLM logons to the DC
The public web server
The mining pool
questions.explain: The internal source repeatedly authenticating to the DC with NTLM is the pivot host.

hints: Filter Windows logons to the DC and check the auth package.
NTLM where you expect Kerberos is a flag.
Look for SMB admin share access from the same source.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker used stolen NTLM hashes to move laterally to the domain controller from an internal pivot host.

scenarios.debrief: A Tier 3 hunt that teaches the difference between authentication that is normal and authentication that is merely valid. Pass-the-hash hides in plain sight.

scenarios.title: Hunt: Kerberoasting and Privilege Escalation

briefing.subject: Hunt for service-ticket abuse in Active Directory
briefing.body: We expect an actor is harvesting Kerberos service tickets to crack offline and escalate. Hunt the domain controller logs for kerberoasting and any privilege escalation that follows.
objectives: Identify kerberoasting
Spot the privilege escalation
Recommend hardening

steps.prompt: What event pattern indicates kerberoasting?
options: A burst of 4769 TGS requests using weak RC4 encryption
A single 4624 success
A DNS query to office365
steps.rationale: Kerberoasting enumerates service tickets, visible as many 4769 events with RC4 encryption for cracking.

steps.prompt: What hardening best prevents recurrence?
options: Strong service-account passwords or gMSA, and disabling RC4
A longer user password policy only
Antivirus updates only
steps.rationale: Long random service-account secrets or group managed service accounts make offline cracking infeasible, and disabling RC4 removes the weak ticket.

questions.prompt: Which encryption type in the ticket requests is the red flag?
options: RC4, shown as 0x17
AES-256
TLS 1.3
questions.explain: RC4 service tickets are weak and crackable offline, the hallmark of kerberoasting.

questions.prompt: What happened after the ticket harvesting?
options: An account was added to Domain Admins
The server rebooted
A bucket was made public
questions.explain: The finale shows privilege escalation, an account added to Domain Admins after cracking.

hints: Filter the DC for 4769 events and read the encryption type.
Count the burst of requests from one source.
Watch the end of the shift for a group membership change.

answerKey.rootCause: Kerberoasting harvested RC4 service tickets for offline cracking, leading to privilege escalation into Domain Admins.

scenarios.debrief: A high-end hunt. Students learn to read Kerberos encryption types and to connect credential access to the privilege escalation it enables.

scenarios.title: Exploited CVE and Vulnerability Management

briefing.subject: A known CVE is being exploited on a public app
briefing.body: Our internet-facing application is being hit with a known remote exploit, and post-exploit activity has started. Confirm the exploitation, contain it, and lead the vulnerability management response across the fleet.
objectives: Confirm exploitation of the CVE
Contain and remediate the host
Scope and prioritize the fleet

steps.prompt: What confirms exploitation of the CVE?
options: A crafted request triggering an outbound lookup, then a callback from the server
A normal login
A blocked port scan
steps.rationale: The injection in the request followed by the server reaching out confirms the exploit landed.

steps.prompt: What is the correct vulnerability management response?
options: Virtual-patch now, patch the software, then scan and prioritize every vulnerable asset
Patch only this one server and close the ticket
Wait for the next maintenance window
steps.rationale: Contain immediately, fix the root with a patch, then treat it as a fleet-wide exposure and prioritize by risk.

questions.prompt: Which is a temporary mitigation versus a permanent fix?
options: WAF virtual patch is temporary, software patch is permanent
Both are permanent
Neither helps
questions.explain: A WAF rule buys time. Patching the vulnerable software is the durable fix.

questions.prompt: After containing this host, what is the priority?
options: Scan the fleet for the same CVE and prioritize remediation by exposure and impact
Reboot every server
Disable logging
questions.explain: One exploited host signals fleet risk. Find every vulnerable asset and prioritize by risk.

hints: Read the exploit request, then watch the server call out.
Separate the stopgap from the real fix.
Scope beyond this one host.

answerKey.rootCause: A known CVE in an internet-facing application was exploited, leading to code execution and C2.

scenarios.debrief: This is the Vulnerability Management capstone. Students connect a live exploit to the broader program: contain, patch, then scope and prioritize across the whole estate.

scenarios.title: Malicious USB Device

briefing.subject: Unknown USB drive triggered an alert
briefing.body: A clinician plugged in a USB drive found in the parking lot, and the endpoint immediately recorded an autorun executable launching PowerShell. Confirm whether code ran and decide on isolation.
objectives: Confirm the removable-media execution
Identify any callout
Decide on host isolation

steps.prompt: What confirms this is more than a plugged-in drive?
options: The autorun executable launched PowerShell that then called out
A USB icon appeared
The user opened a folder
steps.rationale: Mount alone is benign. Execution plus a callout is the compromise.

steps.prompt: First containment step?
options: Isolate the host and block the C2, preserving the drive as evidence
Format the USB drive
Tell the user to unplug it
steps.rationale: Isolate to stop the attacker and preserve the device for forensics.

questions.prompt: Did code execute from the device?
options: Yes, autorun launched PowerShell that beaconed out
No, the drive only mounted
questions.explain: The mount is followed by PowerShell execution and a periodic beacon, so code ran.

questions.prompt: Which policy would have prevented this?
options: Blocking USB mass storage and disabling autorun
A longer password
Antivirus signatures only
questions.explain: Device control and disabling autorun stop parking-lot USB attacks at the source.

hints: Start at the 6416 device event and follow what spawned next.
Look for a beacon after the PowerShell line.
The C2 domain and payload hash are in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A malicious USB drive auto-executed a PowerShell payload that established C2.

scenarios.debrief: Removable media is a classic initial-access vector. The lesson: a mount is nothing, execution plus callout is everything, and device control prevents the whole class.

scenarios.title: Suspicious Scheduled Task

briefing.subject: A new scheduled task looks wrong
briefing.body: Endpoint logging shows a scheduled task created on a workstation that runs hidden PowerShell at every logon. Determine if this is persistence and recommend a response.
objectives: Confirm the persistence mechanism
Identify what it runs
Recommend removal

steps.prompt: Why is this task suspicious?
options: It runs hidden, encoded PowerShell at every logon from a non-standard path
It is named after Windows Update
It runs once a year
steps.rationale: Hidden encoded PowerShell on a logon trigger is a textbook persistence pattern, regardless of the friendly name.

steps.prompt: What is the correct response?
options: Remove the task, find what created it, and scan for related artifacts
Disable the task and move on
Rename the task
steps.rationale: Removing the task without finding the parent leaves the door open. Trace and sweep.

questions.prompt: What trigger does the task use?
options: At logon
At shutdown only
Never
questions.explain: The 4698 event shows an ONLOGON trigger, giving the attacker persistence across reboots.

questions.prompt: Which technique is this?
options: Scheduled task persistence
Brute force
Phishing
questions.explain: A scheduled task created for re-execution is persistence via scheduled task.

hints: Find the 4698 task-creation event and read the action.
An encoded command hidden from view is a flag.
Check what the task reaches out to.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker created a logon-triggered scheduled task to persist and run a PowerShell payload.

scenarios.debrief: Persistence is where attackers settle in. Recognizing a hostile scheduled task by its action and trigger, not its name, is core Tier 1 work.

scenarios.title: Security Tooling Disabled

briefing.subject: Defender was switched off on a workstation
briefing.body: Endpoint telemetry shows real-time protection disabled on a machine, followed by script activity. Disabling defenses is a common precursor to the main attack. Investigate and respond.
objectives: Confirm the defense was disabled
Identify follow-on activity
Recommend containment

steps.prompt: Why does disabled protection matter so much?
options: It usually precedes the real attack and blinds detection
It improves performance
It is a routine update
steps.rationale: Turning off defenses is rarely benign. It clears the way for what comes next.

steps.prompt: Best response?
options: Isolate, re-enable and verify protection, then hunt for what followed
Just turn Defender back on
Reboot the machine
steps.rationale: Re-enabling alone ignores the activity that the attacker ran while blind. Isolate and hunt.

questions.prompt: How was protection disabled?
options: A PowerShell Set-MpPreference command
A Windows Update
A user setting in the GUI
questions.explain: The log shows Set-MpPreference disabling real-time monitoring, a scripted tamper.

questions.prompt: What happened after defenses dropped?
options: Encoded PowerShell ran and the host beaconed out
Nothing
The machine shut down
questions.explain: With protection off, the attacker ran a payload and established C2.

hints: Look for a Defender event showing real-time protection off.
What ran in the window after it was disabled?
Tamper protection would have blocked this.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker disabled Defender real-time protection, then ran a payload and beaconed out.

scenarios.debrief: Defense evasion is a tell, not a footnote. When protection goes off without a change ticket, treat the window that follows as hostile.

scenarios.title: MFA Push Fatigue

briefing.subject: A user is being flooded with MFA prompts
briefing.body: Identity logs show a stream of MFA push notifications to one user from a foreign IP, then a successful sign-in. This is push-bombing aimed at wearing the user down. Investigate and contain.
objectives: Recognize MFA fatigue
Confirm whether a prompt was approved
Contain the identity

steps.prompt: What does a burst of pending MFA pushes indicate?
options: An attacker has the password and is hammering MFA to get one approval
A network glitch
The user forgot their phone
steps.rationale: Push bombing means the password is already known and only MFA stands in the way.

steps.prompt: Best containment?
options: Revoke sessions, reset the password, and move the user to number matching or passkeys
Tell the user to approve once to stop the prompts
Ignore it
steps.rationale: Never approve to make it stop. Contain the identity and harden the MFA method.

questions.prompt: Was a prompt eventually approved?
options: Yes, a successful sign-in followed the push storm
No, all were denied
questions.explain: A successful sign-in after the push burst means the user approved one, accidentally or out of fatigue.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents push fatigue?
options: Number matching or phishing-resistant passkeys
More frequent password changes
Email alerts only
questions.explain: Number matching and passkeys defeat blind-approval attacks.

hints: Count the pending push notifications from the foreign IP.
Look for the sign-in that follows the burst.
The fix is the MFA method, not the password alone.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker with the password used MFA push bombing until the user approved a prompt.

scenarios.debrief: MFA fatigue beats weak MFA, not strong MFA. The defense is method design, and the user instinct to approve must be replaced with number matching.

scenarios.title: Malicious Email Attachment

briefing.subject: An invoice attachment ran a macro
briefing.body: A user opened an invoice attachment and the workstation recorded Word spawning PowerShell. Confirm whether the document was weaponized and what to block.
objectives: Confirm the malicious attachment
Trace the execution
Extract indicators to block

steps.prompt: What confirms the attachment was malicious?
options: winword.exe spawned powershell after the document opened
The email had a logo
The subject said invoice
steps.rationale: Office spawning a script interpreter is the macro-execution signature.

steps.prompt: What protects others fastest?
options: Block the sender domain and document hash, then purge the message
Warn the team in a meeting
Nothing, one person opened it
steps.rationale: Block the indicators and purge the campaign tenant-wide.

questions.prompt: Which process chain is the red flag?
options: winword.exe to powershell.exe
explorer.exe to chrome.exe
outlook.exe to teams.exe
questions.explain: Word launching PowerShell is the hallmark of a macro-weaponized document.

questions.prompt: Which indicators go on the blocklist?
options: The sender domain
The document hash
The reporter mailbox
office365.com
questions.explain: Block the adversary domain and the document hash, never legitimate infrastructure.

hints: Find the 4688 event where Word spawns PowerShell.
The document hash is your durable indicator.
Block and purge across all mailboxes.

answerKey.rootCause: A macro-enabled invoice attachment executed PowerShell on open.

scenarios.debrief: Attachment phishing differs from link phishing in the evidence trail. The Office-to-script process chain is the thing to recognize and block on.

scenarios.title: Compromised Mailbox Sending Spam

briefing.subject: One of our mailboxes is blasting outbound mail
briefing.body: A user mailbox is suddenly sending hundreds of outbound messages, and an inbox rule was added to hide the bounces. The account is likely compromised. Confirm and contain.
objectives: Confirm the account abuse
Find the concealment
Contain the mailbox

steps.prompt: What signals account takeover here?
options: A sudden surge of outbound mail plus a hiding inbox rule
A single sent email
A calendar invite
steps.rationale: Mass outbound mail and a concealment rule together point to a hijacked mailbox.

steps.prompt: Best containment?
options: Revoke sessions, reset the password, remove the rule, and throttle outbound mail
Delete the sent items only
Ask the user to stop
steps.rationale: Contain the identity, strip persistence, and stop the spam at the gateway.

questions.prompt: What is the inbox rule doing?
options: Hiding bounce and reply messages from the real owner
Sorting newsletters
Backing up mail
questions.explain: The rule conceals the campaign so the owner does not notice the abuse.

questions.prompt: Which two are containment actions?
options: Revoke sessions and reset password
Remove the malicious inbox rule
Reboot the mail server
Email all staff
questions.explain: Contain the identity and remove the persistence first.

hints: Look at the outbound recipient count.
Check for a new inbox rule on the mailbox.
The forward target domain is in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised mailbox was used to send spam, with an inbox rule hiding the activity.

scenarios.debrief: A noisy mailbox is often a hijacked one. The concealment rule is the giveaway that this is takeover, not a busy sender.

scenarios.title: DNS Tunneling Exfiltration

briefing.subject: Strange DNS traffic from a server
briefing.body: The DNS logs show a host issuing a stream of long, high-entropy TXT queries to one external domain. This pattern fits DNS tunneling used to exfiltrate data past the firewall. Investigate and contain.
objectives: Confirm DNS tunneling
Identify the channel
Recommend containment

steps.prompt: What marks DNS tunneling?
options: High-entropy, oversized TXT queries to one domain at a steady rate
Normal A-record lookups for office365
A single failed lookup
steps.rationale: Long random subdomains in TXT queries to one domain encode data in DNS.

steps.prompt: Best containment?
options: Sinkhole or block the domain, isolate the host, and force DNS through inspected resolvers
Restart the DNS server
Allow it and monitor
steps.rationale: Cut the channel at DNS and isolate the host, then route DNS through inspection.

questions.prompt: Which record type carries the tunnel?
options: TXT
MX
PTR
questions.explain: The high-entropy data rides in TXT queries to the attacker domain.

questions.prompt: Why did this evade the firewall?
options: DNS is usually allowed out and rarely inspected
The firewall was off
It used port 3389
questions.explain: DNS egress is typically permitted and unmonitored, which tunneling abuses.

hints: Filter syslog for named query lines and look at the query length.
All the odd queries go to one domain.
Containment is at the DNS layer.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised host exfiltrated data through DNS TXT tunneling to an attacker domain.

scenarios.debrief: DNS tunneling hides in a protocol everyone trusts. Recognizing entropy and volume in DNS, and inspecting egress DNS, is the defense.

scenarios.title: Double-Extortion Ransomware

briefing.subject: Files are encrypting and data left first
briefing.body: We have encryption underway on a file server, but the firewall shows a large outbound transfer that happened before the encryption. This is double extortion: steal, then encrypt. Contain and scope the data loss.
objectives: Confirm encryption and prior exfiltration
Scope the data taken
Contain the spread

steps.prompt: What makes this double extortion?
options: A large exfiltration occurred before the encryption stage
Only files were encrypted
Only data was stolen
steps.rationale: Theft before encryption gives the attacker leverage even if you restore from backup.

steps.prompt: What does the prior exfil change about response?
options: A breach notification analysis is required even with good backups
Nothing, just restore
Pay immediately
steps.rationale: Restoring fixes availability, not the disclosure. The stolen data drives notification duties.

questions.prompt: What happened first?
options: Data was staged and exfiltrated, then files were encrypted
Encryption, then theft
Only a port scan
questions.explain: The staging and large transfer precede the file-encryption events.

questions.prompt: Why are backups not a complete answer?
options: The attacker already stole the data and can leak it
Backups never work
Encryption is harmless
questions.explain: Double extortion means recovery does not remove the disclosure risk.

hints: Order the events by time and look before the encryption.
Find the large outbound transfer and its destination.
The stolen data drives the notification question.

answerKey.rootCause: An intrusion staged and exfiltrated data, then deployed ransomware for double extortion.

scenarios.debrief: Modern ransomware steals before it encrypts. Students must look earlier in the timeline and understand that recovery and disclosure are separate problems.

scenarios.title: Malicious OAuth Consent Grant

briefing.subject: A user consented to a sketchy app
briefing.body: After a phishing lure, a user granted an OAuth application broad mailbox and file permissions. The app now reads mail without needing the password again. Investigate the illicit consent and revoke it.
objectives: Confirm the illicit consent
Identify the granted scopes
Revoke and contain

steps.prompt: Why is OAuth consent dangerous?
options: The app keeps mailbox access via a token even after a password reset
It is just a login
It expires instantly
steps.rationale: Consented apps hold their own token, so password resets alone do not revoke them.

steps.prompt: What revokes this access?
options: Remove the app consent and revoke its tokens, then strip the inbox rule
Reset the password only
Block one IP
steps.rationale: You must revoke the application grant itself, not just the user credentials.

questions.prompt: What persistence did the attacker gain?
options: An OAuth app with Mail.Read and offline_access
A scheduled task
A new firewall rule
questions.explain: The consented app with offline_access reads mail indefinitely until revoked.

questions.prompt: Does a password reset remove the app access?
options: No, the app token must be revoked separately
Yes, fully
Only after 90 days
questions.explain: OAuth grants survive password resets and require explicit revocation.

hints: Find the consent event and read the scopes.
Note offline_access, which means lasting reach.
Revoke the app, not just the password.

answerKey.rootCause: A phishing lure led a user to grant a malicious OAuth app persistent mailbox access.

scenarios.debrief: Illicit consent is identity persistence without a password. The key insight is that the application grant is the thing to revoke.

scenarios.title: Database Dump Exfiltration

briefing.subject: A huge query dumped the patients table
briefing.body: The database logs show a bulk export of the entire patients table, followed by a large outbound transfer. Confirm the exfiltration, scope the records, and contain.
objectives: Confirm the bulk export
Scope the records taken
Contain the path

steps.prompt: What confirms exfiltration of records?
options: A bulk SELECT INTO OUTFILE followed by a large outbound transfer
A single row lookup
A failed login
steps.rationale: The mass export plus the matching outbound transfer is the exfiltration.

steps.prompt: Best containment?
options: Block the destination, isolate the database, and scope the records for notification
Restart the database
Ignore the transfer
steps.rationale: Cut the path, isolate the source, and quantify the exposure for breach analysis.

questions.prompt: How many records were exported?
options: About 480,000 patient rows
One row
None
questions.explain: The query log shows roughly 480,000 rows exported via OUTFILE.

questions.prompt: Why is record count important here?
options: It drives the breach notification scope under HIPAA
It does not matter
It sets the CPU usage
questions.explain: The number and type of records determine notification obligations.

hints: Find the bulk SELECT in the database log.
Match it to the outbound transfer size.
Record count feeds the notification analysis.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker exported the patients table in bulk and exfiltrated it over HTTPS.

scenarios.debrief: Data-centric incidents turn on scope. The skill is tying the database export to the egress and quantifying exactly what left.

scenarios.title: Supply Chain Malicious Update

briefing.subject: A trusted updater pulled something hostile
briefing.body: A vendor agent on a server fetched an update that then spawned an unexpected process and beaconed out. This looks like a compromised software supply chain. Confirm and contain.
objectives: Confirm the malicious update
Identify the callout
Contain and scope the blast radius

steps.prompt: What is unusual about a signed updater here?
options: A trusted updater spawned an unexpected child and beaconed out
It updated on schedule
It checked a version number
steps.rationale: Trust in the vendor process is the very thing the attacker abused.

steps.prompt: What is the right scoping move?
options: Identify every host running the same agent and version, then contain broadly
Isolate just this one server
Wait for the vendor
steps.rationale: Supply-chain compromise is fleet-wide by nature. Scope by the affected software.

questions.prompt: Why is this hard to catch?
options: The malicious code arrives through a trusted, signed update path
The firewall was off
It used a USB drive
questions.explain: Supply-chain attacks ride legitimate, trusted distribution channels.

questions.prompt: What scopes the blast radius?
options: Finding all hosts on the same affected agent version
Counting failed logons
Checking the printer queue
questions.explain: Every host with the compromised update is potentially affected.

hints: Look at what the updater process spawned.
Follow the beacon to its destination.
Scope by the software, not the single host.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised vendor update delivered a backdoor that beaconed to C2.
containment: Pin or roll back the agent

scenarios.debrief: Supply-chain compromise weaponizes trust. The defining response skill is scoping by the affected software across the whole estate.

scenarios.title: Web Defacement

briefing.subject: Our homepage was replaced
briefing.body: The public homepage now shows attacker content. The web logs show an exploit, a web shell, and then a write to the index page. Reconstruct the attack and contain.
objectives: Reconstruct the kill chain
Find the foothold
Contain and restore

steps.prompt: What was the sequence?
options: Exploit, then web shell, then a write to the index page
Just a port scan
A phishing email
steps.rationale: The logs show exploitation, a foothold, then the defacement write.

steps.prompt: What restores trust in the server?
options: Remove the shell, patch the entry, restore the page from a known-good source, and rotate secrets
Just re-upload the homepage
Reboot the server
steps.rationale: Re-uploading the page leaves the foothold. Eradicate the shell and the entry first.

questions.prompt: How did the attacker change the page?
options: Through a web shell after exploiting the app
By guessing the admin password
Through email
questions.explain: The exploit dropped a web shell that was used to write the new index page.

questions.prompt: Why is re-uploading the homepage insufficient?
options: The web shell and entry vulnerability remain
It always fixes the issue
The server cannot be reinfected
questions.explain: Without removing the shell and patching the entry, the attacker simply does it again.

hints: Order the web events: exploit, shell, write.
Find the uploaded shell.
Eradicate before you restore the page.

answerKey.rootCause: An exploited web app let the attacker drop a shell and deface the homepage.

scenarios.debrief: Defacement is visible and embarrassing, but the real lesson is kill-chain reconstruction and the discipline to eradicate before restoring.

scenarios.title: Hunt: Persistence Sweep

briefing.subject: Hunt for footholds across the workstations
briefing.body: We believe an actor planted persistence on at least one host. Hunt the endpoint logs for scheduled tasks and registry Run keys that do not belong, and scope the spread.
objectives: Find the persistence mechanisms
Tie them to one actor
Recommend remediation

steps.prompt: Which two mechanisms are you hunting?
options: Scheduled tasks and registry Run keys with suspicious targets
Only failed logons
Only DNS lookups
steps.rationale: Scheduled tasks and Run keys are two of the most common persistence techniques.

steps.prompt: How do you confirm one actor?
options: Both mechanisms point to the same payload and callout
They have different names
They run at different times
steps.rationale: A shared payload and C2 destination tie disparate persistence to one campaign.

questions.prompt: What is a registry Run key used for?
options: Auto-starting a program at logon for persistence
Encrypting files
Scanning ports
questions.explain: Run keys execute their target at logon, a lightweight persistence method.

questions.prompt: What ties the two mechanisms together?
options: A shared payload path and the same callout
Nothing
The time of day
questions.explain: The common payload and C2 confirm a single actor planted both.

hints: Search for 4698 task creations and Sysmon 13 Run-key writes.
Compare the targets and callouts.
Scope to every host showing the same pattern.

answerKey.rootCause: An actor planted scheduled-task and registry Run-key persistence pointing to one payload and C2.

scenarios.debrief: A persistence hunt rewards breadth. Knowing the common mechanisms and tying them to a shared payload turns scattered alerts into one story.

scenarios.title: Hunt: Golden Ticket

briefing.subject: Hunt for forged Kerberos tickets
briefing.body: Intel suggests the krbtgt hash may be compromised, enabling forged golden tickets. Hunt the domain controller for tickets with anomalous lifetimes and missing authentication requests, then assess the damage.
objectives: Identify forged tickets
Confirm the privileged access
Recommend recovery

steps.prompt: What betrays a golden ticket?
options: A ticket with an absurd lifetime and no preceding authentication request
A normal sign-in
A DNS query
steps.rationale: Forged tickets carry attacker-chosen lifetimes and skip the normal AS-REQ.

steps.prompt: What actually recovers from a golden ticket?
options: Reset the krbtgt password twice and rebuild trust in privileged accounts
Reset one user password
Reboot the DC
steps.rationale: Only rotating the krbtgt secret invalidates forged tickets. A double reset is standard.

questions.prompt: Which detail flags the forged ticket?
options: A 10-year lifetime with no prior AS-REQ
A 10-hour lifetime
A normal logon type
questions.explain: Golden tickets show impossible lifetimes and lack the authentication request a real ticket has.

questions.prompt: What is the required recovery action?
options: Reset the krbtgt account password twice
Change the firewall rules
Patch the web server
questions.explain: Rotating krbtgt invalidates all forged tickets. It must be done twice to be effective.

hints: Look at 4769 tickets and their lifetimes.
A missing AS-REQ before the ticket is the tell.
Recovery centers on the krbtgt account.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised krbtgt hash allowed forged golden tickets granting domain-wide access.

scenarios.debrief: Golden tickets are among the hardest incidents to recover from. Students learn the forensic tells and that only a krbtgt reset truly closes it.

scenarios.title: Hunt: Data Staging Before Exfil

briefing.subject: Hunt for data being collected for theft
briefing.body: We suspect an actor is gathering data to steal. Hunt for bulk file access and large archives being staged, then catch the exfiltration before or as it happens.
objectives: Find the staging activity
Tie it to exfiltration
Recommend containment

steps.prompt: What does staging look like?
options: Bulk file access followed by a large archive created in a temp path
A single file open
A failed login
steps.rationale: Collection plus a large RAR or ZIP in a temp directory is the staging signature.

steps.prompt: Why is catching staging valuable?
options: It lets you intervene before the data leaves
It is too late by then
It only matters after exfil
steps.rationale: Staging precedes exfiltration, so detecting it is the chance to prevent the loss.

questions.prompt: What is the staging artifact?
options: A large RAR archive created in a temp directory
A new user account
A web shell
questions.explain: The bulk access then a 2.4 GB RAR in Temp is the staged collection.

questions.prompt: Where did the data go?
options: An external host over HTTPS
Back to the file server
A printer
questions.explain: The staged archive was sent to an external destination over HTTPS.

hints: Look for bulk file access by one account.
Then find a large archive in a temp path.
Tie the archive to the outbound transfer.

answerKey.rootCause: An actor collected and archived data, then exfiltrated it over HTTPS.

scenarios.debrief: Catching the staging step is the difference between a near miss and a breach. The hunt teaches the collection-to-exfil pattern.

scenarios.title: Hunt: Cloud IAM Enumeration

briefing.subject: Hunt for cloud reconnaissance
briefing.body: A set of access keys signed in from an unusual location and immediately began enumerating IAM. This is cloud reconnaissance that often precedes privilege escalation. Hunt the CloudTrail logs and scope it.
objectives: Identify the enumeration
Tie it to a suspect identity
Recommend containment

steps.prompt: What does IAM enumeration look like?
options: A burst of ListUsers, ListRoles, and authorization-detail calls from one key
A single GetObject
Normal console logins
steps.rationale: Rapid IAM listing calls are reconnaissance to map privileges before escalating.

steps.prompt: Best containment?
options: Disable the key, review what it created, and audit the IAM changes
Rotate one password
Ignore the recon
steps.rationale: Disable the credential and reverse any persistence it established.

questions.prompt: What usually follows IAM enumeration?
options: Privilege escalation or persistence
A reboot
Nothing
questions.explain: Recon maps the account so the attacker can escalate or persist, which the logs then show.

questions.prompt: What did the key do after enumerating?
options: Created persistence by attaching admin access
Logged off
Downloaded an image
questions.explain: The enumeration is followed by an AttachUserPolicy persistence action.

hints: Filter CloudTrail to the suspect access key.
Look for a burst of List and Get-authorization calls.
Check what the key did next.

answerKey.rootCause: A stolen access key enumerated IAM from a foreign location, then established persistence.

scenarios.debrief: Cloud recon is quiet and fast. Recognizing an IAM enumeration burst gives you the chance to stop the escalation that follows.

scenarios.title: Cloud IAM Key Compromise and Escalation

briefing.subject: A leaked access key is escalating in our cloud
briefing.body: An access key that leaked in a code repo is now enumerating IAM, attaching admin policy to a new user, and spinning up large compute. Contain the identity and stop the resource abuse.
objectives: Confirm the key compromise
Identify the escalation and abuse
Contain and remediate

steps.prompt: What is the chain here?
options: Enumerate IAM, attach admin to a new user, then launch costly compute
Just a failed login
A phishing email
steps.rationale: The key maps the account, escalates, and then abuses compute, the classic cloud takeover.

steps.prompt: First containment action?
options: Disable the leaked key, remove the new admin user, and terminate the rogue instances
Change the console password
Wait for the bill
steps.rationale: Kill the credential and reverse every change it made, including the running instances.

questions.prompt: What did the attacker create for persistence?
options: A new IAM user with AdministratorAccess
A scheduled task
A web shell
questions.explain: The AttachUserPolicy event grants the new user full admin, a durable backdoor.

questions.prompt: What was the impact action?
options: Launching large GPU instances to mine
Encrypting a file server
Defacing a website
questions.explain: The RunInstances burst of GPU instances is resource hijacking for mining.

hints: Trace the leaked key through CloudTrail.
Find the new admin user it created.
Terminate the rogue compute and rotate everything.

answerKey.rootCause: A leaked access key escalated to admin and launched compute for cryptomining.

scenarios.debrief: Leaked cloud keys escalate fast. The capstone skill is reversing every change a credential made, not just disabling it.

scenarios.title: Prompt Injection Data Exfiltration

briefing.subject: Our AI assistant leaked data after a poisoned input
briefing.body: The internal AI assistant processed a document containing hidden instructions, then used a tool action to post patient records to an external endpoint. This is indirect prompt injection. Investigate and contain.
objectives: Confirm the prompt injection
Identify the egress
Recommend guardrails

steps.prompt: What is indirect prompt injection?
options: Hidden instructions inside content the AI processes hijack its tool actions
A brute force on the AI login
A port scan
steps.rationale: The model follows instructions embedded in untrusted content, here causing a data-posting tool call.

steps.prompt: What guardrail helps most?
options: Constrain the assistant tool permissions and egress, and isolate untrusted content
A longer system prompt only
Disable logging
steps.rationale: Least-privilege tools and egress allowlists limit what an injected instruction can do.

questions.prompt: How did the data leave?
options: The assistant made an http_post tool call to an external endpoint
Email
A USB drive
questions.explain: The injected instruction drove a tool action that posted records out.

questions.prompt: Which control limits the blast radius of injection?
options: Least-privilege tools and an egress allowlist
Faster GPUs
More training data
questions.explain: Restricting what tools can do and where they can send data contains the damage.

hints: Look for an agent tool-call posting data out.
Note the injected instruction in the log.
The fix is tool permissions and egress control.

answerKey.rootCause: Indirect prompt injection drove the AI assistant to exfiltrate records via a tool call.

scenarios.debrief: AI-era incidents are real now. Students learn that prompt injection turns trusted assistants into exfiltration tools, and that least privilege still saves you.

scenarios.title: AI-Assisted Vishing and BEC

briefing.subject: A cloned voice got an MFA reset
briefing.body: The help desk reset MFA for a finance user after a convincing phone call that used a cloned voice. The attacker then signed in and pushed a fraudulent wire request. Investigate and contain.
objectives: Connect the vishing to the takeover
Stop the fraudulent payment
Recommend process fixes

steps.prompt: What enabled the takeover?
options: A help-desk MFA reset granted on a weakly verified phone call
A brute force
A public bucket
steps.rationale: Social engineering of the help desk, aided by a cloned voice, bypassed the technical controls.

steps.prompt: What stops the loss and prevents recurrence?
options: Halt the wire, verify out of band, and require stronger help-desk identity proofing
Reset the password next week
Nothing
steps.rationale: Stop the money, then fix the human process that the attacker exploited.

questions.prompt: Where did the control fail?
options: Weak caller verification at the help desk
The firewall
The antivirus
questions.explain: The MFA reset was granted on a phone call with weak identity proofing.

questions.prompt: What is the durable fix?
options: Stronger help-desk identity proofing and out-of-band payment verification
A longer password
More email banners
questions.explain: Process hardening at the help desk and in finance defeats this attack.

hints: Tie the help-desk reset to the later sign-in.
Follow the takeover into the wire request.
The fix is human process, not just technology.

answerKey.rootCause: A cloned-voice vishing call obtained an MFA reset, enabling account takeover and a fraudulent wire request.

scenarios.debrief: AI makes social engineering more convincing. The defense is process: rigorous identity proofing and out-of-band verification that a cloned voice cannot satisfy.

scenarios.title: CI/CD Pipeline Compromise

briefing.subject: Someone slipped a step into our deploy pipeline
briefing.body: A compromised developer account added a build step to the production pipeline that fetches and runs a remote script, and the build server then beaconed out. Contain the pipeline and the foothold.
objectives: Confirm the pipeline tampering
Identify the callout
Contain and harden the pipeline

steps.prompt: What is the risk of a tampered pipeline?
options: It runs with high privilege and ships to production, so the blast radius is large
It only affects one laptop
It is harmless
steps.rationale: Pipelines hold powerful credentials and push to production, making them a prime target.

steps.prompt: What contains and hardens this?
options: Revert the pipeline, rotate pipeline secrets, and require reviewed, signed changes
Just delete the bad build
Ignore the beacon
steps.rationale: Remove the step, rotate the now-exposed secrets, and add change control and signing.

questions.prompt: How was the malicious step introduced?
options: A compromised developer account added it, bypassing approval
Through a USB drive
By a port scan
questions.explain: The injected step was added by a compromised account with approval bypassed.

questions.prompt: Why rotate pipeline secrets?
options: The malicious step could have stolen them
Secrets never need rotation
To free up disk space
questions.explain: Any secret the pipeline could access during the malicious run must be considered exposed.

hints: Find the added build step and who added it.
Follow the build server beacon.
Rotate every secret the pipeline could reach.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised developer account injected a malicious build step that fetched a remote payload and beaconed out.

scenarios.debrief: The pipeline is production with the keys to everything. Students learn to treat CI/CD as crown-jewel infrastructure and to rotate secrets after any tampering.

scenarios.title: VPN Password Spray

briefing.subject: Account lockouts on the VPN portal
briefing.body: The identity team flagged a burst of failed sign-ins on the BVHN VPN portal, all from one external address. Many nurse and billing accounts hit lockout within a few minutes. Confirm whether this is a password spray, find the source, and decide if any account fell.
objectives: Confirm the spray pattern
Identify the source and scope
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Spray source on a foreign ASN
Tor exit used for the later sign-in

steps.prompt: What marks a password spray rather than brute force?
options: One password tried across many accounts from one source
Many passwords against a single account
A lone failed sign-in from an office IP
steps.rationale: Spraying stays low and slow across many accounts to dodge lockout on any one.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block the source IP and force-reset any account that authenticated
Reimage the VPN concentrator
Disable VPN for the entire hospital
steps.rationale: Stop the source and reset only the accounts that succeeded.

questions.prompt: Did any account authenticate successfully?
options: Yes, a later sign-in from a Tor exit succeeded
No, every attempt failed
questions.explain: A success from the same actor after the spray confirms one account fell.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: MFA plus smart lockout and impossible-travel detection
A longer password policy only
Endpoint antivirus
questions.explain: MFA and smart lockout defeat spraying without locking out real clinicians.

hints: Group the failed sign-ins by source IP and count distinct users.
Look for a success from the same actor after the spray, this time over Tor.
Both source IPs are flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An internet-facing VPN portal without MFA was password-sprayed and one account later signed in from a Tor exit.
containment: Block the spray and Tor source IPs

scenarios.debrief: Spraying hides under the lockout threshold. The skill is spotting one password across many users and catching the single success that follows.

scenarios.title: Rogue OAuth Consent

briefing.subject: Unexpected app consent in M365
briefing.body: A radiologist approved a consent prompt for an app called QuickInbox Sync after a phishing email. The app requested broad mailbox permissions in BVHN's Microsoft 365 tenant. Confirm whether this is a malicious consent grant and decide what to revoke.
objectives: Confirm the consent grant is illegitimate
Identify the permissions and scope
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: App registration domain behind QuickInbox Sync
Consent lure link in the phishing email

steps.prompt: Why is a broad mailbox OAuth consent dangerous?
options: The app keeps token-based access to mail even after a password reset
It only changes the user's theme
It expires the moment the browser closes
steps.rationale: OAuth tokens persist independent of the password, so a reset alone does not revoke them.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Revoke the app's consent and refresh tokens, then remove the registration
Reset only the user's password
Block the user's mailbox from all clients
steps.rationale: Revoking consent and tokens cuts the app's standing access that a password reset misses.

questions.prompt: What confirms this consent is malicious?
options: An unverified publisher requesting Mail.ReadWrite right after a phishing click
The app is from Microsoft itself
The user is a known IT admin
questions.explain: An unverified app asking for broad mail scopes after a phish is the tell.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Require admin consent for new apps and restrict user consent
Disable email for radiology
Longer passwords only
questions.explain: Admin-consent workflows stop users from granting risky scopes.
