hints: Check the consent event for the app name and requested scopes.
An inbox rule created right after often hides the attacker's replies.
The app registration domain is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A phishing email lured a radiologist into granting a rogue OAuth app broad mailbox access in M365.
containment: Revoke the app consent and tokens
Restrict user consent to admin approval

scenarios.debrief: Illicit consent grants survive password resets. Triage means reading the requested scopes and revoking the token, not just the password.

scenarios.title: USB Autorun At Clinic

briefing.subject: Unknown USB on a clinical workstation
briefing.body: A removable drive was mounted on a nurse station in the cardiology clinic and a program launched on its own. The asset is a shared clinical workstation with PHI access. Confirm whether the USB ran code and decide whether to isolate the host.
objectives: Confirm code ran from the USB
Identify the executable and host
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Autorun payload dropped from the USB
Loader staged on the cardiology nurse-station workstation

steps.prompt: What confirms the USB actually executed code?
options: A mount event followed by a child process launching from the removable drive
The drive letter simply appearing
An antivirus definition update
steps.rationale: Execution needs a process spawned from the removable volume, not just the mount.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Network-isolate the workstation and collect the USB for analysis
Reboot the workstation and move on
Email the nurse to be careful next time
steps.rationale: Isolation stops spread while you triage the dropped payload and persistence.

questions.prompt: Which detail shows this is more than a benign drive?
options: A payload ran and then Defender was turned off
A photo file was copied
The drive was formatted FAT32
questions.explain: Code running plus security tooling being disabled signals an attack, not casual use.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Disable autorun and enforce USB device control on clinical endpoints
Buy faster USB drives
Disable the firewall
questions.explain: Device control and disabled autorun stop removable-media execution.

hints: Tie the mount event to the process that spawned right after.
Watch for Defender being disabled and a run key being written.
The dropped file hash is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A malicious USB autorun executed a payload on a cardiology clinical workstation and set up persistence.
containment: Disable autorun and enforce USB device control

scenarios.debrief: A mount alone is not an incident. The lesson is linking the mount to a process and persistence to confirm execution.

scenarios.title: Impossible Travel Sign-In

briefing.subject: Impossible-travel alert on M365
briefing.body: A billing clerk's Microsoft 365 account signed in from Houston and then from another continent twelve minutes later. Both sessions touched the mailbox. Confirm whether this is a true compromise or an explainable VPN artifact, then decide what to do.
objectives: Confirm the travel is physically impossible
Rule out a VPN or proxy false positive
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Foreign sign-in IP on a hosting ASN

steps.prompt: What confirms impossible travel rather than a VPN artifact?
options: Two distant logins too close in time, with the far IP on a hosting ASN not a known VPN
The user simply logged in twice
A single login from the office
steps.rationale: A corporate VPN egress is a known range, a hosting-provider IP an ocean away is not.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Revoke sessions, force MFA re-registration, and reset the password
Ask the user to ignore the alert
Whitelist the foreign IP
steps.rationale: Killing sessions and resetting credentials cuts the attacker and re-secures the account.

questions.prompt: Why is this not just a VPN false positive?
options: The far IP is on a hosting ASN and an MFA-fatigue prompt followed
The user works remotely sometimes
Both logins used the same browser
questions.explain: A hosting ASN plus push spamming points to a real takeover attempt.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Risk-based conditional access with phishing-resistant MFA
Allow logins from anywhere
Disable mailbox auditing
questions.explain: Risk-based access blocks anomalous geographies and weak MFA flows.

hints: Compare the two sign-in geos and the time gap between them.
Check whether the far IP belongs to a known BVHN VPN range or a hosting provider.
Watch for an inbox rule created from the foreign session.

answerKey.rootCause: A billing clerk's M365 account was accessed from a foreign hosting IP minutes after a local login, confirming credential compromise.

scenarios.debrief: Impossible travel is only real after you rule out the VPN. The skill is checking the ASN and the time gap before you act.

scenarios.title: Billing Macro Execution

briefing.subject: Office macro fired on a billing PC
briefing.body: A billing clerk opened an invoice attachment and a Word macro spawned a script process. The endpoint handles claims data and PHI. Confirm whether the macro ran malicious code and decide whether to isolate the host.
objectives: Confirm the macro executed code
Identify the spawned process and document
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: PowerShell payload dropped by the macro

steps.prompt: What confirms the macro ran malicious code?
options: Word spawned an encoded PowerShell child process
The document simply opened
A spelling-check process ran
steps.rationale: Office spawning a script interpreter is the classic macro-to-payload chain.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Isolate the host and block the C2 destination
Just close the document
Delete the user's mailbox
steps.rationale: Isolation halts the beacon while you triage the dropped payload.

questions.prompt: Which sequence proves this is malicious?
options: Macro runs, encoded PowerShell launches, then a C2 beacon starts
The document was emailed internally
The file is over one megabyte
questions.explain: Macro to encoded shell to outbound beacon is a textbook intrusion chain.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Block macros from the internet and use ASR rules on Office child processes
Allow all macros by default
Disable the proxy logs
questions.explain: Blocking internet macros and Office child processes stops this class outright.

hints: Look for Word as the parent of a PowerShell process.
Decode the encoded command and find the outbound beacon.
The document and payload hashes are flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A billing clerk opened a weaponized invoice whose macro launched encoded PowerShell and a C2 beacon.
containment: Block internet macros and enable ASR rules

scenarios.debrief: A macro alert is real when Office spawns a script. The lesson is following the parent-child chain to the beacon.

scenarios.title: DNS TXT Tunneling

briefing.subject: Odd outbound DNS TXT queries
briefing.body: A pharmacy workstation is sending a steady stream of long DNS TXT lookups to one external domain. The volume and entropy look automated. Confirm whether this is DNS tunneling for covert C2 or exfil, and decide how to contain it.
objectives: Confirm the TXT traffic is tunneling
Identify the destination domain and host
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Tunneling domain answering long TXT records
Secondary domain receiving the staged HTTPS upload

steps.prompt: What signals DNS tunneling rather than normal lookups?
options: High-volume, long, high-entropy TXT queries to one rarely seen domain
A single A record for a known site
An NTP sync request
steps.rationale: Encoded data rides in long random subdomains and TXT answers, unlike normal resolution.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Sinkhole or block the domain at the resolver and isolate the host
Increase the DNS cache size
Ignore it as noisy DNS
steps.rationale: Blocking the domain and isolating the host cuts the covert channel.

questions.prompt: Which detail confirms exfiltration intent?
options: Long encoded TXT queries followed by an HTTPS upload to the same actor
A cached lookup for the intranet
A reverse DNS lookup
questions.explain: Encoded DNS plus a follow-on HTTPS upload shows data leaving the network.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Force internal DNS with logging and alert on TXT volume and entropy
Open DNS to all external resolvers
Disable DNS logging
questions.explain: Controlled DNS with anomaly alerting catches tunneling early.

hints: Measure query length, entropy, and rate to the destination domain.
Tunneling pairs with a beacon and a later upload.
The destination domain is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A pharmacy workstation used DNS TXT tunneling to one malicious domain for covert C2 and staged exfiltration.
containment: Block or sinkhole the domain at the resolver
Force internal DNS with TXT-volume alerting

scenarios.debrief: Bursty, long, high-entropy TXT lookups to one domain are tunneling, not noise. The skill is measuring the pattern before dismissing DNS.

scenarios.title: External EHR Port Scan

briefing.subject: Port scan against the EHR subnet
briefing.body: The perimeter sensor flagged sequential connection attempts across the EHR subnet from one external IP. This subnet hosts patient-record systems. Confirm whether this is reconnaissance, judge the exposure, and decide what to do.
objectives: Confirm the activity is a port scan
Identify the source and targeted ports
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: External source sweeping the EHR subnet
EHR service ports probed by the scan

steps.prompt: What confirms a port scan rather than normal traffic?
options: One source touching many ports and hosts in sequence over a short window
A steady session to a single known service
A DNS lookup for the EHR host
steps.rationale: Sequential many-port, many-host probing from one source is reconnaissance.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block the source at the perimeter and verify EHR ports are not exposed
Shut down the EHR systems
Open the firewall to log more
steps.rationale: Blocking the scanner and checking exposure stops follow-on exploitation.

questions.prompt: Why does this scan warrant action despite being low severity?
options: It probed the EHR subnet and was followed by a CVE exploit attempt
Scans are always harmless
The source is an internal printer
questions.explain: Recon against patient-record systems plus an exploit attempt is a real precursor.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents exposure here?
options: Tight perimeter ACLs and rate-based scan detection on the EHR subnet
Public access to all EHR ports
Disabling perimeter logging
questions.explain: Restricting exposed ports and detecting sweeps blunts reconnaissance.

hints: Count distinct destination ports and hosts hit by the one source.
A scan often precedes an exploit attempt on whatever answered.
The scanning source IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An external host scanned the EHR subnet for exposed services and then attempted a CVE exploit.
containment: Block the source at the perimeter
Verify and close exposed EHR ports

scenarios.debrief: A port scan is low severity but a recon precursor. The skill is confirming the sweep and checking what was exposed before it escalates.

scenarios.title: Tor Exit Sign-In

briefing.subject: Tor-exit sign-in for a clinician
briefing.body: A physician's Microsoft 365 account signed in from a known Tor exit node outside normal hours. The clinician has no reason to anonymize traffic. Confirm whether this is account abuse or an explainable event, then decide what to do.
objectives: Confirm the sign-in came from a Tor exit
Rule out a benign explanation
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Tor exit node used for the sign-in
Physician account signed in over Tor

steps.prompt: Why is a Tor-exit sign-in suspicious for a clinician?
options: Clinicians have no need for anonymized access and Tor hides the true origin
Tor is faster than the hospital network
Tor is a Microsoft service
steps.rationale: Anonymizing access for a clinical account points to credential abuse, not normal use.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Revoke sessions, reset the password, and re-register MFA
Add Tor exits to the allow list
Email the physician to change their password later
steps.rationale: Killing sessions and resetting credentials cuts the attacker quickly.

questions.prompt: What confirms this is account abuse?
options: A Tor-exit sign-in followed by MFA push spam and a bulk file download
The physician travels often
The login used the corporate laptop
questions.explain: Tor plus push fatigue plus mass download is a clear takeover pattern.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Conditional access blocking anonymizer IPs with phishing-resistant MFA
Allow sign-ins from any IP
Disable sign-in logging
questions.explain: Blocking anonymizer ranges and using strong MFA stops Tor-based abuse.

hints: Confirm the source IP is on a Tor exit list in Threat Intel.
Tor abuse often pairs with MFA fatigue and a data grab.
Check what the session accessed right after sign-in.

answerKey.rootCause: A physician's M365 account was accessed from a Tor exit node and used to bulk-download files.
containment: Block anonymizer IPs via conditional access

scenarios.debrief: A Tor-exit sign-in for a clinician is rarely benign. The skill is confirming the source and watching what the session did next.

scenarios.title: AI Spear Phish

briefing.subject: Exec assistant reported a slick CEO email
briefing.body: An assistant in the CEO's office reported an email that looked exactly like our chief of staff asking to review a vendor contract. The grammar is flawless and the tone is spot on, which is unusual for our usual phishing. Confirm whether this is a targeted AI-generated spear-phish and find the malicious link.
objectives: Confirm the email is a targeted spear-phish
Identify the malicious link and sender
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Sender domain spoofs chief of staff

steps.prompt: What signals an AI-generated spear-phish over generic spam?
options: Flawless tone and personalized context aimed at one named target
Lots of spelling errors and a generic greeting
A blank subject line with no body
steps.rationale: AI lets attackers write clean, tailored lures that name real people and roles.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Quarantine the message tenant-wide and block the phishing domain and URL
Reply to the sender to ask if it is real
Delete only the assistant's copy
steps.rationale: Pull every copy and block the indicators before anyone clicks.

questions.prompt: Why is this email more dangerous than typical phishing?
options: It is personalized and well written, so it is more likely to be trusted
It came from inside the network
It has a large attachment
questions.explain: Targeted, polished AI lures defeat the gut-check users rely on.

questions.prompt: Which control best reduces this risk?
options: DMARC enforcement plus user reporting and link rewriting
A stronger spam keyword filter only
Antivirus on the mail server
questions.explain: Authentication, safe links, and easy reporting catch spoofed targeted mail.

hints: Compare the sender domain to bvhn.org letter by letter.
The reported link points to a contract review portal flagged in Threat Intel.
Polished tone and a named target are the tells of an AI-crafted lure.

answerKey.rootCause: A targeted AI-generated spear-phish spoofed the chief of staff to lure an exec assistant to a malicious portal.
containment: Block the phishing domain and URL

scenarios.debrief: AI removes the spelling tells we trained users to spot. Judge intent and sender authenticity, not grammar.

scenarios.title: PHI In Public Chatbot

briefing.subject: PHI pasted into a public chatbot
briefing.body: Our CASB flagged a billing clerk pasting a block of patient records into a public AI chatbot to draft an appeal letter. This is shadow AI and it may be a HIPAA reportable event. Confirm what data left, who did it, and whether it was malicious or careless.
objectives: Confirm PHI was pasted into the public chatbot
Identify the user and what data left
Recommend a containment action

steps.prompt: What makes this shadow AI a real incident?
options: Sensitive PHI left to an unsanctioned external service
An employee used an approved internal tool
A search engine query with no data attached
steps.rationale: PHI leaving to an unapproved service is a data exposure regardless of intent.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block the chatbot domain, capture what was pasted, and open a HIPAA review
Fire the clerk immediately
Ignore it since the clerk meant well
steps.rationale: Stop the channel, preserve evidence, and start the privacy review.

questions.prompt: Was this exposure most likely malicious or careless?
options: Careless, the clerk used the tool to do real work faster
Malicious data theft for sale
An external attacker breach
questions.explain: Shadow AI usually comes from well-meaning staff seeking productivity, not theft.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents recurrence?
options: A sanctioned AI tool plus DLP rules that block PHI to public services
Blocking the entire internet
A reminder email once a year
questions.explain: Give a safe option and enforce DLP so PHI cannot leave to public AI.

hints: Check the CASB log for the destination domain and the data type.
The user is a billing clerk, not an admin, which points to intent.
Look for the same paste repeated as the clerk refines the prompt.

answerKey.rootCause: A billing clerk pasted patient records into an unsanctioned public chatbot, exposing PHI to a third party.
containment: Preserve the pasted content as evidence

scenarios.debrief: Shadow AI leaks PHI through good intentions. Provide a sanctioned tool and enforce DLP so the safe path is the easy path.

scenarios.title: DLP Blocked PHI

briefing.subject: DLP blocked a PHI upload, please triage
briefing.body: Our DLP tool blocked an outbound transfer that matched patient identifiers leaving to a personal file share. The control worked, but we still need to know who, what, and why. Triage the alert and decide if this needs escalation or a quiet coaching conversation.
objectives: Confirm the DLP block was a true positive
Identify the user and destination
Decide whether to escalate or coach

steps.prompt: What does a DLP block alert tell you first?
options: The egress was stopped, so you triage intent and scope, not active loss
All the data has already left the network
The user's machine is encrypted by ransomware
steps.rationale: DLP blocked the transfer, so the job is to understand intent and whether more was attempted.

steps.prompt: Best next action after confirming the block?
options: Interview the user and check for other egress attempts
Wipe the user's laptop right away
Close the alert with no review
steps.rationale: Confirm intent and look for retries or other channels before closing.

questions.prompt: Did patient data actually leave the network?
options: No, DLP blocked the transfer before it completed
Yes, it was fully exfiltrated
Yes, it was emailed externally
questions.explain: The block means egress was prevented, but intent still matters.

questions.prompt: When should this be escalated rather than coached?
options: If repeated attempts or other channels show intent to bypass controls
Never, DLP blocks are always benign
Always, every block is a breach
questions.explain: One careless attempt is coaching, repeated evasion is an insider concern.

hints: Confirm the rule that fired matched real patient identifiers.
Look for repeat attempts or a switch to another upload channel.
A single careless block differs from deliberate, repeated evasion.

answerKey.rootCause: A user attempted to upload PHI to a personal cloud share and DLP blocked the egress.

scenarios.debrief: A DLP block is a win, not a breach. Triage the intent behind it and decide between coaching and escalation.

scenarios.title: Nurse MFA Fatigue

briefing.subject: Repeated MFA prompts on a nurse account
briefing.body: A floor nurse called the help desk about a flood of MFA push notifications she did not request. Someone likely has her password and is spamming approvals hoping she taps yes. Confirm whether this is an MFA fatigue attack and whether any push was approved.
objectives: Confirm the MFA push fatigue pattern
Determine if any push was approved
Recommend a containment action

steps.prompt: What defines an MFA fatigue attack?
options: Repeated push prompts the user did not start, hoping for an accidental approval
A single failed password attempt
A user resetting their own password
steps.rationale: The attacker has the password and floods pushes to wear the user down.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Reset the password, revoke sessions, and block the source IP
Tell the nurse to approve one push to stop the noise
Disable MFA for the account
steps.rationale: Cut the attacker's access and stop the prompts at the source.

questions.prompt: What does the prompt flood imply the attacker already has?
options: The user's valid password
Domain admin rights
Physical access to the building
questions.explain: Push fatigue only works once the password is known, leaving MFA as the last gate.

questions.prompt: Which control best defeats MFA fatigue?
options: Number matching or FIDO2 keys instead of simple approve prompts
A longer password policy
Email-based one-time codes
questions.explain: Number matching and phishing-resistant keys stop blind approvals.

hints: Count the push prompts per minute against the nurse's normal pattern.
Check whether any push shows an approved result.
The push source IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker with the nurse's password spammed MFA pushes to coerce an accidental approval.

scenarios.debrief: If the password is gone, MFA is the last line. Number matching turns a blind tap into a deliberate choice.

scenarios.title: Defender Turned Off

briefing.subject: Defender disabled on a radiology workstation
briefing.body: Our endpoint console shows Microsoft Defender real-time protection switched off on a radiology workstation. No one filed a change request. Confirm whether this was a user, an admin, or an attacker disabling defenses, and decide if the host needs isolation.
objectives: Confirm Defender protection was disabled
Determine who or what disabled it
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Account on the radiology workstation with protection off

steps.prompt: Why is Defender being disabled a red flag?
options: Attackers turn off defenses to evade detection before acting
It always means a routine software update
It improves the machine's performance safely
steps.rationale: Disabling protection is a classic defense evasion step before malware runs.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Isolate the host, re-enable protection, and scan for what ran while it was off
Leave it off and monitor
Reboot and assume it is fine
steps.rationale: Contain the host and investigate the window where protection was down.

questions.prompt: What was the likely purpose of disabling Defender?
options: To evade detection so other malicious actions could run
To free up disk space
To install a printer driver
questions.explain: Defense evasion clears the way for the next stage of an attack.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Tamper protection and alerting on protection-state changes
A faster CPU
Disabling Windows updates
questions.explain: Tamper protection blocks unauthorized changes and alerts on them.

hints: Check the event for the account and process that changed the setting.
Look for an encoded PowerShell command near the same time.
No change request plus a script tool points away from a sanctioned change.

answerKey.rootCause: A script disabled Microsoft Defender on a radiology workstation as a defense evasion step.

scenarios.debrief: Defenses going dark with no change ticket is rarely benign. Treat protection-off as an early evasion signal.

scenarios.title: Scheduled Task Persistence

briefing.subject: New scheduled task firing at logon
briefing.body: EDR flagged a new scheduled task on a finance workstation that runs a script every time the user logs on. No software was installed that would explain it. Confirm whether this is attacker persistence and identify what it launches.
objectives: Confirm the scheduled task is malicious persistence
Identify what the task launches
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Finance account whose logon triggers the task

steps.prompt: Why do attackers create scheduled tasks?
options: To re-launch their code automatically and survive reboots
To speed up Windows boot times
To uninstall antivirus updates
steps.rationale: A logon-triggered task gives durable persistence across restarts.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Disable the task, capture the payload, and isolate the host
Just delete the task and move on
Reboot to clear it
steps.rationale: Preserve the payload and isolate before the next logon fires it again.

questions.prompt: What does the logon trigger achieve for the attacker?
options: Persistence, the payload runs every time the user signs in
A one-time benign script
Faster network speeds
questions.explain: Logon triggers re-run the payload reliably without re-infection.

questions.prompt: Which artifact best confirms intent?
options: The command line and payload hash the task executes
The task's display name only
The desktop wallpaper
questions.explain: The action it runs reveals whether it is malicious or routine.

hints: Inspect the task's action and the binary or script it calls.
Check the payload hash against Threat Intel.
A logon trigger with no matching install is a persistence tell.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker created a logon-triggered scheduled task to persist a malicious payload on a finance workstation.

scenarios.debrief: Scheduled tasks are a favorite persistence trick. Read what the task runs, not just its name, to judge intent.

scenarios.title: Registry Run Key

briefing.subject: New Run key added in the registry
briefing.body: EDR reported a new value under the registry Run key on a billing workstation that points to a file in a temp folder. Run keys launch programs at logon and are a common persistence spot. Confirm whether this entry is malicious and what it starts.
objectives: Confirm the Run key entry is malicious persistence
Identify the file it launches
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Binary referenced by the Run key
Billing account whose logon runs the key

steps.prompt: What makes a Run key value suspicious?
options: It auto-launches a file from a temp or unusual path at logon
It is a default Windows entry
It only stores a wallpaper setting
steps.rationale: Legitimate software rarely autoruns from a user temp folder.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Remove the Run value, capture the file, and isolate the host
Delete the file but leave the key
Restart the machine and ignore it
steps.rationale: Pull the autorun, preserve the payload, and contain the host.

questions.prompt: When does the Run key payload execute?
options: At user logon, giving the attacker persistence
Only during a full system scan
Never, Run keys are inert
questions.explain: Run keys execute their target at logon, a simple persistence method.

questions.prompt: Which detail most strongly suggests malice?
options: The target file sits in a temp folder and is flagged in Threat Intel
The key has a long name
The value was added on a Monday
questions.explain: A temp-folder payload with a bad reputation is a strong malicious signal.

hints: Look at the path the Run value points to, especially temp folders.
Check the referenced file's hash against Threat Intel.
Compare the entry to known good autoruns to rule out a false positive.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker added a registry Run key on a billing workstation to autorun a malicious binary at logon.

scenarios.debrief: Run keys are a quiet persistence spot. A logon autorun from a temp folder with a bad hash is a clear tell.

scenarios.title: BEC Wire Fraud Rule

briefing.subject: Finance flagged a redirected vendor payment
briefing.body: A controller in BVHN Finance almost wired a vendor payment to a new account after an email thread that looked normal. The mailbox of our AP clerk shows a hidden inbox rule moving vendor replies into an archive folder. Contain the account, find the rule, and confirm whether any wire actually left.
objectives: Confirm the account takeover and the hidden rule
Trace the fraudulent wire request
Contain the mailbox and reset access

iocs.note: AP clerk mailbox with hidden rule
Lookalike vendor domain in the thread

steps.prompt: What is the purpose of the hidden inbox rule in a BEC case?
options: Hide vendor replies so the real owner never sees the fraud thread
Forward spam to junk automatically
Sort newsletters into a folder
steps.rationale: Attackers create rules to move or delete legitimate replies so the victim stays unaware while the wire is arranged.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Reset the AP clerk credentials, revoke sessions, and delete the malicious inbox rule
Block the entire vendor domain at the firewall
Reimage the finance workstation
steps.rationale: Kill the attacker session, restore mailbox integrity, and remove the persistence rule before anything else.

questions.prompt: What confirms this is BEC and not normal vendor email?
options: A hidden rule plus a sign-in from an impossible-travel location preceded the wire request
The email had an attachment
The vendor changed their logo
questions.explain: Account takeover signals plus the concealment rule are the BEC signature.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this fraud?
options: Out-of-band callback verification for any bank-detail change plus MFA
A stronger spam filter only
Longer passwords
questions.explain: Verifying payment changes by phone to a known number defeats the redirect even if email is compromised.

hints: Check mailbox audit logs for new inbox rules created by the user.
Correlate the rule creation time with an impossible-travel sign-in.
The lookalike vendor domain is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: The AP clerk mailbox was taken over, a hidden rule concealed vendor replies, and the attacker requested a redirected wire.
containment: Verify and halt the wire out-of-band

scenarios.debrief: BEC hides in plain sight. The tell is a concealment rule paired with an anomalous sign-in, and the fix is verifying money movement out-of-band.

scenarios.title: AiTM Token Theft

briefing.subject: Stolen session token and a new app grant
briefing.body: A clinician fell for an adversary-in-the-middle phishing page that proxied the real login and stole a session token. The attacker replayed the token without MFA and then granted a sketchy OAuth app broad mailbox access. Confirm the token theft, find the rogue grant, and contain the identity.
objectives: Confirm the AiTM token replay
Identify the malicious OAuth consent
Revoke tokens and contain the account

iocs.note: Replayed session token, no MFA prompt

steps.prompt: Why did MFA not stop this sign-in?
options: A stolen session token was replayed, so MFA was already satisfied
MFA was disabled tenant-wide
The user shared their password
steps.rationale: AiTM steals the post-MFA token, so replay bypasses the prompt entirely.

steps.prompt: Best containment for stolen tokens plus a rogue OAuth app?
options: Revoke all refresh tokens, reset credentials, and remove the malicious app consent
Block the user mailbox only
Force a password change and stop
steps.rationale: Tokens persist until revoked and the OAuth grant survives a password reset, so both must be killed.

questions.prompt: What is the persistence mechanism here?
options: The OAuth consent granting an app standing mailbox access
A scheduled task on the endpoint
A hidden local admin account
questions.explain: The OAuth grant keeps access even after the token is revoked, so it must be removed.

questions.prompt: Which control best counters AiTM?
options: Phishing-resistant MFA and token binding plus conditional access
A longer session timeout
Antivirus on endpoints
questions.explain: FIDO2 and token binding stop replay of stolen session tokens.

hints: Look for a sign-in that satisfied MFA with no interactive prompt.
Check the audit log for new OAuth app consents by the user.
The AiTM proxy URL is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An AiTM page stole a post-MFA session token, the attacker replayed it, then added a malicious OAuth grant for persistence.
containment: Revoke all refresh and session tokens

scenarios.debrief: AiTM beats password-based MFA by stealing the token. Containment must revoke tokens and pull the OAuth grant, not just reset the password.

scenarios.title: Double Extortion Ransomware

briefing.subject: File server encrypting and a ransom note appeared
briefing.body: The BVHN clinical file server is encrypting shares fast and staff report a ransom note on their desktops. Before encryption we see a large outbound transfer over HTTPS, so this is double extortion. Confirm the exfil, scope the encryption, and contain the host now.
objectives: Confirm the exfiltration before encryption
Scope the encryption and the ransom note
Isolate the file server immediately

iocs.note: Ransomware locker binary on the file server

steps.prompt: What makes this double extortion rather than plain ransomware?
options: Data was exfiltrated over HTTPS before the files were encrypted
Multiple hosts were hit
The note demands cryptocurrency
steps.rationale: Double extortion steals data first, then encrypts, so victims face leak plus lockout.

steps.prompt: Best immediate containment action?
options: Isolate the file server from the network to stop encryption and any further exfil
Pay the ransom to get the key
Run a full antivirus scan and wait
steps.rationale: Network isolation halts spread and exfil while preserving the host for forensics.

questions.prompt: Which event proves data was stolen, not just encrypted?
options: A large HTTPS upload to an external IP that preceded the encryption
The ransom note text
High CPU on the server
questions.explain: The outbound transfer before encryption is the exfiltration evidence.

questions.prompt: What recovery step matters most after isolation?
options: Restore from clean offline backups after eradicating the foothold
Reboot the server
Decrypt files with a public tool
questions.explain: Clean offline backups enable recovery without paying, once the intrusion is removed.

hints: Look for a large outbound HTTPS transfer just before the encryption burst.
Group rapid file modification events on the file server host.
The exfil destination IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An intruder staged and exfiltrated clinical data over HTTPS, then deployed ransomware that encrypted the file server shares.
containment: Isolate the file server from the network

scenarios.debrief: Double extortion means the breach happened before the lock. Always check for exfiltration so HIPAA breach notification is handled, not just the encryption.

scenarios.title: Patient Portal Web Shell

briefing.subject: Suspicious commands from the patient portal
briefing.body: Our internet-facing patient portal is throwing odd web requests and now appears to be running shell commands. It looks like an unpatched vulnerability was exploited and a web shell was dropped. Confirm the exploit, find the shell, and contain the web host before PHI is touched.
objectives: Confirm the exploited vulnerability
Locate the web shell and its activity
Contain the web server and remove the shell

iocs.note: Web shell file dropped on the patient portal

steps.prompt: What is the clearest sign of a web shell?
options: The web service spawning command interpreters in response to web requests
High page-load latency
A spike in legitimate logins
steps.rationale: A web server process launching cmd or shell binaries is the hallmark of a deployed web shell.

steps.prompt: Best containment for an exploited web host?
options: Isolate the host, remove the web shell, and patch the exploited vulnerability
Restart the web service only
Block the source IP and leave the shell
steps.rationale: You must remove the shell and close the vulnerability, or the attacker simply returns.

questions.prompt: How did the attacker gain initial access?
options: Exploiting an unpatched vulnerability on the internet-facing portal
A phished employee credential
A malicious USB device
questions.explain: The exploit requests against the portal preceded the shell, marking the CVE as the entry point.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this entry?
options: Timely patching plus a WAF and least-privilege for the web service account
Disabling email links
Longer passwords
questions.explain: Patching closes the hole and a WAF blocks exploit traffic before it lands.

hints: Look for the web service process spawning a command interpreter.
Trace the exploit requests back to a single external source.
The exploit source IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An unpatched vulnerability on the patient portal was exploited and a web shell was dropped for remote command execution.

scenarios.debrief: Internet-facing apps invite web shells. The skill is spotting the web process spawning a shell, then patching the root vulnerability rather than just killing the process.

scenarios.title: Lateral Move To DC

briefing.subject: Admin hash reused toward the domain controller
briefing.body: A compromised workstation is making SMB connections to other hosts using a stolen admin hash, and the trail now points at our domain controller. This is pass-the-hash lateral movement and the DC is the prize. Confirm the technique, map the path, and contain before domain dominance.
objectives: Confirm pass-the-hash lateral movement
Map the path toward the domain controller
Contain the affected hosts and credentials

iocs.note: SMB 445 connections targeting the domain controller

steps.prompt: What signals pass-the-hash rather than a normal admin logon?
options: Network logons using NTLM hash auth with no interactive logon, spreading host to host
A single failed RDP attempt
A user changing their password
steps.rationale: PtH shows NTLM network logons reusing the same credential material across many hosts without interactive sign-in.

steps.prompt: Best containment for active PtH toward the DC?
options: Isolate compromised hosts and reset the admin credential, including the krbtgt if needed
Reboot the domain controller
Block outbound internet only
steps.rationale: Cutting the hosts and rotating the exposed admin credential breaks the reuse chain before DC takeover.

questions.prompt: Why is the domain controller the critical target?
options: Control of the DC gives domain-wide credential and access dominance
It stores email backups
It runs the public website
questions.explain: Owning the DC lets the attacker mint tickets and access any system, so it is the high-value endpoint.

questions.prompt: Which control best limits pass-the-hash?
options: Credential isolation, tiered admin, and disabling NTLM where possible
A longer password policy only
More antivirus signatures
questions.explain: Tiered admin and credential isolation stop a single stolen hash from reaching the DC.

hints: Look for NTLM network logons reusing one admin credential across hosts.
Follow the SMB connection chain toward the domain controller.
The reused admin account is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A stolen admin hash was reused over SMB to move laterally from a workstation toward the domain controller.
containment: Rotate krbtgt if the DC was reached

scenarios.debrief: Pass-the-hash turns one stolen credential into domain reach. Tiered admin and credential isolation keep a workstation compromise from becoming a DC compromise.

scenarios.title: App Server Cryptomining

briefing.subject: Pinned CPU on a billing app server
briefing.body: A BVHN billing application server is pegged at full CPU around the clock and the bill-run jobs are slow. Network logs show steady traffic to a mining pool. Confirm cryptomining, find how it persists, and contain the host without breaking the billing service longer than needed.
objectives: Confirm the cryptomining activity
Identify the persistence mechanism
Contain and clean the app server

iocs.note: Cryptominer binary on the billing app server

steps.prompt: What best confirms cryptomining over a runaway process?
options: Sustained max CPU paired with steady traffic to a known mining pool
A one-time CPU spike
High disk usage only
steps.rationale: Mining shows constant CPU plus persistent connections to a pool, not a transient spike.

steps.prompt: Best containment that limits billing downtime?
options: Block the pool, kill the miner, and remove the scheduled-task persistence, then validate the service
Power off the server indefinitely
Ignore it since it is only CPU
steps.rationale: Removing the miner and its persistence restores capacity while keeping the billing service available.

questions.prompt: How does the miner survive a reboot?
options: A scheduled task relaunches the miner
A browser extension
A signed driver
questions.explain: The scheduled task is the persistence that restarts the miner after reboot.

questions.prompt: Why does cryptomining still matter even if no data is stolen?
options: It signals a foothold and degrades clinical and billing services
It is harmless background noise
It improves performance
questions.explain: A miner means an attacker has code execution and could pivot, and it starves real workloads.

hints: Correlate sustained full CPU with outbound connections to a mining pool.
Check scheduled tasks for an unfamiliar entry that relaunches a process.
The mining pool domain is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An exploited billing app server runs a cryptominer kept alive by a scheduled task and beaconing to a mining pool.
containment: Kill the miner and remove the scheduled task

scenarios.debrief: A miner is a symptom of a foothold, not just wasted CPU. Remove the persistence and find the entry point, or it comes right back.

scenarios.title: Macro Beacon Chain

briefing.subject: Enabled macro led to an encoded payload
briefing.body: A radiology coordinator opened a macro-enabled document that ran an encoded PowerShell command, which then started beaconing to a command-and-control server. Confirm the macro execution, follow the encoded payload, and contain the endpoint before the beacon pulls more tooling.
objectives: Confirm the malicious macro execution
Decode the PowerShell payload behavior
Contain the endpoint and cut the beacon

steps.prompt: What is the execution chain here?
options: Document macro launched encoded PowerShell that started a C2 beacon
A user manually ran a script
A driver loaded at boot
steps.rationale: The macro spawned an encoded PowerShell child that established the beacon, a classic lure-to-C2 chain.

steps.prompt: Best containment for the beaconing endpoint?
options: Isolate the host, kill the PowerShell process, and block the C2 domain
Delete the email only
Reboot and hope it clears
steps.rationale: Isolation and blocking the C2 cut the channel before more tooling is pulled down.

questions.prompt: Why was the PowerShell encoded?
options: To obfuscate the command and evade simple detection
To run faster
To compress a large file
questions.explain: Base64 or encoded commands hide intent from basic logging and signatures.

questions.prompt: Which control best breaks this chain early?
options: Disable Office macros from the internet and enable script-block logging
Block all PDF files
Disable the firewall
questions.explain: Blocking internet-sourced macros stops the first stage before PowerShell ever runs.

hints: Look for an Office process spawning powershell.exe with an encoded command.
Trace the PowerShell child making outbound beacon connections.
The C2 domain is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A macro-enabled document ran encoded PowerShell that beaconed to a C2 server, giving the attacker a foothold.

scenarios.debrief: The lure-to-beacon chain starts with one enabled macro. Blocking internet macros and watching for Office spawning PowerShell stops it at the door.

scenarios.title: HTTPS Data Exfiltration

briefing.subject: Large encrypted upload from a compromised host
briefing.body: After a known foothold on a BVHN workstation, DLP and network logs show data being collected into an archive and pushed out over HTTPS to an external endpoint. This looks like PHI leaving the network. Confirm the staging, scope the exfil, and contain before more records are lost.
objectives: Confirm the data staging on the host
Scope the HTTPS exfiltration volume
Contain the host and block the destination

iocs.note: HTTPS exfil destination for staged data
Compromised account staging data into an archive

steps.prompt: What confirms exfiltration over normal web traffic?
options: Local staging into an archive followed by a large sustained HTTPS upload to one external IP
A few small web requests
A user browsing news sites
steps.rationale: Staging plus a large one-directional upload to a single endpoint is the exfil pattern, not casual browsing.

steps.prompt: Best containment to stop ongoing exfil?
options: Isolate the host and block the destination IP at the egress
Delete the staged archive only
Wait for the transfer to finish
steps.rationale: Isolation and an egress block cut the channel immediately and stop further data loss.

questions.prompt: Why is HTTPS a common exfil channel?
options: It blends with normal encrypted web traffic and is rarely blocked
It is faster than other protocols
It cannot be logged
questions.explain: Encrypted outbound web traffic is ubiquitous, so exfil hides in the noise unless volume and destination are watched.

questions.prompt: What must follow containment given likely PHI?
options: Scope the records lost and start HIPAA breach assessment
Close the ticket
Only reset the user password
questions.explain: PHI leaving the network triggers breach assessment and notification obligations.

hints: Look for files being collected into an archive before the upload.
Measure outbound HTTPS volume to a single external IP over time.
The exfil destination IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A previously compromised workstation staged data into an archive and exfiltrated it over HTTPS to an external endpoint.
containment: Block the exfil destination at egress
Scope records lost and begin breach assessment

scenarios.debrief: Exfil hides inside ordinary HTTPS. Watching upload volume and destination, plus catching the staging step, is how you stop a PHI breach in progress.

scenarios.title: Cloud Ransom Backup Wipe

briefing.subject: Radiology image buckets re-encrypted, backups gone
briefing.body: Our cloud team cannot open imaging studies in the radiology object store. Backup snapshots were deleted minutes before and objects are now re-encrypted with a key we do not own. An access key tied to a service identity is signing in from a strange region. Contain the identity and stop the destruction before more PHI is locked.
objectives: Confirm the cloud ransom and backup deletion
Identify the abused IAM identity and access key
Contain the key and protect remaining objects

iocs.note: Service key used from foreign region

steps.prompt: What confirms a cloud ransom rather than a storage outage?
options: Backups deleted then objects re-encrypted with an external key
A single bucket returning a slow read
A region failover event
steps.rationale: Deleting backups first, then re-encrypting objects, is the cloud ransom pattern.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Disable the compromised access key and revoke its sessions
Delete the radiology bucket
Rotate every key in the org at once
steps.rationale: Kill the abused key and its sessions to stop further destruction, then scope wider.

questions.prompt: Why did the attacker delete backups before encrypting?
options: To remove the easy recovery path and force payment
To free up storage space
To speed up replication
questions.explain: Removing snapshots first denies a clean restore and increases ransom leverage.

questions.prompt: Which control best limits this blast radius?
options: Immutable backups plus least-privilege keys and MFA on deletes
A longer bucket name
Disabling logging
questions.explain: Immutable backups and tight key scope stop one key from wiping recovery.

hints: Trace the access key in cloud trail and note the foreign region.
Backup deletes precede the re-encryption events.
The access key is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A leaked service access key with broad permissions was used to delete backups and re-encrypt imaging objects.

scenarios.debrief: Cloud ransom kills your backups first. Immutable snapshots and least-privilege keys decide whether you recover or pay.

scenarios.title: Rogue Admin Persistence

briefing.subject: New domain admin nobody created
briefing.body: After we cleaned a phishing foothold last night, a new account quietly appeared in the Domain Admins group. It was created off-hours and then elevated. The intruder may be holding persistence so they can walk back in. Find the rogue account, confirm how it got elevated, and lock it down.
objectives: Confirm the unauthorized account creation
Trace the privilege escalation path
Contain the rogue admin and persistence

steps.prompt: What signals a rogue admin rather than normal provisioning?
options: An account created off-hours with no ticket then added to Domain Admins
A help desk password reset
A user changing their display name
steps.rationale: Off-hours creation with no change record plus immediate elevation is attacker persistence.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Disable the rogue account and remove it from privileged groups
Reboot the domain controller
Email all staff to reset passwords
steps.rationale: Disable and de-privilege the rogue account first, then hunt other persistence.

questions.prompt: Why does an intruder create a new admin account?
options: To keep durable access even after the first foothold is cleaned
To reduce license costs
To improve logon speed
questions.explain: A fresh admin account survives cleanup of the original entry point.

questions.prompt: Which detection catches this earliest?
options: Alerting on new members of privileged groups
Counting failed logons only
Disk usage monitoring
questions.explain: Privileged group membership changes are high signal and rare in normal ops.

hints: Look for account creation events with no matching change ticket.
Check who added the account to Domain Admins and when.
The account name is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An intruder created a new account and elevated it to Domain Admin to persist after the initial foothold was remediated.
containment: Audit other new accounts and scheduled tasks

scenarios.debrief: Cleaning the entry point is not enough. Attackers plant a fresh admin to return, so hunt persistence after every eviction.

scenarios.title: Deepfake CFO Wire

briefing.subject: Urgent wire approved on a fake CFO call
briefing.body: Accounts payable got a video call that looked and sounded like our CFO approving an urgent vendor wire. A confirmation email followed asking to skip the usual dual approval. The call was an AI deepfake and the wire is being staged now. Confirm the fraud and stop the transfer.
objectives: Confirm the deepfake and the wire request
Identify the fraudulent payment instruction
Recommend stopping the wire and recall

iocs.note: Lookalike domain used in the follow-up email
Payment portal link in the email

steps.prompt: What is the strongest sign this CFO request is fraud?
options: A deepfake call pushing to bypass dual approval and rush a new payee
A wire that follows the normal approval chain
A vendor invoice with a matching purchase order
steps.rationale: Urgency plus pressure to skip controls and a new payee is the BEC and deepfake tell.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Contact the bank to halt and recall the wire, freeze the payee
Reply to the email to ask for confirmation
Wait for the CFO to call back
steps.rationale: Speed matters. Call the bank to halt and recall before funds settle.

questions.prompt: How was the CFO impersonated?
options: An AI deepfake video and voice on a live call
A stolen laptop
A misconfigured firewall
questions.explain: The attacker synthesized the CFO on a video call to manufacture trust.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this loss?
options: Out-of-band verification and enforced dual approval for new payees
A faster email server
Larger mailbox quotas
questions.explain: Verifying through a known channel and never skipping dual approval defeats the rush.

hints: The follow-up email comes from a lookalike domain, not the real one.
Look for pressure to bypass dual approval on a brand new payee.
The phishing domain is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An AI deepfake call and lookalike follow-up email tricked accounts payable into approving a fraudulent vendor wire.
containment: Call the bank to halt and recall the wire

scenarios.debrief: A convincing voice is not authorization. Out-of-band verification and dual approval stop deepfake wire fraud cold.

scenarios.title: Mailbox Spam Blast

briefing.subject: Billing mailbox blasting spam to patients
briefing.body: A billing clerk mailbox is sending thousands of outbound messages to external recipients. A hidden inbox rule is auto-deleting the bounce replies so the user has no idea. The account was likely phished. Confirm the takeover, find the rule, and contain the mailbox.
objectives: Confirm the compromised mailbox and spam blast
Find the malicious inbox rule
Contain the account and remove the rule

steps.prompt: What confirms a hijacked mailbox over a noisy newsletter?
options: High volume outbound spam plus a hidden rule deleting bounces
A user replying to a known vendor
A scheduled report email
steps.rationale: Mass outbound to externals with a stealth rule hiding bounces is account takeover.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Reset the account, revoke sessions, and delete the malicious rule
Block the patient recipients
Delete the user mailbox
steps.rationale: Reset and revoke to stop sending, then remove the rule that hid the activity.

questions.prompt: Why did the attacker create a hidden inbox rule?
options: To auto-delete bounces and replies so the spam goes unnoticed
To sort mail into folders for the user
To set an out-of-office reply
questions.explain: Deleting bounce-backs keeps the real owner from spotting the blast.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents the takeover?
options: MFA plus alerting on new inbox rules and outbound spikes
A bigger mailbox quota
Disabling read receipts
questions.explain: MFA stops the phish and rule alerts catch the stealth step early.

hints: Check outbound mail volume for the billing mailbox.
Look for a recently created rule that deletes incoming bounces.
The phishing URL is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A phished billing mailbox was used to send spam, with a hidden inbox rule deleting bounces to stay quiet.

scenarios.debrief: Outbound spam from a real user means a takeover. The hidden bounce-deleting rule is the giveaway, so always check mailbox rules.

scenarios.title: Vishing Helpdesk Takeover

briefing.subject: Helpdesk reset MFA for the wrong person
briefing.body: Someone called the helpdesk pretending to be a traveling physician and talked an agent into resetting their MFA. Minutes later that account signed in from two countries in an impossible window. The attacker now controls the account. Confirm the social engineering and contain the takeover.
objectives: Confirm the vishing-driven MFA reset
Spot the impossible travel sign-in
Contain the account and reverse the reset

steps.prompt: What confirms a vishing-led takeover?
options: A phone-driven MFA reset followed by an impossible-travel sign-in
A user logging in from their usual office
A password change during business hours
steps.rationale: A helpdesk reset by phone then a sign-in from a far region in minutes is the takeover chain.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Disable the account, revoke sessions, and re-enroll MFA in person
Tell the physician to try again later
Block the entire country range
steps.rationale: Disable and re-verify identity in person before restoring access.

questions.prompt: What made the helpdesk reset succeed?
options: The agent verified identity weakly under social-engineering pressure
A software bug in the MFA app
A network outage
questions.explain: The caller used urgency and a believable story to bypass weak verification.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Strong callback verification before any MFA reset
Longer passwords only
Disabling email forwarding
questions.explain: Verified callbacks and identity proofing stop attackers from resetting MFA by phone.

hints: Correlate the helpdesk MFA reset time with the next sign-in.
Two logins from distant locations in minutes is impossible travel.
The foreign sign-in IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker vished the helpdesk into resetting MFA, then signed in from a foreign location to take over the physician account.

scenarios.debrief: The helpdesk is an identity control. Strong callback verification before any MFA reset stops vishing takeovers.

scenarios.title: Database Dump Exfil

briefing.subject: Large patient database export leaving over HTTPS
briefing.body: A service account ran a full export of the patient records database, then staged the dump and pushed it to an external host over HTTPS. The volume is far above any normal report job. This looks like deliberate PHI theft. Confirm the dump and the exfil channel and contain it.
objectives: Confirm the database dump activity
Trace the HTTPS exfiltration channel
Contain the account and block the destination

iocs.note: Service account that ran the export

steps.prompt: What separates this from a normal reporting job?
options: A full table dump staged then sent to an unknown external host
A nightly report to an internal share
A small query returning one record
steps.rationale: A full export staged and pushed to an unknown external host is theft, not reporting.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Disable the service account and block the external destination
Restart the database server
Archive the logs and wait
steps.rationale: Kill the account and block the egress to stop the bleed, then preserve evidence.

questions.prompt: How was the data taken out?
options: Staged locally then exfiltrated over HTTPS to an external host
Printed to paper
Copied to the local screen
questions.explain: The dump was staged and tunneled out over HTTPS to blend with normal web traffic.

questions.prompt: Which control best detects this earlier?
options: Egress monitoring with data volume baselines on service accounts
A new screensaver policy
Disabling USB ports only
questions.explain: Baselining service-account egress flags an abnormal bulk transfer fast.

hints: Compare the export size to a normal nightly report.
Follow the staged file to the HTTPS upload destination.
The exfil host IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A misused service account dumped the patient database and exfiltrated it over HTTPS to an external host.
containment: Preserve logs and notify privacy office

scenarios.debrief: Bulk dumps over HTTPS hide in normal web traffic. Egress baselines on service accounts catch the abnormal volume.

scenarios.title: SQLi PHI Theft

briefing.subject: Billing portal SQL injection hitting PHI
briefing.body: The public billing portal is taking malformed inputs that pull patient records straight from the database. The web logs show classic injection strings and the app is returning more rows than any page should. DLP blocked one large response, but the attacker is still probing. Confirm the SQL injection and contain it.
objectives: Confirm the SQL injection on the billing app
Assess what PHI was exposed
Contain the web app and the attacker

iocs.note: External host the records were sent to

steps.prompt: What confirms SQL injection over a benign error?
options: Malformed inputs returning extra rows and database error strings
A single 404 on a missing image
A slow page load once
steps.rationale: Injection payloads that change query results and leak DB errors confirm SQLi.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block the source IP and put the app behind a WAF rule or take it offline
Increase the database memory
Email patients to change passwords
steps.rationale: Stop the attacker and the vulnerable path first, then patch the input handling.

questions.prompt: Why did DLP only block part of the theft?
options: DLP caught one large response but probing continued on other queries
DLP fixed the injection flaw
DLP rebuilt the database
questions.explain: DLP blocks symptoms like big responses but the underlying injection stays open.

questions.prompt: Which control truly fixes the root cause?
options: Parameterized queries and input validation in the app
A stronger admin password
More disk space
questions.explain: Parameterized queries stop user input from altering the SQL itself.

hints: Search web logs for injection payloads on the billing portal.
Note the DLP block and check what queries ran around it.
The attacker IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: The public billing portal was vulnerable to SQL injection, letting an attacker pull patient records until DLP and containment kicked in.
containment: Put the app behind a WAF or take it offline

scenarios.debrief: DLP buys time but does not fix the hole. Parameterized queries are the real cure for SQL injection on PHI apps.

scenarios.title: Beacon Hunt By Timing

briefing.subject: Hypothesis: a quiet C2 beacon on the billing subnet
briefing.body: No alert fired, but threat intel says actors are using long, jittered beacons to stay under volume thresholds. We think one billing workstation may be calling out on a fixed cadence. Hunt the egress logs for regular, low-volume connections and tie them to a host process.
objectives: Find a regular low-volume beacon by its timing pattern
Identify the LOLBin process making the calls
Confirm the destination is the C2 and scope the host

steps.prompt: What signature best reveals a hidden C2 beacon in egress logs?
options: Regular, low-volume connections to one destination at a fixed interval with jitter
A single large file upload
Many failed logons from one source
steps.rationale: Beacons phone home on a cadence. Grouping by destination and measuring the time delta exposes the rhythm even when each call is tiny.

steps.prompt: Best first containment for the beaconing host?
options: Isolate the host from the network and block the C2 domain and IP at the proxy
Reboot the workstation and move on
Email the user to stop browsing
steps.rationale: Isolation cuts the channel while you preserve the host for forensics, and blocking the C2 stops other hosts from reaching it.

questions.prompt: Which process was making the beacon calls?
options: A trusted Windows binary abused as a LOLBin to proxy traffic
The browser updater
The print spooler service
questions.explain: The beacon rode a signed system binary so it blended with normal traffic, which is why volume thresholds never tripped.

questions.prompt: Why did no alert fire on this activity?
options: The beacon stayed below volume and rate thresholds by design
The SIEM was offline
The host had no logging agent
questions.explain: Low and slow beacons are tuned to evade rate-based detection, so timing analysis, not volume, is what finds them.

hints: Group egress by destination and compute the time delta between connections.
A near-constant interval with small jitter is the tell, not the byte count.
Pivot from the destination IP to the local process that opened the socket.

answerKey.rootCause: A billing workstation ran an implant that beaconed to C2 on a jittered fixed interval through a signed LOLBin to evade volume thresholds.
containment: Hunt other hosts for the same cadence

scenarios.debrief: C2 beacons hide in timing, not volume. Group by destination, measure the interval, and the cadence gives the implant away.

scenarios.title: DNS Tunnel Covert Channel

briefing.subject: Hypothesis: data leaving over DNS
briefing.body: DNS is allowed out everywhere, which makes it a perfect covert channel. We suspect a host in radiology is encoding data into long DNS queries to a single domain. Hunt the resolver logs for abnormal query length and volume to one zone and confirm whether PHI could be leaving this way.
objectives: Spot the abnormal DNS query pattern to one domain
Identify the host running the tunnel
Confirm the channel is exfiltration and scope it

steps.prompt: What in resolver logs signals DNS tunneling?
options: High volume of long, high-entropy subdomain queries to one zone
A single failed name lookup
A normal A record for a known site
steps.rationale: Tunnels encode data into subdomain labels, so queries are unusually long, random looking, and concentrated on one zone.

steps.prompt: Best containment for the tunneling domain?
options: Sinkhole or block the domain at the resolver and isolate the source host
Disable DNS for the whole site
Ignore it since DNS is normal traffic
steps.rationale: Blocking the zone at the resolver kills the channel network-wide while isolating the host stops further encoding.

questions.prompt: What was being carried in the DNS queries?
options: Encoded chunks of data exfiltrated from the host
Normal cached lookups
NTP synchronization data
questions.explain: The long encoded labels are data smuggled out a label at a time, which is how DNS tunneling exfiltrates while looking like name resolution.

questions.prompt: Why is DNS attractive for a covert channel?
options: DNS is almost always allowed outbound and rarely inspected deeply
DNS is encrypted by default
DNS cannot carry arbitrary data
questions.explain: Because resolvers forward queries freely, attackers tunnel data through a protocol few teams inspect at the payload level.

hints: Sort DNS by query length and count queries per parent zone.
High entropy in the subdomain labels means encoded data, not real hostnames.
One zone receiving thousands of unique long names is the tunnel.

answerKey.rootCause: A radiology host ran a DNS tunneling client that encoded data into long subdomain queries to a single attacker zone to exfiltrate over an allowed protocol.

scenarios.debrief: DNS tunneling hides exfiltration in name lookups. Query length, entropy, and volume to one zone expose the covert channel.

scenarios.title: Kerberoasting Service Tickets

briefing.subject: Hypothesis: someone is roasting our service accounts
briefing.body: We have several service accounts with weak passwords and SPNs set. Threat intel suggests an attacker inside the domain may be requesting service tickets in bulk to crack them offline. Hunt the domain controller logs for a single account pulling many encrypted service tickets, then check for follow-on privilege escalation.
objectives: Find the burst of service ticket requests from one account
Confirm the encryption type points to offline cracking
Tie the cracked credential to privilege escalation

iocs.note: Requested many SPN tickets in a burst
Targeted service account with weak password

steps.prompt: What DC log pattern indicates Kerberoasting?
options: One account requesting many service tickets, often with RC4 encryption, in a short window
A single Kerberos pre-auth failure
A normal user logging into their workstation
steps.rationale: Roasting requests TGS tickets for many SPNs at once, frequently downgraded to RC4 so the hashes crack faster offline.

steps.prompt: Best containment for the roasted service accounts?
options: Rotate the service account passwords with long random secrets and enforce AES, then disable the source account
Delete every service account
Block all Kerberos traffic
steps.rationale: Long random passwords defeat offline cracking, AES removes the weak RC4 path, and disabling the source account stops the collection.

questions.prompt: Why did the attacker request RC4 encrypted tickets?
options: RC4 hashes crack faster offline than AES
RC4 tickets cannot be logged
RC4 grants admin automatically
questions.explain: Downgrading to RC4 yields weaker hashes, so the attacker can recover the service password offline without touching the DC again.

questions.prompt: What made these accounts attractive targets?
options: They had SPNs set and weak, non-rotated passwords
They had no password at all
They were disabled accounts
questions.explain: Any domain user can request a ticket for an account with an SPN, and a weak password makes the offline crack trivial.

hints: Look for event 4769 service ticket requests grouped by the requesting account.
A burst of distinct SPNs from one account, especially RC4, is the roast.
After the crack, watch for the service account gaining new privileges.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised domain account bulk-requested RC4 service tickets for SPN accounts, cracked a weak service password offline, and used it to escalate privileges.

scenarios.debrief: Kerberoasting trades a normal ticket request for an offline crack. Long random service passwords and AES make the loot worthless.

scenarios.title: Pass The Hash Spread

briefing.subject: Hypothesis: an admin hash is moving host to host
briefing.body: We think an attacker has a local admin hash and is using it to authenticate across the clinical subnet without ever knowing the plaintext password. Hunt for NTLM logons that reuse the same account from one source to many hosts over SMB, with no matching interactive logon.
objectives: Find the reused NTLM logon hopping across hosts
Confirm it is pass-the-hash, not a normal logon
Map the lateral path and scope affected hosts

iocs.note: First foothold, source of lateral SMB

steps.prompt: What distinguishes pass-the-hash from a normal admin logon?
options: NTLM network logons reusing one account from a single source to many hosts with no interactive logon
A user typing their password at the console
A successful Kerberos logon with a fresh TGT
steps.rationale: PtH authenticates with the hash over NTLM, so you see type 3 network logons fanning out with no matching interactive sign-in.

steps.prompt: Best containment for a spreading admin hash?
options: Reset the admin account, isolate the source host, and force a domain credential refresh on touched hosts
Change the network cabling
Tell users to log off for the day
steps.rationale: Resetting the credential invalidates the stolen hash, isolating the source stops the spread, and refreshing credentials clears cached secrets.

questions.prompt: Why did the attacker never need the plaintext password?
options: The NTLM hash itself authenticates over the network
NTLM logs the password in clear text
SMB does not require credentials
questions.explain: NTLM proves possession of the hash, so the hash alone authenticates and the attacker can move without ever cracking it.

questions.prompt: Which logon type marks the lateral SMB authentications?
options: Type 3 network logon with NTLM
Type 2 interactive logon
Type 10 remote interactive logon
questions.explain: SMB lateral movement shows up as type 3 network logons, and the NTLM package with a reused account is the pass-the-hash tell.

hints: Group NTLM type 3 logons by account and count distinct destination hosts.
One source touching many hosts with the same admin, no interactive logon, is PtH.
Pivot from the first host to find the original foothold.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker stole a local admin NTLM hash and reused it over SMB to authenticate to many clinical hosts without the plaintext password.
containment: Force credential refresh on touched hosts

scenarios.debrief: Pass-the-hash needs no password, just the hash. Watch for one account fanning out over NTLM with no interactive logon.

scenarios.title: Golden Ticket Forged TGT

briefing.subject: Hypothesis: a forged TGT is granting access
briefing.body: If an attacker stole the krbtgt key, they can mint Kerberos tickets for any user with any privilege. We have signs of access that the directory cannot explain. Hunt for tickets with anomalous lifetimes and accounts that act with privileges they were never granted.
objectives: Find the forged ticket by its anomalous lifetime and claims
Confirm the krbtgt key was likely compromised
Recommend the recovery action that invalidates all golden tickets

iocs.note: DC issuing tickets the directory cannot explain

steps.prompt: What signals a golden ticket in Kerberos logs?
options: TGTs with anomalous lifetimes or group claims the account was never assigned
A normal short-lived ticket for a real user
A single failed pre-authentication
steps.rationale: Forged TGTs are minted offline, so they often carry odd lifetimes and privileged group claims that do not match the directory.

steps.prompt: What action actually invalidates a golden ticket?
options: Reset the krbtgt account password twice and rotate it again on schedule
Reset the targeted user once
Reboot the domain controllers
steps.rationale: Golden tickets are signed with the krbtgt key, so resetting it twice invalidates every forged ticket. User resets alone do nothing.

questions.prompt: What did the attacker need to forge these tickets?
options: The krbtgt account key from the domain
The user's plaintext password
A valid smartcard
questions.explain: The krbtgt key signs all TGTs, so with it an attacker can mint tickets for any user with any privilege, bypassing normal authentication.

questions.prompt: Why is a golden ticket strong persistence?
options: It survives user password resets and grants arbitrary privilege until krbtgt is rotated
It expires within minutes
It only works on one host
questions.explain: Because it is signed with the krbtgt key rather than tied to a user secret, only rotating krbtgt removes it, which makes it durable.

hints: Compare ticket group claims against what the directory actually grants the account.
Anomalous TGT lifetimes are a classic forged-ticket tell.
Recovery centers on the krbtgt key, not the affected user.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker stole the krbtgt key and forged a golden TGT, granting arbitrary privileges that the directory cannot account for.
containment: Hunt for forged tickets in use

scenarios.debrief: A golden ticket is signed by krbtgt, so it ignores user resets. Anomalous lifetimes and unearned privilege expose it, and a double krbtgt reset kills it.

scenarios.title: DCSync Secret Theft

briefing.subject: Hypothesis: a non-DC is replicating directory secrets
briefing.body: DCSync lets an attacker impersonate a domain controller and pull password hashes through replication. We suspect a workstation or non-DC account has requested directory replication. Hunt for replication requests coming from anything that is not a real domain controller and confirm whether secrets were pulled.
objectives: Find the replication request from a non-DC source
Confirm the account abused replication rights
Recommend resetting the exposed directory secrets

iocs.note: Requested replication from a non-DC source
DC that served the replication pull

steps.prompt: What in DC logs reveals DCSync?
options: A directory replication request from an account or host that is not a domain controller
A normal user changing their own password
A failed RDP attempt
steps.rationale: Only DCs should request replication. A replication pull from a workstation or non-DC account is the unmistakable DCSync signature.

steps.prompt: Best response once DCSync is confirmed?
options: Reset the exposed account secrets including krbtgt, and strip replication rights from the abusing account
Reboot the workstation
Ignore it as a replication glitch
steps.rationale: Pulled hashes are now known to the attacker, so the exposed secrets and krbtgt must be reset, and the unneeded replication rights revoked.

questions.prompt: What does DCSync abuse to steal hashes?
options: The directory replication protocol used between domain controllers
A buffer overflow in the DC service
Plaintext passwords stored on disk
questions.explain: DCSync impersonates a DC and asks for replication of account secrets, so it pulls hashes over a legitimate protocol without dropping malware.

questions.prompt: Which right makes an account capable of DCSync?
options: Directory replication rights such as Replicating Directory Changes
Local administrator on a workstation
Print operator membership
questions.explain: The replication extended rights let an account request secrets, so an over-privileged account is what enables the pull.

hints: Hunt for DRSUAPI replication requests and check whether the source is a real DC.
A non-DC account asking to replicate is the smoking gun.
Anything pulled must be treated as compromised, including krbtgt.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised account with replication rights performed DCSync from a non-DC source and pulled directory password hashes.

scenarios.debrief: DCSync steals hashes through legitimate replication. Replication requests from anything but a real DC are the tell, and exposed secrets must be reset.

scenarios.title: Staging Before Exfil

briefing.subject: Hypothesis: PHI is being gathered for theft
briefing.body: Before exfiltration, attackers usually stage data, copying and compressing files into one place. We think someone is collecting PHI from file shares into a single archive on a host before pushing it out over HTTPS. Hunt for the collection and archiving step, then catch the outbound transfer.
objectives: Find the data staging where files are collected and compressed
Identify the host and account doing the staging
Catch the HTTPS exfiltration and scope what left

iocs.note: Staging host where PHI was archived

steps.prompt: What does data staging look like before exfiltration?
options: Files collected from many shares and compressed into a single archive on one host
A user opening one document
A routine nightly backup to the backup server
steps.rationale: Staging gathers and compresses data into one bundle so it can be moved in a single transfer, which is the step before exfil.

steps.prompt: Best containment when staging is found before the transfer completes?
options: Isolate the staging host, block the exfil destination, and preserve the archive for scoping
Delete the archive immediately and move on
Wait to see if anything is uploaded
steps.rationale: Isolating and blocking stops the transfer, and preserving the archive lets you scope exactly which PHI was at risk for breach reporting.

questions.prompt: Why do attackers stage data before exfil?
options: Compressing into one archive makes the outbound transfer faster and quieter
Staging encrypts the network
Staging is required by HTTPS
