questions.explain: Bundling data into a single compressed file reduces the number of transfers and the noise, so the exfil is one quiet push rather than many.

questions.prompt: What confirms the data actually left the network?
options: An outbound HTTPS transfer of the archive to an external destination
A local file copy on the same host
A DNS lookup for an internal name
questions.explain: The exfil is confirmed when the staged archive is pushed out over HTTPS to an external IP, which is the finale of the chain.

hints: Look for many file reads from shares converging into one archive on a single host.
The staging host and account come before the transfer, hunt that step first.
The exfil finale is an outbound HTTPS push to an external destination.

answerKey.rootCause: An account collected PHI from file shares, compressed it into one archive on a staging host, and exfiltrated it over HTTPS.
containment: Preserve the archive to scope the PHI exposed

scenarios.debrief: Exfil is usually preceded by staging. Catch the collection and archiving step and you can stop the transfer before PHI leaves.

scenarios.title: Certutil LOLBin Download

briefing.subject: Hypothesis: living off the land with certutil
briefing.body: Attackers avoid dropping tools by abusing built-in binaries. We suspect a host is using certutil to download a payload and encoded PowerShell to run it, all without new files that antivirus would flag. Hunt process logs for certutil pulling from a URL and for base64 encoded PowerShell commands.
objectives: Find certutil used to download a remote payload
Find the encoded PowerShell that executed it
Tie both to one host and confirm the source URL

steps.prompt: What is the living-off-the-land tell for certutil?
options: Certutil invoked with a URL to download a remote file rather than for certificate work
Certutil checking a local certificate store
Certutil run by the Windows updater at boot
steps.rationale: Certutil is a certificate tool, so a command line that points it at a URL to fetch a file is a classic LOLBin download abuse.

steps.prompt: Best containment for the LOLBin host?
options: Isolate the host, block the payload URL, and hunt for the encoded PowerShell elsewhere
Uninstall certutil from Windows
Reset the user's mailbox password only
steps.rationale: Isolation and blocking the URL stop the payload, and you cannot remove built-in binaries, so you hunt the technique across the fleet.

questions.prompt: Why use certutil and encoded PowerShell instead of dropping a tool?
options: Built-in signed binaries and obfuscated commands evade signature-based antivirus
They are faster than a normal download
They require no network access
questions.explain: Abusing trusted binaries and base64 encoding the command avoids new files and obvious strings, which slips past signature detection.

questions.prompt: How do you decode what the PowerShell actually did?
options: Base64 decode the encoded command to reveal the real script
Read the file extension
Check the antivirus quarantine
questions.explain: The encoded command is just base64, so decoding it reveals the real intent, often a download and execute of the certutil payload.

hints: Search process command lines for certutil with a URL argument.
Look for powershell with an encoded command flag and a long base64 blob.
Decode the base64 to see what the script fetched and ran.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker used certutil to download a payload and base64 encoded PowerShell to execute it, living off the land to avoid antivirus.
containment: Hunt the fleet for the same LOLBin and encoded PowerShell pattern

scenarios.debrief: Living off the land abuses trusted binaries. Certutil fetching a URL and encoded PowerShell are the patterns to hunt, and decoding the command reveals the payload.

scenarios.title: Supply Chain Implant Hunt

briefing.subject: Hunt for a poisoned vendor update
briefing.body: A trusted imaging vendor just disclosed that one of their signed updaters may have shipped a backdoor. We pushed that updater to several radiology workstations last week. No alert has fired, so we are going hunting. Assume the implant is already running and look for its quiet outbound calls.
objectives: Form a hypothesis that a signed update planted an implant
Find the implant's beaconing pattern
Scope which hosts are calling out

steps.prompt: What hunting hypothesis fits a poisoned update with no alert?
options: A signed updater planted an implant that now beacons on a fixed interval
A user clicked a phishing link this morning
The radiology PACS storage filled up
steps.rationale: Supply-chain implants ride trusted signatures, so hunt the behavior after install, not the signature.

steps.prompt: Best first containment for the affected hosts?
options: Isolate the hosts running the bad updater hash and block the C2 domain
Reimage every workstation in the building
Email all staff to ignore the vendor
steps.rationale: Scope by the known-bad hash, isolate those hosts, and cut the beacon at the network.

questions.prompt: What is the clearest sign of beaconing in the logs?
options: Regular outbound calls to one domain on a steady interval
A single large file download
Many failed logons
questions.explain: Fixed-interval callbacks to the same destination are the beacon signature.

questions.prompt: Why did no alert fire on the original install?
options: The updater was signed and trusted, so it bypassed reputation checks
Antivirus was uninstalled by the vendor
The firewall was offline that day
questions.explain: Trusted signatures let supply-chain implants slip past static defenses.

hints: Start from the vendor updater hash, then pivot to what those hosts did next.
Group outbound connections by destination and look for a steady interval.
The beacon domain is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A backdoored signed updater from a trusted imaging vendor planted an implant that beacons to attacker C2.
containment: Isolate hosts with the bad updater hash
Coordinate a clean re-deploy with the vendor

scenarios.debrief: Supply-chain implants hide behind trust. Hunt the post-install behavior, like steady beacons, not the signature.

scenarios.title: RAG Knowledge Poison Hunt

briefing.subject: Hunt for poisoned clinical AI answers
briefing.body: Our clinical RAG assistant has started giving subtly wrong dosing guidance to nurses. Nobody filed a malware alert, but the answers changed after our last knowledge-base sync. We suspect someone slipped tainted documents into the index. Go hunt for the poisoning, not for a virus.
objectives: Form a hypothesis that the knowledge base was poisoned
Trace which ingested documents changed the model's answers
Identify the account that pushed the tainted content

steps.prompt: What hunting hypothesis explains subtly wrong AI answers after a sync?
options: Tainted documents were ingested into the RAG index and now shape responses
The GPU server overheated
A nurse typed the question wrong
steps.rationale: RAG answers reflect indexed content, so poisoned documents in the corpus skew output.

steps.prompt: Best first containment once a poisoned doc is found?
options: Remove the tainted documents and roll back the index to the last clean sync
Shut down the whole hospital network
Delete all nurse accounts
steps.rationale: Purge the poison and restore a known-good index so guidance is safe again.

questions.prompt: What is the strongest evidence of knowledge-base poisoning?
options: Wrong answers that trace back to specific documents added in the last sync
A spike in CPU usage
A new printer on the network
questions.explain: Tying bad output to specific newly ingested documents confirms poisoning.

questions.prompt: Why would no malware alert fire here?
options: The attack is data poisoning, not executable malware
The antivirus license expired
The logs were deleted
questions.explain: Poisoning the corpus changes data, not binaries, so endpoint tools stay quiet.

hints: Compare AI answer quality before and after the last knowledge-base sync.
Find which documents were ingested and who uploaded them.
The tainted document hash is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A suspicious account uploaded poisoned documents into the clinical RAG index, skewing dosing guidance.
containment: Roll back the index to the last clean sync
Disable the uploading account pending review

scenarios.debrief: AI poisoning hides in data, not code. Hunt by tying bad answers back to specific ingested documents and the account that added them.

scenarios.title: IAM Enumeration Persistence Hunt

briefing.subject: Hunt for quiet cloud IAM recon
briefing.body: A leaked access key for our billing cloud account surfaced on a paste site. No login alert fired, but we think someone is already poking around our IAM. Hunt for enumeration of users and roles, then check whether they left a way back in. Treat this as an adversary already inside the account.
objectives: Hypothesize that a leaked key is enumerating IAM
Find the enumeration calls and the source
Detect any new persistence the adversary created

steps.prompt: What hunting hypothesis fits a leaked key with no login alert?
options: The key is being used to enumerate IAM and quietly add persistence
A user forgot their password
The billing report ran late
steps.rationale: Leaked keys often lead to silent recon followed by a foothold the attacker can reuse.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Disable the leaked access key and remove any new IAM users or keys it created
Delete the whole cloud account
Rotate every employee's email password
steps.rationale: Kill the leaked key first, then unwind the persistence it planted.

questions.prompt: What pattern signals IAM enumeration?
options: A burst of list and describe calls across users, roles, and policies
One successful login
A single S3 read
questions.explain: Mass list and describe calls are classic IAM reconnaissance.

questions.prompt: What persistence sign should the hunter confirm?
options: A new IAM user or access key created by the leaked key
A rebooted server
A new printer driver
questions.explain: Creating a fresh user or key gives the attacker a durable way back in.

hints: Filter cloud audit logs for list and describe calls grouped by the leaked key.
Check whether that key created any new IAM users or keys.
The leaked access key is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A leaked access key enumerated IAM and created a new IAM user to maintain access to the billing account.
containment: Remove attacker-created IAM users and keys
Enable alerting on IAM enumeration bursts

scenarios.debrief: Leaked keys lead to quiet recon and a hidden foothold. Hunt the enumeration calls, then always check what new access was created.

scenarios.title: Idle Region Cryptomining Hunt

briefing.subject: Hunt for compute in a region we never use
briefing.body: Finance flagged an odd jump in cloud spend last month. We only run workloads in two regions, yet billing shows compute in a third we never touch. No security alert fired. Hunt for cryptomining spun up in that idle region and find who launched it.
objectives: Hypothesize that an idle region hides unauthorized compute
Confirm the workload is cryptomining
Identify the identity that launched the instances

steps.prompt: Why hunt in a region the org never uses?
options: Attackers pick idle regions because nobody watches them
Idle regions are cheaper to mine in
The billing team prefers that region
steps.rationale: Unused regions get little monitoring, so they are ideal for stealthy mining.

steps.prompt: Best first containment for the rogue compute?
options: Terminate the unauthorized instances and disable the launching identity
Pay the extra cloud bill and move on
Open all regions for general use
steps.rationale: Kill the miners and the identity that started them to stop the spend and the abuse.

questions.prompt: What confirms the workload is mining?
options: High-CPU instances connecting out to a known mining pool
A sudden drop in storage use
A new database table
questions.explain: Sustained high CPU plus mining-pool traffic is the signature of cryptomining.

questions.prompt: What first tipped off the hunt?
options: A jump in cloud spend in a region the org never uses
A failed VPN login
A phishing email
questions.explain: Unexpected spend in an idle region is the cost signal that triggers the hunt.

hints: Pull compute inventory by region and flag any region outside the two you use.
Check the instances' outbound traffic for a mining-pool endpoint.
The mining-pool IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker used a cloud identity to launch cryptomining instances in an unused region to avoid detection.
containment: Restrict and alert on instance launches in unused regions

scenarios.debrief: Attackers mine where no one is looking. Hunt by region and tie unexpected spend to high-CPU compute calling a mining pool.

scenarios.title: Scheduled Task Persistence Sweep

briefing.subject: Sweep hosts for hidden persistence
briefing.body: We cleaned a malware infection on one billing workstation last week, but the attacker may have left autostart entries on other hosts. No new alert has fired. Sweep the fleet for suspicious scheduled tasks and run keys that point at the same tooling. Assume the adversary is still resident on at least one host.
objectives: Hypothesize that persistence was planted across several hosts
Find suspicious scheduled tasks and run keys
Scope how many hosts share the same persistence

iocs.note: Binary launched by the scheduled task
Host with a malicious run key

steps.prompt: What hunting hypothesis fits a cleaned host plus quiet fleet?
options: The attacker planted autostart persistence on other hosts too
The cleanup deleted a printer driver
The billing app needs a reboot
steps.rationale: Attackers spread persistence, so one cleaned host rarely means the threat is gone.

steps.prompt: Best containment once shared persistence is found?
options: Remove the malicious tasks and run keys on every affected host
Reboot one host and assume it spread nowhere
Disable Task Scheduler on the domain controller only
steps.rationale: Eradicate the persistence everywhere it appears, not just on the first host.

questions.prompt: What links the persistence across hosts?
options: Scheduled tasks and run keys that launch the same malicious binary
Different printers on each host
Random reboots
questions.explain: A shared launch binary across autostart entries shows one campaign on many hosts.

questions.prompt: Why does autostart persistence often evade alerts?
options: It blends in with the many legitimate scheduled tasks and run keys
It encrypts the disk loudly
It deletes the event log service
questions.explain: Legitimate autostart entries are common, so malicious ones hide in the noise.

hints: List scheduled tasks and run keys across hosts and group by the launched binary.
Watch for entries that call the same tool hash on multiple machines.
The persistence tool hash is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: After one host was cleaned, the attacker's scheduled tasks and run keys persisted on additional hosts launching the same tool.
containment: Remove malicious scheduled tasks and run keys on all affected hosts
Verify each host is clean before returning it to service

scenarios.debrief: Cleaning one host is not eradication. Sweep the fleet for shared autostart entries that point at the same tooling.

scenarios.title: Personal Cloud Exfil Hunt

briefing.subject: Hunt for PHI leaving to personal cloud
briefing.body: HR opened a quiet review on a departing billing analyst. We have no DLP alert, but we worry PHI is trickling out to a personal cloud account. Hunt for covert uploads and any DNS tunneling used to hide them. Assume the insider is still active and careful.
objectives: Hypothesize covert PHI exfil to a personal cloud
Find uploads to non-corporate cloud storage
Detect DNS tunneling used to mask the transfer

steps.prompt: What hunting hypothesis fits a departing insider and no DLP alert?
options: PHI is being uploaded to personal cloud and hidden with DNS tunneling
The analyst is on vacation
The billing app is slow
steps.rationale: Careful insiders use personal cloud and covert channels that skip standard DLP rules.

steps.prompt: Best first containment for confirmed exfil?
options: Suspend the insider account and block the personal cloud domain
Wait until the analyst's last day
Email the whole company a reminder
steps.rationale: Cut the account and the destination to stop the PHI from leaving.

questions.prompt: What signals covert exfil here?
options: Uploads to a non-corporate cloud domain paired with odd DNS traffic
A normal email to a coworker
A scheduled backup job
questions.explain: Personal cloud uploads plus DNS tunneling together point to deliberate covert exfil.

questions.prompt: Why use DNS tunneling alongside the upload?
options: To hide data in DNS queries that often go unmonitored
To speed up the corporate VPN
To reset the user's password
questions.explain: DNS is frequently allowed out and lightly inspected, making it a stealthy channel.

hints: Look for uploads to cloud domains outside your corporate tenant.
Check for unusually long or frequent DNS queries to one domain.
The personal cloud domain is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A departing insider uploaded PHI to a personal cloud account and used DNS tunneling to mask the transfer.
containment: Tighten DLP and DNS monitoring for departing staff

scenarios.debrief: Careful insiders avoid loud DLP rules. Hunt for personal cloud uploads and covert DNS channels around departures.

scenarios.title: Web Beacon Staging Hunt

briefing.subject: Hunt for a web-protocol beacon and staging
briefing.body: Threat intel warns of an actor that beacons over normal web ports and stages data before exfil. No alert has fired here, but the pattern would blend into our clinical web traffic. Hunt for HTTP and HTTPS beaconing and any host quietly collecting files into one place. Assume the actor is already inside.
objectives: Hypothesize a web-protocol beacon hiding in clinical traffic
Find the beacon to one external host
Locate the staging directory before exfil

steps.prompt: What hunting hypothesis fits beaconing that blends into web traffic?
options: An actor uses HTTP and HTTPS to beacon and stages files before exfil
A clinician left a browser tab open
The web proxy needs a restart
steps.rationale: Web-protocol C2 hides in normal traffic, so hunt for steady callbacks plus a staging spot.

steps.prompt: Best first containment once both are found?
options: Block the beacon destination and isolate the staging host
Reboot the web proxy and wait
Delete the clinical web app
steps.rationale: Cut the C2 and isolate the host holding the staged data before it leaves.

questions.prompt: How do you spot a web-protocol beacon in noisy traffic?
options: Steady, regular callbacks to one external host on web ports
A one-time large download
A burst of failed logons
questions.explain: Regular timing to a single destination separates a beacon from normal browsing.

questions.prompt: What does the staging directory indicate?
options: The actor is collecting files in one place to prepare for exfil
The host is running a backup
A user is sorting photos
questions.explain: A growing collection point is the prep step right before data leaves.

hints: Group web traffic by destination and look for fixed-interval callbacks.
Check the beaconing host for a directory that keeps gathering files.
The beacon destination IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An actor beaconed over web protocols to C2 and staged collected files on a host in preparation for exfiltration.
containment: Hunt other hosts for the same callback timing

scenarios.debrief: Web-protocol C2 hides in normal traffic. Hunt for regular callbacks and a staging point that signals exfil is coming.

scenarios.title: Injected Doc Egress

briefing.subject: Assistant summarized a doc then phoned home
briefing.body: A clinician asked the BVHN intake assistant to summarize a referral PDF that arrived from outside. Right after the summary, the assistant made an outbound web call to an unknown domain. We think hidden text in the PDF told the assistant to send patient data out. Confirm the prompt injection and the egress.
objectives: Confirm the injected instruction in the document
Trace the outbound data egress
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Domain encoded in the injected PDF instruction
Endpoint that received the assistant's outbound call

steps.prompt: Why did the assistant send data outward after reading the PDF?
options: Hidden text in the document was treated as a trusted instruction
The clinician typed an export command
The SIEM mislabeled a normal request
steps.rationale: Indirect prompt injection hides commands inside content the model later processes as instructions.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block the egress domain and disable the assistant's outbound tool until input is sanitized
Reset the clinician's password
Reimage the clinician's laptop
steps.rationale: Cut the data path and stop the tool that acted on untrusted content.

questions.prompt: What is the trust-boundary failure here?
options: Untrusted document content was given the same trust as the user's own instructions
The PDF was infected with malware
The clinician had too many permissions
questions.explain: The assistant did not separate data to be summarized from commands to obey.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Isolate retrieved content from instructions and gate outbound tools with allow-lists
Longer passwords for clinicians
Antivirus on the mail server
questions.explain: Keeping content and instructions separate plus egress allow-listing defeats indirect injection.

hints: Read the assistant trace for instruction-like text that came from the document, not the user.
The egress domain is encoded inside the injected instruction.
Compare the timing of the summary and the first outbound call.

answerKey.rootCause: An externally supplied PDF carried a hidden instruction that the assistant obeyed, causing PHI to be sent to an attacker domain.

scenarios.debrief: The lesson is the AI trust boundary. Content the model reads is data, not commands. Treat everything retrieved from outside as untrusted and never let it drive privileged tools.

scenarios.title: Shadow AI Leak

briefing.subject: PHI pasted into a public chatbot
briefing.body: DLP flagged a billing analyst pasting large blocks of patient records into a public consumer LLM website to draft appeal letters. This is shadow AI and a HIPAA exposure. Confirm what left the network, how much, and whether the gateway stopped it.
objectives: Confirm the PHI was sent to an unsanctioned LLM
Estimate the scope of exposed records
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Unsanctioned public LLM endpoint receiving pasted PHI

steps.prompt: What does the DLP and proxy data show?
options: A user pasted clearly identifiable PHI into a public LLM site
A normal upload to the sanctioned EHR
A malware beacon to a C2
steps.rationale: The pattern is bulk PHI in web form posts to a consumer AI domain, not malware.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block the public LLM domain at the proxy and notify privacy for breach review
Fire the analyst immediately
Shut down the billing system
steps.rationale: Stop further leakage and start the HIPAA assessment without overreacting to a non-malicious user.

questions.prompt: Why is this still a serious incident even without an attacker?
options: PHI left the organization's control into a third-party model with unknown retention
No data actually moved
The analyst used MFA
questions.explain: Once PHI enters an external model the org loses control of it, which is a reportable exposure.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Sanctioned in-tenant AI plus DLP that blocks PHI to unapproved AI domains
Telling staff to be careful
Disabling all internet access
questions.explain: Give a safe approved tool and enforce DLP so the easy path is also the compliant path.

hints: Check the DLP match content, not just the alert count.
Confirm whether the gateway blocked or only logged each paste.
The destination is a consumer AI domain, not your tenant.

answerKey.rootCause: A billing analyst used an unsanctioned public LLM and pasted PHI into it, sending protected data outside the network.

scenarios.debrief: The lesson is that the AI trust boundary includes where your data goes. A public LLM is outside your control, so sanctioned tooling and DLP must define which AI services may receive PHI.

scenarios.title: Model Scraping API

briefing.subject: Odd volume on the AI inference API
briefing.body: BVHN's clinical triage model is exposed through an internal inference API. Telemetry shows one service token sending huge volumes of crafted queries that systematically probe the model's behavior. This looks like model theft by extraction. Confirm the scraping and scope the risk.
objectives: Confirm the extraction query pattern
Identify the abused token and source
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Service token abused for high-volume scraping

steps.prompt: What distinguishes extraction from normal model use?
options: Massive systematic queries that map the model's decision boundary, far above clinical need
A single triage request
A failed login
steps.rationale: Extraction sends crafted high-volume queries to reconstruct the model, not to get one answer.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Revoke the abused token, rate-limit the API, and block the source
Take the model offline for all clinicians
Retrain the model from scratch
steps.rationale: Cut the abusing identity and throttle the API while keeping clinical service available.

questions.prompt: What is the asset at risk?
options: The model's learned behavior and the data it encodes can be cloned
Only the API server's CPU
Nothing of value
questions.explain: Extraction can reproduce a proprietary model and may leak patterns from training data.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Per-token rate limits, anomaly detection on query volume, and output throttling
A longer model name
Disabling logging
questions.explain: Limiting query volume and watching for systematic probing defeats extraction attacks.

hints: Compare query volume per token against a normal clinician baseline.
Look for inputs that sweep the input space rather than asking real questions.
The abused identity is a service token, not a person.

answerKey.rootCause: A service token was abused to send high-volume crafted queries to the inference API to extract and clone the clinical model.

scenarios.debrief: The lesson is that an exposed model is itself an asset behind a trust boundary. Treat inference endpoints like sensitive data stores with rate limits, authentication, and volume anomaly detection.

scenarios.title: Poisoned RAG Index

briefing.subject: Clinical assistant giving wrong guidance
briefing.body: Clinicians report the BVHN knowledge assistant is recommending an incorrect medication dose. The assistant pulls from a RAG index that ingests internal documents. We suspect someone planted a tampered document so retrieval steers answers. Confirm the poisoning and find the source document.
objectives: Confirm the poisoned source document in the index
Identify who or what injected it
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Tampered clinical document seeded into the RAG index
Account that uploaded the poisoned document

steps.prompt: Why is the assistant giving the wrong dose?
options: Retrieval is surfacing a planted document that overrides correct guidance
The model weights were stolen
The clinician misread the screen
steps.rationale: RAG poisoning seeds a document the retriever ranks highly, steering generated answers.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Remove the poisoned document, reindex, and freeze new ingestion pending review
Delete the entire knowledge base
Ignore it until the next release
steps.rationale: Pull the bad source and rebuild the index, then control what may be ingested.

questions.prompt: What is the trust-boundary failure?
options: Unvetted documents were trusted as authoritative clinical sources
The network firewall failed
The assistant ran out of memory
questions.explain: The index treated an unverified upload as trusted ground truth for answers.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Provenance checks and approval workflow before documents enter the index
Faster GPUs
Disabling the assistant for everyone
questions.explain: Gating ingestion with provenance and review stops poisoned content from being retrieved.

hints: Find the source document the assistant cited for the wrong dose.
Check who uploaded that document and when.
Reindexing without removing the source will not fix it.

answerKey.rootCause: A tampered clinical document was ingested into the RAG index and retrieval steered the assistant to give wrong clinical guidance.

scenarios.debrief: The lesson is that a RAG index is a trust boundary. What you ingest becomes what the model treats as truth, so document provenance and review must gate the index.

scenarios.title: Deepfake CEO Wire

briefing.subject: Finance got a CEO voice call to wire funds
briefing.body: A BVHN finance officer received a live phone call that sounded exactly like the CEO, urgently authorizing a large vendor wire transfer. The voice was likely an AI deepfake. A wire was initiated before anyone verified. Confirm the deepfake, trace the wire, and stop the loss.
objectives: Confirm the call was a synthetic deepfake
Trace the fraudulent wire request
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Finance officer targeted by the deepfake call
Spoofed vendor domain on the follow-up wire instruction

steps.prompt: What is the core deception here?
options: A synthetic voice impersonated the CEO to authorize a wire
A real CEO approved the wire
A server was encrypted by ransomware
steps.rationale: Deepfake voice fraud impersonates a trusted executive to rush a payment.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Recall or freeze the wire with the bank and verify via a known-good channel
Wire a second payment to confirm
Wait for the CEO to call back
steps.rationale: Stop the money movement first, then verify through an out-of-band trusted channel.

questions.prompt: Why did urgency matter to the attacker?
options: Pressure discouraged out-of-band verification of the request
Banks move faster at night
Urgency reduces wire fees
questions.explain: Urgency is social engineering designed to bypass the verify-before-pay step.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Mandatory call-back verification and dual approval for wires regardless of who asks
Trusting any executive voice
Faster wire processing
questions.explain: Out-of-band verification and dual control defeat voice impersonation.

hints: A voice alone is not proof of identity.
Look for a spoofed vendor domain on the written follow-up.
The most urgent action is contacting the bank to freeze the wire.

answerKey.rootCause: An AI-generated deepfake voice impersonated the CEO and pressured finance into initiating a fraudulent wire transfer.

scenarios.debrief: The lesson is that identity cannot be inferred from voice or face anymore. The trust boundary is verified identity, so high-risk actions need out-of-band confirmation no matter how convincing the request sounds.

scenarios.title: AI Phishing Wave

briefing.subject: Polished phishing wave harvesting logins
briefing.body: BVHN staff are receiving unusually flawless phishing emails, likely AI-generated, with no spelling tells. The links lead to a fake login that proxies our real portal and steals session tokens. Some users already clicked. Confirm the credential harvest and the token theft.
objectives: Confirm the AI-generated phishing wave
Identify the credential harvest and token theft
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: AI-generated lure linking to an adversary-in-the-middle portal
Stolen session token replayed after harvest

steps.prompt: Why is this wave harder to spot than usual phishing?
options: AI removed the grammar and spelling tells users rely on
The emails are obviously fake
The links go to a known good site
steps.rationale: AI-generated lures are fluent and tailored, defeating the classic typo heuristic.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block the phishing URL, revoke stolen sessions, and force re-auth for clicked users
Only delete the emails
Wait to see who logs in
steps.rationale: Cut the lure and kill stolen sessions, because AiTM steals tokens not just passwords.

questions.prompt: Why does resetting only passwords fall short here?
options: The attacker captured live session tokens that bypass the password
Passwords never matter
The portal is offline
questions.explain: AiTM proxies the real login and steals the post-MFA token, so sessions must be revoked too.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Phishing-resistant MFA such as FIDO2 plus token binding
A spam filter alone
Telling users to look for typos
questions.explain: Phishing-resistant MFA and token binding defeat AiTM token theft.

hints: The fluency of the emails is the AI tell, not a typo.
Trace clicks to the AiTM portal and then the token replay.
Revoke sessions, do not just reset passwords.

answerKey.rootCause: An AI-generated phishing wave lured staff to an adversary-in-the-middle portal that harvested credentials and stole session tokens.
containment: Force re-auth and roll out FIDO2

scenarios.debrief: The lesson is that AI erases the old phishing tells, so the trust boundary shifts to strong authentication. Assume the lure is convincing and rely on phishing-resistant MFA and session revocation, not user spotting.

scenarios.title: Agent Tool Abuse

briefing.subject: AI assistant pulled records it should not have
briefing.body: BVHN's agentic scheduling assistant can query the EHR and send email on a clinician's behalf. A crafted message in its task queue made the agent chain its own tools to pull patient records and email them outward. We think an injected instruction abused the agent's tools. Confirm the tool abuse and the exfil.
objectives: Confirm the injected instruction in the agent task
Trace the tool-chained record exfiltration
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Destination the agent emailed records to
Agent service identity that queried the EHR and sent mail

steps.prompt: How did records leave through the assistant?
options: An injected task made the agent chain its EHR-read and email tools to exfiltrate
A clinician manually emailed the records
A backup job ran normally
steps.rationale: The agent followed an injected instruction and combined legitimate tools for a malicious goal.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Suspend the agent, revoke its tokens, and require approval for outbound email and bulk reads
Email the attacker to stop
Reboot the EHR server
steps.rationale: Disable the over-privileged agent and gate its high-risk tools behind human approval.

questions.prompt: What made this so damaging?
options: The agent had standing access to both read records and send email without approval
The agent had no tools at all
Email was disabled
questions.explain: Broad standing tool access let one injected instruction become full exfiltration.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: Least-privilege scopes, human-in-the-loop for sensitive tools, and egress allow-lists
Giving the agent more tools
Disabling logging on the agent
questions.explain: Constrain what the agent can do and require approval for risky actions.

hints: Look for an instruction in the task queue that did not come from the clinician.
Follow the agent's tool calls from EHR read to outbound email.
The agent's own identity is the actor in the EHR logs.

answerKey.rootCause: An injected task instruction caused the agentic assistant to chain its EHR-read and email tools to exfiltrate patient records.

scenarios.debrief: The lesson is that an agent's tools define its blast radius. The trust boundary is least privilege and human approval, so an agent should never hold standing access to both read sensitive data and send it outward.

scenarios.title: Safeguard Bypass Exfil

briefing.subject: Assistant ignored its PHI guardrail
briefing.body: BVHN's clinical assistant normally refuses to output full patient identifiers. A crafted prompt convinced it to ignore that safeguard, then it dumped PHI into a shared export. The guardrail was disabled by the prompt itself, not by an admin. Confirm the bypass and the data leak.
objectives: Confirm the prompt that disabled the safeguard
Trace the resulting PHI leak
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Session that issued the jailbreak prompt
Shared export location where PHI landed

steps.prompt: How was the PHI guardrail defeated?
options: A crafted prompt told the model to ignore its safety rules, then extract PHI
An admin turned the guardrail off
The model crashed and reset
steps.rationale: A jailbreak-style prompt injection overrode the assistant's own safeguard from inside the conversation.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Disable the assistant, purge the exposed export, and enforce guardrails outside the prompt
Trust the model to behave next time
Delete the SIEM logs
steps.rationale: Stop the leak and move enforcement to a layer the prompt cannot override.

questions.prompt: Why is an in-prompt safeguard insufficient?
options: Anything the prompt can instruct, another prompt can override
Prompts are always safe
The model has no memory
questions.explain: A guardrail living in the prompt is in scope for injection and can be talked out of.

questions.prompt: Which control best prevents this?
options: External output filtering and policy enforcement the model cannot disable
A politer system prompt
Hoping users do not jailbreak
questions.explain: Enforcement outside the model survives prompt injection and jailbreaks.

hints: Find the prompt that told the model to ignore its rules.
Confirm the PHI output that followed the bypass.
Move guardrails to a layer the prompt cannot reach.

answerKey.rootCause: A crafted prompt disabled the assistant's PHI safeguard from within the conversation, then the assistant leaked patient data to a shared export.

scenarios.debrief: The lesson is that a safeguard living inside the prompt sits on the wrong side of the trust boundary. Security controls must be enforced in a layer the model and its inputs cannot modify.

scenarios.title: AiTM Token Theft Persists

briefing.subject: Billing director signed in from a new device after a phish link
briefing.body: A billing director clicked a link that mimicked our Microsoft 365 sign-in page. Soon after, a session token from her account appeared on an unknown device with no fresh MFA prompt. We think the attacker proxied the login and stole the live session. Confirm the token theft and check for any new cloud foothold.
objectives: Confirm the AiTM token theft
Trace any cloud persistence created with the stolen session
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Stolen M365 session token replayed from new device

steps.prompt: Why did the attacker bypass MFA here?
options: They stole the live session token after MFA already passed
They guessed the MFA code
They disabled MFA on the tenant
steps.rationale: An AiTM proxy captures the authenticated session cookie, so the attacker rides the session without re-prompting for MFA.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Revoke the user's sessions and reset credentials, then remove attacker persistence
Email the user a warning
Block the phishing domain only
steps.rationale: Revoking sessions kills the stolen token, and you must also undo any cloud persistence the attacker added.

questions.prompt: What did the attacker steal in this attack?
options: A valid authenticated session token
The user's password hash
An MFA hardware key
questions.explain: The AiTM proxy captured the session cookie after the user completed MFA on the fake page.

questions.prompt: What persistence did the attacker add with the stolen session?
options: A new cloud identity or credential for ongoing access
A scheduled task on a laptop
A new firewall rule
questions.explain: iam_persist shows the attacker created a cloud foothold so they keep access even after the token is revoked.

hints: Match the phish link click to the new-device sign-in with no fresh MFA.
A token replay from a new IP soon after login signals AiTM.
Check the cloud audit log for a new credential or identity added after the sign-in.

answerKey.rootCause: An adversary-in-the-middle phishing page proxied a billing director's M365 login, stole the live session token, and used it to add cloud persistence.
containment: Revoke all sessions for the account

scenarios.debrief: AiTM defeats classic MFA by stealing the session after the prompt. The lesson is to revoke tokens, not just reset passwords, and to hunt for new cloud footholds.

scenarios.title: Public Bucket Leaks PHI

briefing.subject: Storage bucket holding patient exports is world readable
briefing.body: Cloud posture scanning flagged an S3 bucket that stores nightly PHI export files as publicly readable. Anonymous internet addresses have already listed and pulled objects from it. We need to confirm the exposure, scope what was downloaded, and lock it down fast under HIPAA rules.
objectives: Confirm the bucket is publicly exposed
Scope which PHI objects were accessed
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: S3 bucket with PHI exports left public

steps.prompt: What confirms the bucket is actually exposed, not just misconfigured?
options: Anonymous GET and LIST requests succeeded from external IPs
The bucket has a long name
It stores PHI
steps.rationale: Successful unauthenticated reads in the cloud access log prove real exposure, not just a risky setting.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block public access on the bucket and rotate any exposed data paths
Delete the whole bucket immediately
Ignore it since data is encrypted at rest
steps.rationale: Remove public access right away to stop further reads, then assess scope. Encryption at rest does not stop authorized public reads.

questions.prompt: Who accessed the PHI export objects?
options: Unauthenticated external addresses
An internal admin only
No one accessed them
questions.explain: The access log shows anonymous external reads, which is a reportable PHI exposure.

questions.prompt: What is the correct fix for this root cause?
options: Enforce block-public-access and least-privilege bucket policy
Rename the bucket
Add a longer object prefix
questions.explain: Blocking public access and tightening the policy removes the exposure at its source.

hints: Look for anonymous GetObject and ListBucket entries in the cloud trail.
Count distinct objects pulled to scope the PHI exposure.
The bucket reference is flagged in the cloud findings.

answerKey.rootCause: An S3 bucket holding nightly PHI exports was left publicly readable and anonymous external clients listed and downloaded objects.
containment: Scope and report the PHI exposure

scenarios.debrief: A single public-access toggle can expose thousands of patient records. The skill is proving real anonymous reads and locking the bucket before scoping the breach.

scenarios.title: Foreign Root Login No MFA

briefing.subject: Cloud root account signed in from overseas with no MFA
briefing.body: Our cloud root account, which should almost never be used, just authenticated from a foreign country with no MFA challenge. Minutes later new access keys and an admin identity appeared. Confirm whether the root credentials are compromised and find any persistence the attacker left.
objectives: Confirm the foreign root login is malicious
Identify the cloud persistence created
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Root account login from foreign geo without MFA
New access key minted after login

steps.prompt: Why is a root login especially alarming here?
options: Root has full control and should rarely sign in, so a foreign no-MFA login is high risk
Root logins are routine and benign
Root cannot create new keys
steps.rationale: The root identity holds unrestricted power, so any unexpected use, especially foreign with no MFA, is treated as compromise.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Rotate root credentials, delete attacker keys, and enforce MFA on root
Email the cloud team a reminder
Lower the severity since data was not touched yet
steps.rationale: You must cut the attacker's access immediately by rotating root and removing the keys and identities they created.

questions.prompt: What did the attacker do after the root login?
options: Created new access keys and an admin identity for persistence
Logged out immediately
Only viewed a billing page
questions.explain: New keys and a rogue admin identity give the attacker durable access independent of the root login.

questions.prompt: Which control would have blocked this initial access?
options: Hardware MFA enforced on the root account
A longer root password
Email alerts only
questions.explain: Enforced MFA on root stops a stolen or guessed root credential from authenticating.

hints: The root account should almost never appear in sign-in logs.
Correlate the foreign login with new key creation in the cloud trail.
The attacker source IP is flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: The cloud root account had no MFA and was signed in from a foreign IP, then used to mint new access keys and a rogue admin identity.
containment: Delete attacker access keys and identities

scenarios.debrief: Root should be locked behind hardware MFA and almost never used. The lesson is to treat any root sign-in as an incident and hunt for the keys it created.

scenarios.title: Illicit OAuth Grant Steals Mail

briefing.subject: A staff member approved a sketchy app that now reads their inbox
briefing.body: A clinical staff member clicked a consent prompt and granted a third-party app broad mailbox permissions. The app immediately began reading mail, and a new inbox rule is auto-forwarding messages out. Confirm the illicit consent grant and stop the mail theft.
objectives: Confirm the illicit OAuth consent grant
Identify the mail exfiltration mechanism
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: User consented to malicious OAuth app

steps.prompt: How did the attacker gain mailbox access without a password?
options: The user granted OAuth consent that gave the app mailbox permissions
The attacker brute forced the mailbox
The mailbox was public
steps.rationale: Illicit consent grants give an app a token to read mail directly, no password needed.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Revoke the app's consent and delete the malicious inbox rule
Reset the user's password only
Block the user's laptop from the network
steps.rationale: Revoking the OAuth grant kills the token, and the auto-forward rule must be deleted to stop mail theft. A password reset alone does not revoke app tokens.

questions.prompt: What gave the malicious app access to the mailbox?
options: An OAuth consent grant approved by the user
A stolen password
An open mail relay
questions.explain: The consent grant issued an access token with mailbox scopes, which is why no password was involved.

questions.prompt: How was mail being exfiltrated?
options: A new inbox rule auto-forwarding messages to an external domain
A printed copy
A shared calendar
questions.explain: The auto-forward inbox rule quietly sends copies of mail to an attacker domain.

hints: Look for a consent grant event tied to a new third-party app.
Check mailbox rules for an auto-forward to an external domain.
A password reset will not revoke an OAuth token, so look at app grants.

answerKey.rootCause: A user granted illicit OAuth consent to a malicious app, which read the mailbox and created an auto-forward rule to exfiltrate mail.
containment: Revoke the malicious app consent grant
Review and restrict user consent permissions

scenarios.debrief: Consent phishing skips passwords by tricking users into granting app access. The skill is revoking the grant and clearing inbox rules, not just resetting a password.

scenarios.title: Cloud Backup Ransom

briefing.subject: Backups deleted and a ransom note left in cloud storage
briefing.body: A leaked cloud access key was used to reach our backup storage. The attacker copied data out, deleted snapshots and backup objects, and dropped a ransom note demanding payment. Confirm the cloud ransom event, find the leaked key, and recommend recovery.
objectives: Confirm the cloud ransom on backups
Identify the leaked key used
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Leaked access key used to reach backups

steps.prompt: What makes this a cloud ransom rather than ordinary data loss?
options: The attacker deleted backups, exfiltrated data, and left a ransom note
A disk simply failed
A user emptied a recycle bin
steps.rationale: Deliberate deletion of backups with a ransom note and prior data theft is a double-extortion cloud ransom pattern.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Disable the leaked access key and restore from immutable or off-cloud backups
Pay the ransom
Create a new bucket and move on
steps.rationale: Kill the leaked credential first, then recover from backups that the attacker could not reach, such as immutable or offline copies.

questions.prompt: How did the attacker reach the backup storage?
options: With a leaked cloud access key
Through a phishing email
Via physical access to a server
questions.explain: The cloud trail shows the leaked access key authenticating and acting on the backup objects.

questions.prompt: What control best enables recovery without paying?
options: Immutable or object-lock backups the attacker cannot delete
More frequent backups in the same bucket
A longer ransom negotiation
questions.explain: Immutable backups survive deletion attempts, so recovery does not depend on the attacker.

hints: Trace the access key that issued the delete and copy operations.
A ransom note object in the bucket confirms the impact stage.
Check whether any backups were immutable and survived.

answerKey.rootCause: A leaked cloud access key was used to exfiltrate backup data, delete snapshots and backup objects, and leave a ransom note.
containment: Restore from immutable or off-cloud backups
Rotate all keys and audit key exposure

scenarios.debrief: Cloud ransom targets backups so victims cannot recover. The lesson is immutable backups and tight key hygiene so a single leaked key cannot wipe recovery options.

scenarios.title: Tor Password Spray Cloud

briefing.subject: Cloud sign-in failures spiking from Tor exit nodes
briefing.body: Our cloud identity provider is logging a wave of failed sign-ins across many staff accounts, all from rotating Tor exit nodes. The low-and-slow pattern looks like a password spray hiding behind anonymity. Confirm the spray, check for any success, and decide on containment.
objectives: Confirm the password spray from Tor
Determine if any account was compromised
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Tor exit node sourcing the spray
Sprayed account that later signed in

steps.prompt: What two signals together define this attack?
options: One password tried across many accounts plus sources that are Tor exit nodes
Many passwords against one account from one IP
A single failed sign-in
steps.rationale: Spraying spreads one guess across many users, and Tor sourcing shows the attacker is hiding the true origin.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Block Tor sign-ins, enforce MFA, and reset any account that authenticated
Disable the cloud tenant
Email all staff to change passwords next month
steps.rationale: Block anonymized logins and MFA defeats the spray, then reset only accounts that actually signed in.

questions.prompt: How is the attacker hiding their origin?
options: By routing sign-ins through rotating Tor exit nodes
By using a corporate VPN
By signing in during business hours
questions.explain: Tor exit nodes rotate the source IP so simple IP blocks miss the attacker.

questions.prompt: Did any account succeed?
options: Yes, a later sign-in from the spray pattern succeeded
No, every attempt failed
The logs do not show sign-ins
questions.explain: A success after the spray, including the foreign follow-on login, confirms one account fell.

hints: Group the failures by account and note the Tor-tagged source IPs.
Look for a single success that follows the spray.
Tor exit nodes are flagged as anonymized sources in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: An attacker password-sprayed the cloud identity provider through Tor exit nodes and one account without MFA eventually authenticated.

scenarios.debrief: Tor hides the source so IP blocking alone fails. The skill is spotting the spray pattern, blocking anonymized logins, and enforcing MFA.

scenarios.title: Poisoned AI Dependency

briefing.subject: Our diagnostic AI pulled a tampered model from a poisoned package
briefing.body: A build of our clinical decision-support service installed a popular AI package that was compromised upstream. The package pulled a poisoned model artifact that now skews predictions and may beacon out. Confirm the supply-chain compromise and the model poisoning, then recommend containment.
objectives: Confirm the supply-chain compromise
Identify the poisoned AI model artifact
Recommend a containment action

iocs.note: Beacon domain from the model loader

steps.prompt: Where did the compromise enter the environment?
options: Through a tampered upstream AI package pulled during the build
Through a phishing email to a clinician
Through a USB device
steps.rationale: The build installed a compromised dependency, which is a software supply-chain attack on the AI pipeline.

steps.prompt: Best first containment action?
options: Pin to a known-good package and model, rebuild, and block the beacon domain
Restart the AI service only
Lower the model confidence threshold
steps.rationale: You must remove the poisoned dependency and model, rebuild from trusted artifacts, and cut the C2 channel.

questions.prompt: What is the root cause of this incident?
options: A compromised upstream AI package delivered a poisoned model
An insider edited the model by hand
A failed disk corrupted the weights
questions.explain: The poisoned model arrived through the tampered package, which is a supply-chain root cause.

questions.prompt: What evidence confirms model poisoning rather than a bug?
options: Mismatched artifact hashes and skewed outputs plus a beacon from the loader
Slower response times only
A higher cloud bill
questions.explain: Hash mismatch on the model, skewed predictions, and outbound beaconing together confirm deliberate poisoning.

hints: Compare the installed package and model hashes against known-good values.
A beacon from the model loader points to malicious code in the artifact.
The tampered package and model hashes are flagged in Threat Intel.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised upstream AI package was pulled during the build and delivered a poisoned model artifact that skewed predictions and beaconed out.
containment: Pin to known-good package and model versions
Block the beacon domain and rotate build credentials

scenarios.debrief: AI pipelines inherit the trust of every dependency. The lesson is to verify package and model integrity so a poisoned upstream artifact cannot reach production.

scenarios.title: Risk Assessment: Student Information System

briefing.subject: Annual risk assessment of the Banner SIS
briefing.body: Policy requires an annual security risk assessment of Banner, the Student Information System that holds records, registration, and grades for about 38,000 students. The April penetration test reached Banner data, and our cyber insurance renewal now depends on this assessment. Work from the materials below: scope the system and its data, pair each threat with the weakness it exploits, rate each risk by likelihood and impact, and recommend a treatment with an owner. Some risks should be mitigated now, and at least one is a reasonable candidate to accept and document. Be ready to defend your ratings with the evidence.
objectives: Scope the system, its data, and every regulation that applies
Pair threats with the specific vulnerabilities in the materials and rate each risk
Recommend a treatment for each risk and justify what to fund first

dossier.title: System and data profile
dossier.intro: What Banner holds, by volume and classification.

dossier.title: Current state and recent findings
bullets: MFA is enforced for IT staff, but only 41% of faculty and staff accounts and 0% of student accounts.
April penetration test: a phished faculty account reached Banner read access in under two hours. No exploit of a Banner software flaw was needed.
The last access recertification was 19 months ago. 230 active accounts belong to separated employees.
Banner integrates with six third-party systems using standing API tokens. None have been rotated in two years.
Vendor security patches are applied on a 90-day cycle. The vendor SLA recommends 30 days for critical patches.
Backups run nightly to an immutable cloud target and were last test-restored eight months ago.

dossier.title: Threat context
bullets: Three peer Texas community colleges reported ransomware via phished credentials in the last year.
The email gateway blocks roughly 1,200 phishing messages per week. Click-through on the last internal phishing simulation was 18%.

deliverables: In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Risk register (Word or Excel)
One row per risk from the materials: the threat-and-vulnerability pairing, likelihood, impact, risk level, recommended treatment (mitigate, accept, transfer, or avoid), and an owner.
Risk assessment report (Word document)
Scope, methodology, findings, and recommendations in your own words, following NIST SP 800-30. Justify each rating against the evidence in the materials.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: the top risks, the single initiative to fund first, and the residual risk you recommend accepting.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes

Required. On camera, present your top risks, how the materials drove your ratings, and your treatment recommendation to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Using the data profile, which scope statement is correct and complete?
options: FERPA is the only regulation in scope, since Banner is a student system.
The data is mostly public, so a light-touch review is enough.
Banner holds FERPA records, GLBA-covered aid data, and SSNs, so FERPA, GLBA, and state breach law all apply.
steps.rationale: The profile lists education records (FERPA), financial aid records (GLBA), and SSNs (state PII and breach law). All three regimes are in scope, not FERPA alone.

steps.prompt: The pen test reached Banner read access in under two hours. Which threat-and-vulnerability pairing best explains the highest-likelihood path to a breach?
options: An unpatched Banner software flaw exploited directly from the internet.
Credential phishing against faculty, exploited because most staff and all student accounts lack MFA.
A malicious registrar insider abusing legitimate access.
steps.rationale: The materials say the test used a phished account and needed no software exploit, and that MFA coverage is weak. The phishing-plus-no-MFA pairing is the evidenced path. The other two are possible but unsupported by the materials.

steps.prompt: How should you rate the likelihood of account takeover leading to a records breach?
options: High: an 18% phishing click rate, weak MFA coverage, and a pen test that already proved the path.
Low: the gateway blocks about 1,200 phishing emails every week.
Medium: it is theoretically possible but has never been demonstrated here.
steps.rationale: Likelihood is high and evidenced. Blocking some phishing does not cover the 18% that get clicked, and the path is not theoretical, the pen test demonstrated it.

steps.prompt: Leadership will fund one initiative this quarter. Which treatment most reduces the highest-rated risk per dollar?
options: Shorten the patch cycle from 90 days to the vendor-recommended 30.
Rotate the six standing integration API tokens.
Enforce MFA across all faculty, staff, and student accounts.
steps.rationale: Both other actions are worthwhile hygiene, but the evidenced highest-likelihood path is phishing into accounts without MFA. Enforcing MFA directly breaks that path and is low cost since licensing already exists.

steps.prompt: Which item is the most defensible candidate to record as an accepted risk rather than mitigate immediately?
options: The 230 active accounts belonging to separated employees.
The 90-day patch cycle, accepted with a documented review date once MFA and access cleanup are done.
The absence of MFA on student accounts.
steps.rationale: Orphaned accounts and missing MFA are high risk and must be fixed. The patch cycle is a lower residual risk once the top paths are closed, so accepting it with a documented review date is a defensible, owned decision.

questions.prompt: Based on the data profile, which regulations are in scope for this assessment?
options: FERPA only.
FERPA and PCI-DSS.
FERPA, GLBA, and state breach law.
None, the SIS is internal.
questions.explain: Education records bring FERPA, financial aid records bring GLBA, and SSNs bring state PII and breach law. There is no card data, so PCI does not apply.

questions.prompt: From the materials, select the two findings that most increase the likelihood of a records breach.
options: Nightly immutable backups.
An 18% phishing simulation click rate.
MFA missing on most staff and all student accounts.
A public course catalog.
questions.explain: The click rate and the MFA gap are the two factors behind the proven account-takeover path. Backups affect recovery, not likelihood, and the public catalog is not sensitive.

questions.prompt: What does the pen test reaching Banner in under two hours most directly establish for your rating?
options: That the backups are inadequate.
That the account-takeover path is demonstrated, not theoretical, supporting a high likelihood.
That the data is misclassified.
questions.explain: A demonstrated path raises likelihood from speculative to evidenced. It says nothing about backups or classification.

questions.prompt: The gateway blocks about 1,200 phishing emails a week. Why is that not a reason to rate the phishing risk as low?
options: Blocking volume says nothing about the messages that get through, and the 18% click rate plus the successful pen test show the residual risk is high.
It is a reason to rate it low, since most phishing is clearly being stopped.
Blocking only matters for spam, not for credential phishing.
questions.explain: A high block count is not a reliable control outcome. What matters is the residual: messages that land and get clicked, which the 18% rate and the pen test prove is a live path.

hints: Read the data profile row by row. Each regulated data type pulls in its own law.
The pen test finding tells you which threat path is real here, so weight it heavily.
Not every finding should be mitigated now. One is a reasonable, documented acceptance once the bigger risks are handled.

answerKey.rootCause: Banner holds FERPA, GLBA, and SSN data, and the evidenced top risk is account takeover by phishing into accounts without MFA, a path the April pen test proved in under two hours. Orphaned accounts and unrotated integration tokens compound it. Enforcing MFA is the highest-value treatment; the patch cycle is a defensible documented acceptance once the top paths are closed.
containment: Enforce MFA on all faculty, staff, and student accounts as the first-funded treatment
Disable the 230 separated-employee accounts and restart access recertification
Rotate the six integration API tokens and document a review date for the 90-day patch cycle

scenarios.debrief: A real risk assessment is driven by evidence, not adjectives. The materials point to one clearly highest risk, account takeover via phishing without MFA, proven by the pen test, and a clear best buy, MFA. A strong submission rates each risk from the facts, funds MFA first, and documents the patch cycle as an accepted residual risk rather than treating every finding as equally urgent.

scenarios.title: Business Impact Analysis: Learning Management System

briefing.subject: Business impact analysis of the Blackboard LMS
briefing.body: Blackboard runs every online course, assignment submission, and grade entry for about 38,000 students, and it is the only path students have to coursework when classes are remote. A storm-related outage last month took it down for six hours during a normal week and the help desk logged 2,400 tickets, so leadership now wants a real business impact analysis before finals. The materials below give you an impact-over-time table and our current recovery posture, including nightly backups, the date of our last disaster recovery test, and two single points of failure. Your job is to estimate how the impact grows with the length and timing of an outage, then set a defensible recovery time objective and recovery point objective. You must also identify which single point of failure to fix first and feed a recovery priority into the contingency plan. Work only from the materials below and be ready to defend every number you choose.
objectives: Estimate how outage impact grows over time and with the academic calendar
Set a recovery time objective and a recovery point objective from the materials
Map the dependencies and name the single point of failure to fix first

dossier.title: Impact of an LMS outage over time
dossier.intro: Estimated impact during finals week, the worst case window for this system.

dossier.title: Current recovery posture
bullets: Backups run nightly at 2 a.m. to an immutable cloud target. There are no intra-day backups, so a midday failure can lose up to 24 hours of submissions and grade entries.
The last full disaster recovery test was 14 months ago and it overran its window. Restore of the LMS database took 9 hours, longer than the 4-hour target leadership had assumed.
Single sign-on through the central identity provider is required to reach the LMS. If SSO is down, students cannot log in even when the LMS itself is healthy. SSO has no tested failover.
The LMS database runs on a single primary server with no hot standby. A hardware failure means a restore from backup, not a failover.
There is no published recovery time objective or recovery point objective for the LMS today. Recovery order during the last outage was decided on the fly.

dossier.title: Academic calendar pressure
paras: Impact is not constant. During the open registration window and during finals week, submission and grade traffic is roughly 5 times a normal day. The two highest-stakes dates this term are the registration opening and the final grade submission deadline. An outage of the same length costs far more during those windows than during a quiet week, which is why the table above is scoped to finals week as the worst case.

deliverables: BIA worksheet (Word or Excel)

One row per finding from the materials: impact over time, the recovery time objective and recovery point objective you set, the dependencies, the single points of failure, and the minimum resources needed to recover the LMS.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: the cost of downtime by duration, your recommended RTO and RPO, and the one single point of failure to fix first.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes

Required. On camera, present your impact findings, your recovery objectives, and the dependency you would fix first to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Using the materials, which scope statement is correct for this BIA?
options: Only the database server's disk capacity, since storage is the constraint.
Only the vendor relationship, since Blackboard is hosted software.
The LMS and the academic services it carries, coursework submission and grade entry for about 38,000 students.
steps.rationale: A BIA scopes the system and the business services that depend on it. The materials describe coursework and grades for 38,000 students, not just disk or the vendor contract.

steps.prompt: The impact table is scoped to finals week. Why does timing matter this much?
options: Because traffic during registration and finals is about 5 times a normal day, so the same outage length costs far more in those windows.
Because outages only ever happen during finals.
Because impact is constant, so the timing makes no difference.
steps.rationale: The calendar note says peak traffic is roughly 5 times normal, so impact is time-dependent. The other options contradict the materials.

steps.prompt: From the table, at what outage duration does impact first reach the High level during finals?
options: At 1 to 4 hours.
At 4 to 12 hours, when about 26,500 students are affected and cost reaches $140,000.
Only after 24 hours.
steps.rationale: The table marks the 4 to 12 hour row as High with 26,500 students and $140,000. The 1 to 4 hour row is Moderate, and over 24 hours is Critical, not the first High.

steps.prompt: Given that the last restore took 9 hours against an assumed 4-hour target, what is the most defensible RTO to set and commit to?
options: Keep assuming 4 hours, since that is what leadership expected.
Set no RTO, because the restore time is unpredictable.
Set a 4-hour RTO as the goal but fix the restore process and standby first, since the tested reality is 9 hours.
steps.rationale: An RTO you cannot meet is not defensible. The honest move is to target 4 hours and close the gap the test exposed. Keeping a number you fail, or setting none, both ignore the evidence.

steps.prompt: Two single points of failure are listed. Which should be fixed first to protect availability?
options: SSO, because if it is down students cannot log in even when the LMS is healthy, and it has no tested failover.
The nightly backup window, because 2 a.m. is inconvenient.
Neither, single points of failure are acceptable for a hosted system.
steps.rationale: SSO is a hard gate on access with no failover, so it can block everyone independent of LMS health. The backup timing is a separate RPO issue, and ignoring single points of failure is not defensible.

steps.prompt: Where do the BIA results go next?
options: Into a one-time slide deck, then archived and forgotten.
Into the disaster recovery and contingency plan, to set recovery priority and a tested RTO and RPO.
Nowhere, since the LMS is vendor-hosted.
steps.rationale: The BIA feeds the contingency plan so systems recover in impact order with committed objectives. The materials note recovery order was decided on the fly last time, which is what this fixes.

questions.prompt: Backups run only nightly at 2 a.m. with no intra-day backup. What recovery point objective does the current posture actually deliver?
options: One hour, since the system is critical.
Zero, because immutable backups never lose data.
Up to 24 hours of data loss, because a midday failure loses everything since the last nightly run.
questions.explain: RPO is set by backup frequency. With only a nightly backup, a midday failure can lose up to 24 hours of submissions and grades. Criticality does not change what the backups actually capture.

questions.prompt: From the materials, select the two single points of failure that can block recovery of the LMS.
options: Single sign-on with no tested failover.
The single primary database server with no hot standby.
The immutable nightly backup target.
The published RTO and RPO.
questions.explain: SSO and the single database server are the named single points of failure. The immutable backup aids recovery, and there is no published RTO or RPO yet, so neither is a point of failure.

questions.prompt: The last disaster recovery test took 9 hours to restore the database. What does this most directly tell you about the assumed 4-hour RTO?
options: The assumed RTO is not currently achievable and the restore process must be improved.
The backups are corrupt.
The RPO is too aggressive.
questions.explain: A tested 9-hour restore against a 4-hour assumption proves the RTO is not met today. It says nothing about backup integrity, and it is an RTO finding, not an RPO one.

questions.prompt: Using the impact table, which outage scenario should drive the recovery priority you set for the LMS?
options: A 30-minute outage at 3 a.m. on a holiday.
An outage exceeding 24 hours during finals, which puts final grades and $600,000-plus at risk.
Any outage, since all outages have identical impact.
questions.explain: Recovery priority is driven by the worst credible case. The table shows that a finals-week outage past 24 hours threatens final grades and the highest cost. A holiday micro-outage is the low end, and impact is clearly not constant.

hints: Read the impact table row by row. Note where the impact level first crosses into High and what is at stake past 24 hours.
RTO and RPO are different. RTO comes from the tested restore time, RPO comes from how often the backups run.
A single point of failure like SSO can block recovery even when the LMS server itself is fine.

answerKey.rootCause: The LMS carries coursework and grades for 38,000 students with impact that rises sharply during finals, reaching Severe past 12 hours and Critical past 24. The current posture cannot meet the assumed 4-hour RTO because the tested restore took 9 hours, and nightly-only backups leave an RPO of up to 24 hours. SSO with no failover and a single database server with no standby are the two single points of failure, with SSO the more urgent because it gates all access.
containment: Set and publish an LMS RTO of 4 hours and an RPO of 1 hour, then fix the restore process and add intra-day backups to make them real
Add tested failover for SSO first, then a hot standby for the LMS database, to remove the two single points of failure
Feed the impact ranking into the disaster recovery plan and schedule a new DR test before finals

scenarios.debrief: A BIA turns a vague fear of downtime into numbers leadership can plan around: how bad by hour, how fast to recover, and how much data loss is acceptable. The honest finding here is that the assumed 4-hour RTO is not met today and a nightly-only backup gives a 24-hour RPO. A strong submission commits to objectives, fixes SSO failover first, and feeds the impact ranking into the contingency plan instead of deciding recovery order on the fly.

scenarios.title: Vendor Risk Assessment: New Tutoring Platform

briefing.subject: Security review of StudyBridge, a new tutoring SaaS
briefing.body: Academic affairs wants to onboard StudyBridge, an online tutoring platform that would hold student names, course enrollment, and tutor session notes for about 6,500 students. Before we sign, we need a vendor risk assessment using the HECVAT questionnaire backed by the vendor's SOC 2 report, because student records make this a FERPA matter. The materials below give you the vendor's HECVAT responses with the evidence they provided and the gaps, plus a mix of controls that pass and controls that fail. Two facts stand out: the vendor does not encrypt student data at rest, and their standard contract promises breach notification within 30 days, which is far too slow. Your job is to weigh the gaps, decide approve, approve with conditions, or reject, and write the conditions the vendor must meet before go-live. Work only from the materials below and defend your recommendation with the evidence.
objectives: Identify the regulated data StudyBridge would hold and the law that applies
Assess the vendor with the HECVAT responses and the SOC 2 evidence
Recommend approve, approve with conditions, or reject, and write the conditions

dossier.title: Data the vendor would hold
paras: StudyBridge would store student names, course enrollment, and tutor session notes for roughly 6,500 students. Enrollment and session notes tied to named students are education records, so FERPA applies and StudyBridge must act as a school official under our direct control. No financial aid data and no card data are involved, so GLBA and PCI are out of scope here.

dossier.title: HECVAT response summary
dossier.intro: The vendor's answers, the evidence they gave, and the gap. A mix of pass and fail.

rows: Current SOC 2 Type II report, clean opinion
High: student data stored unencrypted at rest
High: 30 days is far too slow, should be 72 hours or less
Medium: no proof subprocessors are bound to equal protection
Data return and deletion on exit
Medium: no guarantee data is returned or destroyed at contract end

dossier.title: SOC 2 and reference notes
bullets: The SOC 2 Type II report is current and covers a 12-month period with a clean opinion, which is strong independent evidence that the audited controls actually operated.
The SOC 2 scope does not include encryption at rest, so the clean opinion does not cover the encryption gap. Read the scope, not just the opinion.
Two reference customers, both regional universities, reported no security incidents in two years of use.
The vendor agreed verbally to fix gaps but has put nothing in writing yet.

deliverables: Completed HECVAT review (Word or Excel)
Summarize the vendor's answers from the table, flag each gap with its severity, and rate the residual risk if the conditions are met.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership with your recommendation, the two High gaps, and the conditions the vendor must meet before signing.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes

Required. On camera, present the vendor's risk profile and your approve-with-conditions recommendation to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: From the materials, what is in scope for this assessment and why?
options: GLBA, because all vendors that touch student systems are covered.
FERPA, because StudyBridge holds enrollment and session notes tied to named students, which are education records.
PCI-DSS, because money changes hands when the college pays the vendor.
steps.rationale: The data note says enrollment and session notes are education records, so FERPA applies and the vendor must act as a school official. No aid data means no GLBA, and no card data means no PCI.

steps.prompt: The vendor's SOC 2 Type II has a clean opinion. How should you treat it given the encryption gap?
options: Treat it as strong evidence but check the scope, since the report does not cover encryption at rest.
Treat the clean opinion as proof that every control, including encryption, is fine.
