Nothing, as long as the password is strong.
steps.rationale: Shared accounts break individual accountability and the audit trail. A strong password does not fix attribution, and saving licensing is not a security justification.

steps.prompt: A records clerk holds the full Banner Administrator role but only needs read and update on application records. How should you classify and treat this?
options: Appropriate, since admin access is simpler to manage.
Over-privileged: reduce her access to the read and update rights her duties require.
A reward for reliability that should stay in place.
steps.rationale: Access far beyond duties is over-privilege. The fix is to reduce it to the role her job needs, not to leave admin in place for simplicity or as a reward.

steps.prompt: What control most reliably prevents access from creeping back after this cleanup?
options: A one-time cleanup and no further action.
Hoping managers report changes voluntarily.
Periodic manager-led recertification on a fixed cadence, with HR-triggered disablement.
steps.rationale: Recurring recertification plus automated disablement on separation keeps access aligned to duties over time. A one-time cleanup decays, and voluntary reporting is not a control.

steps.prompt: What is the single most urgent action to put first in the leadership brief?
options: Disable the 230 separated-employee accounts and the two ex-contractor domain admins immediately.
Rewrite the paper role definitions before touching any account.
Convert the 12 shared logins to named accounts next quarter.
steps.rationale: Standing access for people who have left, especially domain admin, is the highest and most immediate risk. Role rewrites and shared-account conversion matter but do not stop an unmonitored privileged account today.

questions.prompt: Per the findings table, how many active accounts belong to separated employees?
questions.explain: The findings table lists 230 orphaned accounts of separated employees. 47 are over-privileged, 186 are dormant, and 12 are shared logins.

questions.prompt: Select the two findings that most violate least privilege and demand reduction, not just disablement.
options: A records clerk holding full Banner Administrator rights.
Nightly backups of the access export.
Two ex-contractors retaining domain administrator membership.
A public list of office hours.
questions.explain: The over-privileged clerk and the standing domain-admin contractors are excessive privilege that must be reduced or removed. Backups and office hours are not access findings.

questions.prompt: Why is the generic advise1 account a governance problem beyond password strength?
options: It breaks individual accountability, so no action can be traced to one advisor.
It uses too much disk space.
It is slower to log in with.
questions.explain: Shared logins destroy attribution and the audit trail regardless of password strength.

questions.prompt: What single control best prevents access creep from returning over time?
options: A one-time spreadsheet cleanup.
Buying more licenses.
Recurring manager recertification plus HR-triggered disablement.
questions.explain: Recertification on a cadence, paired with automated disablement on separation, keeps access tied to duties as people and roles change.

hints: Read the findings table by count. The biggest standing-access risk is the largest unmonitored group.
Tie every grant back to a real job duty. If the duty is narrow, the access should be too.
A one-time cleanup decays. Recertification and HR-triggered disablement keep it clean.

answerKey.rootCause: Years of unpruned access left 230 orphaned accounts, 47 over-privileged accounts, shared logins, and dormant access in Banner, with no recertification in 19 months and no HR-triggered disablement. The fix disables separated-employee and ex-contractor access first, reduces over-privileged roles to job duties, replaces shared logins with named accounts, and establishes recurring manager recertification.
containment: Disable the 230 separated-employee accounts and the two ex-contractor domain admins immediately
Reduce over-privileged accounts to their job duties and replace shared logins with named accounts
Establish annual manager recertification and automated disablement on HR separation

scenarios.debrief: Access governance is unglamorous and high value. Most breaches get worse because a compromised account held far more access than the job needed. The materials point to one clearly highest risk, standing privileged access for people who have left, and a durable fix, least privilege backed by recertification and HR-triggered disablement.

scenarios.title: Cloud Security Posture Review: Microsoft 365

briefing.subject: Posture review of the college Microsoft 365 tenant
briefing.body: The college runs email, files, and identity for about 41,000 students and staff in one Microsoft 365 tenant. A consultant pulled the tenant configuration and the numbers are concerning: MFA covers only 68% of accounts, legacy authentication is still enabled, there are 19 global administrators, and external sharing defaults to anyone with the link. The unified audit log is on, which is the one bright spot. Measure the tenant against the CIS Microsoft 365 benchmark using the table below, decide which gaps are highest risk, and recommend a prioritized fix order. Be ready to defend why your first two fixes beat the others on risk, not just on ease.
objectives: Measure the tenant against the CIS benchmark in the materials
Identify the gaps that most increase the risk of account takeover
Recommend a prioritized, specific hardening order with justification

dossier.title: Tenant configuration versus CIS benchmark
dossier.intro: Current settings compared to the CIS Microsoft 365 Foundations benchmark.

rows: Yes: 32% of accounts have no MFA
Yes: legacy auth bypasses MFA entirely
Yes: anonymous links to org files

dossier.title: Identity and sign-in context
bullets: Sign-in logs show 31 successful basic-authentication logons in the last 30 days, all on legacy protocols that skip MFA.
Of the 19 global admins, 11 have not signed in as admin in over 90 days.
No conditional access policy currently blocks legacy authentication or enforces MFA for all users.
Break-glass emergency accounts exist but are mixed in with the 19 standing global admins.

dossier.title: Sharing and data exposure
bullets: A scan found 1,460 SharePoint and OneDrive items shared by anonymous link, including 38 with the word payroll or SSN in the file name.
External sharing is unrestricted by domain, so links work for any recipient anywhere.
Data loss prevention policies are not configured for the tenant.

deliverables: In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Posture review findings (Word or Excel)
One row per gap from the table: the control, the current state, the benchmark, the risk, and the specific setting change, in priority order.
Hardening plan (Word document)
Scope, method, and recommendations in your own words against the CIS benchmark and NIST SP 800-53. Justify the fix order by risk.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: the top tenant risks and the first two fixes to make this week.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the posture gaps to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What should you measure the tenant against to make findings defensible?
options: Whatever the last admin happened to prefer.
A competing college's tenant settings.
A recognized standard like the CIS Microsoft 365 benchmark.
steps.rationale: A published benchmark gives an objective, defensible standard. Personal preference and another org's tenant are neither objective nor authoritative.

steps.prompt: From the table, which gap most directly defeats MFA even where MFA is enabled?
options: External sharing set to anyone with the link.
Legacy authentication still enabled.
Nineteen global administrators.
steps.rationale: Legacy auth protocols do not support MFA, so they bypass it entirely. The sign-in logs even show 31 basic-auth logons. Sharing and admin sprawl are serious but do not bypass MFA.

steps.prompt: MFA covers 68% of accounts. What does the 32% gap mean for risk?
options: Roughly a third of accounts can be taken over with a stolen password alone.
It is acceptable because most users are covered.
It only matters for student accounts.
steps.rationale: Any account without MFA falls to a single stolen or sprayed password. A third of the tenant unprotected is a large takeover surface, not an acceptable remainder.

steps.prompt: Nineteen global admins, 11 inactive for 90+ days, with break-glass mixed in. How should you treat this?
options: Keep all 19 for redundancy.
Add more admins so no one is ever blocked.
Reduce to a few active admins and separate, secure dedicated break-glass accounts.
steps.rationale: Admin sprawl is attack surface. Reduce to a handful of active admins and keep break-glass accounts separate and tightly controlled. Adding admins makes it worse.

steps.prompt: Leadership asks for the first two fixes by risk. Which pair do you recommend?
options: Configure DLP, then rename the tenant.
Block legacy authentication, then enforce MFA for all users.
Reduce admins, then write new documentation.
steps.rationale: Blocking legacy auth closes the MFA bypass and enforcing MFA everywhere closes the 32% gap. Together they break the account-takeover path. DLP and admin reduction follow but are lower on the takeover-risk order.

steps.prompt: Which finding should the brief explicitly call out as already meeting the benchmark?
options: The unified audit log is on.
External sharing is set to anyone with the link.
There are 19 global administrators.
steps.rationale: The unified audit log is the one control that meets the benchmark and gives investigators evidence. The other two are open gaps, not strengths.

questions.prompt: Per the configuration table, what is the tenant's current MFA coverage?
questions.explain: The table lists current MFA coverage at 68%, leaving 32% of accounts unprotected against the 100% benchmark.

questions.prompt: Select the two gaps that most directly enable account takeover.
options: Legacy authentication enabled.
Unified audit log on.
MFA missing on 32% of accounts.
A configured DLP policy.
questions.explain: Legacy auth bypasses MFA and the 32% MFA gap leaves accounts open to a stolen password. The audit log is a strength and DLP is not even configured here.

questions.prompt: Why is legacy authentication a top-priority gap even though MFA is partly deployed?
options: It is slow.
Legacy protocols do not support MFA, so they bypass it entirely.
It costs extra licensing.
questions.explain: Legacy auth skips MFA by design, which is why the logs show 31 basic-auth logons. Blocking it is the first fix.

questions.prompt: Which control in the table already meets the CIS benchmark?
options: Unified audit log.
External sharing default.
Global administrator count.
questions.explain: The unified audit log is on and meets the benchmark, giving investigators an evidence trail. The others are open gaps.

hints: Measure against the CIS benchmark so every finding is defensible.
Anything that bypasses MFA, like legacy auth, ranks above everything else.
The audit log is your one strength. Call it out and keep it on.

answerKey.rootCause: The M365 tenant has legacy authentication enabled, only 68% MFA coverage, 19 global admins, and external sharing open to anyone with the link, while the unified audit log meets the benchmark. The evidenced top risk is account takeover via the MFA bypass and the MFA gap, so the first fixes are blocking legacy auth and enforcing MFA for all users.
containment: Block legacy authentication and enforce MFA for all users first
Reduce global admins to a few active accounts and separate secured break-glass accounts
Restrict external sharing to authenticated or specific people and remediate anonymous links

scenarios.debrief: Cloud posture work is about closing the defaults attackers love: legacy auth, admin sprawl, and open sharing. Benchmark the tenant, fix the MFA-bypassing gaps first, and report the few changes that matter most. The audit log being on is the one thing already done right.

scenarios.title: NIST 800-171 / CUI Assessment

briefing.subject: 800-171 readiness for federal research and aid data
briefing.body: A new federal research grant and certain student aid records count as Controlled Unclassified Information, so NIST SP 800-171 now applies to the systems that handle them. A walkthrough found CUI in four places, including a shared drive and email, and several control families are only partly met. The most serious issues are CUI emailed in clear text to personal accounts and a weak network boundary between the research lab and the general campus network. Work from the materials below: confirm where the CUI lives, rate each control family's status, and record every gap in a plan of action and milestones with owners and dates. Be ready to defend which gap you remediate first and why.
objectives: Confirm where CUI is created, stored, and transmitted
Rate each 800-171 control family against the materials
Record gaps in a POA&M and justify the first remediation

dossier.title: CUI location inventory
dossier.intro: Where the walkthrough found CUI across college systems.

rows: No encryption at rest, broad group access
CUI emailed in clear text to personal accounts

dossier.title: Control family status
dossier.intro: Self-assessment against selected 800-171 control families.

rows: Broad shared-drive groups, no least privilege on CUI
Unencrypted USB copies, no media controls
Weak boundary between lab and campus, CUI in clear-text email
MFA enforced on the aid system

dossier.title: Boundary and transmission notes
bullets: The research lab sits on the flat general campus VLAN with no firewall segmentation, so any campus host can reach the lab drive.
A sample of 14 faculty emails containing grant data showed 9 sent without enforced encryption, some to gmail and yahoo addresses.
There is no current System Security Plan and no POA&M, both of which the grant requires.

deliverables: In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
800-171 gap assessment and POA&M (Word or Excel)
One row per gap from the materials: the control family, the gap, the risk, the planned fix, an owner, and a due date.
Assessment report (Word document)
Scope, method, the CUI inventory, control-family status, and recommendations in your own words against NIST SP 800-171. Justify the first remediation.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: the CUI risk, the grant obligation, and the cost of getting compliant.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the CUI scope and gaps to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What is the first step of an 800-171 assessment?
options: Find where CUI is created, stored, and transmitted.
Buy new laptops for the research lab.
Rename the campus network.
steps.rationale: You cannot protect CUI you have not located. The inventory of where CUI lives and flows comes first. Hardware and renaming are not scoping.

steps.prompt: How is NIST SP 800-171 organized?
options: As a single pass-or-fail checkbox.
As control families covering access, audit, media, comms protection, and more.
As an optional set of suggestions.
steps.rationale: 800-171 is organized into control families derived from 800-53 and tailored for CUI in nonfederal systems. It is neither a single checkbox nor optional.

steps.prompt: From the status table, which control family is most clearly Not met and tied to the worst transmission gap?
options: Identification and Authentication (3.5).
Audit and Accountability (3.3).
System and Communications Protection (3.13).
steps.rationale: 3.13 is Not met and covers the weak lab-to-campus boundary and the clear-text CUI email. I&A is Met, and 3.3 is only Partial.

steps.prompt: CUI emailed in clear text to a personal gmail account is best described as:
options: A control gap: CUI must be encrypted in transit and kept within authorized systems.
Acceptable if it is faster for the researcher.
Encouraged, to keep a personal backup.
steps.rationale: CUI must be protected in transit and stay within authorized boundaries. Sending it unencrypted to a personal account violates both. Speed and personal backups are not justifications.

steps.prompt: How should every identified gap be tracked?
options: On a whiteboard that gets erased.
In a plan of action and milestones with owners and due dates.
Verbally in meetings.
steps.rationale: A POA&M is the standard, grant-required way to track 800-171 gaps to closure with accountability. Whiteboards and verbal notes are not auditable.

steps.prompt: Which gap is the most defensible first remediation?
options: Improve the aid system's minor logging gap.
Replace all faculty laptops.
Stop clear-text CUI email and segment the lab network, the worst transmission and boundary risks.
steps.rationale: The clear-text email and flat-network boundary are the most exposed CUI paths and map to the Not-met 3.13 family. The logging gap is minor and a laptop refresh is not targeted at the top risk.

questions.prompt: What does NIST SP 800-171 protect?
options: Only classified national security data.
Public marketing websites.
Internal-only HR memos.
Controlled Unclassified Information in nonfederal systems.
questions.explain: 800-171 is the control set for CUI handled in nonfederal systems, like the college's grant and aid data.

questions.prompt: Per the control-family table, select the two families marked Not met.
options: Media Protection (3.8).
Identification and Authentication (3.5).
System and Communications Protection (3.13).
Access Control (3.1).
questions.explain: Media Protection and System and Communications Protection are Not met. I&A is Met and Access Control is only Partial.

questions.prompt: CUI emailed in clear text to a personal account violates which requirement most directly?
options: Protection of CUI in transit and within authorized boundaries.
Records retention scheduling.
Software licensing terms.
questions.explain: CUI must be encrypted in transit and kept within authorized systems, both of which clear-text personal email breaks.

questions.prompt: How are 800-171 gaps formally tracked to closure?
options: In a press release.
In a plan of action and milestones (POA&M).
In a calendar invite.
questions.explain: A POA&M records each gap, its owner, and its due date, and is required by the grant.

hints: You can only protect CUI you have located. Build the inventory first.
800-171 mirrors 800-53 control families, tailored for CUI. Rate each family against the materials.
The clear-text email and flat network are your worst exposure. Track every gap in a POA&M.

answerKey.rootCause: Federal research and certain aid data are CUI, but practice does not meet several 800-171 control families. Media Protection and System and Communications Protection are Not met, with CUI emailed in clear text to personal accounts and a flat, unsegmented lab network as the worst exposures. There is no System Security Plan and no POA&M, both required by the grant.
containment: Stop clear-text CUI email and enforce encryption in transit, and segment the research lab from the flat campus network
Apply least privilege and encryption to the CUI shared drive and control or eliminate unencrypted USB copies
Record every gap in a POA&M with owners and due dates and stand up a System Security Plan

scenarios.debrief: 800-171 is what protects the federal data the college handles. The work is methodical: find the CUI, rate each control family from the evidence, and track gaps in a POA&M leadership can fund. The materials point to clear-text email and a flat network as the first things to fix.

scenarios.title: Records Retention and Data Governance

briefing.subject: Records retention and disposal review
briefing.body: The college keeps student records indefinitely because no one ever set a retention schedule. We are now storing about 1.2 million documents going back 40 years, and old data we no longer need is risk, not value. The materials show several record types held far past what FERPA and the state schedule require, including transcripts, disciplinary files, and old financial aid records with SSNs. Build a retention and disposal approach: set how long each record type is kept, define verifiable secure disposal, and align it with FERPA and state requirements. Be ready to defend which over-retained category you would dispose of first and why.
objectives: Define retention periods by record type from the materials
Set verifiable secure disposal rules and a destruction process
Align the schedule with FERPA and state requirements and prioritize disposal

dossier.title: Record types and retention gap
dossier.intro: Current versus required retention for the main record types.

dossier.title: Over-retention volumes
dossier.intro: Estimated documents held past their required period.

rows: Old financial aid records with SSN

dossier.title: Governance state
bullets: There is no written retention schedule and no owner for records disposal.
Deletion, when it happens, means dragging files to the recycle bin, which is recoverable and not verifiable.
Transcripts are correctly treated as permanent, so they are not a disposal target.
FERPA does not require indefinite retention. State schedules set defined periods for most non-transcript records.

deliverables: In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Retention schedule and disposal policy (Word document)
One row per record type: the retention period, the legal basis, the disposal method, and the owner. Mark which categories are over-retained and slated for disposal.
Disposal prioritization memo (Word document)
In your own words, the order of disposal by risk and the verifiable destruction method for each, justified against the materials.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership on the risk of over-retention and the first category to dispose of.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the retention schedule and disposal plan to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Why is keeping every record indefinitely a problem?
options: It improves security.
It is always more valuable to keep everything.
Old data you no longer need is breach risk and storage cost with no offsetting value.
steps.rationale: Data you do not need is liability. The less you hold, the less can be exposed. Indefinite retention grows risk, it does not improve security.

steps.prompt: What sets the correct retention period for a record type?
options: Legal and operational requirements, like FERPA and the state schedule.
The amount of storage space available.
How long the records have already been kept.
steps.rationale: Retention is driven by law and business need, not preference. The materials cite FERPA and state schedules as the basis.

steps.prompt: Which record type in the table is correctly retained and should NOT be a disposal target?
options: Admissions applications for students who never enrolled.
Transcripts, which are a permanent academic record.
Routine email older than two years.
steps.rationale: Transcripts are correctly permanent and stay. The other two are over-retained against their required periods and are disposal candidates.

steps.prompt: What does secure disposal of sensitive records require?
options: Dragging files to the recycle bin.
Leaving them on an old server no one uses.
Verifiable destruction, like shredding or cryptographic erasure.
steps.rationale: Disposal must be secure and verifiable so the data cannot be recovered. Recycle bin and abandoned servers leave the data intact.

steps.prompt: What makes the new schedule official and enforceable?
options: An unsigned draft in a folder.
Records owner and leadership sign-off with an effective date.
A rumor that it exists.
steps.rationale: A retention schedule needs a named owner and leadership approval with an effective date to actually be followed.

steps.prompt: By risk, which over-retained category should be disposed of first?
options: The 96,000 old financial aid records with SSNs past their required period.
The 640,000 routine emails, because the count is largest.
The transcripts, to free space.
steps.rationale: The aid records carry SSNs past the required period, the highest sensitivity per record. Routine email is high volume but low value, and transcripts are permanent and never disposed.

questions.prompt: Keeping data past its required retention is best described as:
options: Always an asset.
Required by FERPA.
A best practice for analytics.
A liability and breach risk.
questions.explain: Unneeded data is risk and cost with no value, and FERPA does not require indefinite retention.

questions.prompt: Per the materials, select the two record types most urgent to dispose of by sensitivity.
options: Old financial aid records with SSNs.
Transcripts.
Disciplinary records past five years.
The course catalog.
questions.explain: Aid records with SSNs and over-age disciplinary records are high-sensitivity over-retention. Transcripts are permanent and the catalog is public.

questions.prompt: Which record type is correctly retained as permanent?
options: Routine email.
Admissions applications for non-enrolled students.
Transcripts.
questions.explain: Transcripts are a permanent academic record. The other two have defined, shorter required periods.

questions.prompt: What does secure, verifiable disposal require?
options: Shredding or cryptographic erasure that makes data unrecoverable.
Moving files to the recycle bin.
Doing nothing.
questions.explain: Disposal must make the data unrecoverable and be verifiable, which the recycle bin is not.

hints: Data you do not need is liability, not value. FERPA does not require keeping everything forever.
Retention periods come from law and operational need. Transcripts are the one permanent record here.
Prioritize disposal by sensitivity per record, not just by volume. SSNs come first.

answerKey.rootCause: Indefinite retention left about 1.2 million documents held with no schedule and no owner, including 96,000 old aid records with SSNs and 22,000 over-age disciplinary records past their required periods. Disposal today is the recycle bin, which is recoverable and not verifiable. Transcripts are correctly permanent and stay.
containment: Set retention periods by record type aligned to FERPA and the state schedule, with transcripts kept permanent
Define verifiable secure disposal such as shredding or cryptographic erasure with a named owner
Dispose of the highest-sensitivity over-retained records first, starting with SSN-bearing aid records, and document each disposal

scenarios.debrief: Good data governance means keeping what you must and destroying what you should not keep. The materials show high-sensitivity SSN records held long past their required period, so those go first. Over-retention quietly grows breach exposure year after year, and a schedule only helps if expired data actually gets destroyed.

scenarios.title: Privacy Impact Assessment: Student Wellness App

briefing.subject: Privacy impact assessment for a wellness app
briefing.body: Student affairs wants to launch a mental-health and wellness app for our roughly 38,000 students, and it would collect very sensitive information. Before we sign anything, run a privacy impact assessment. The vendor's data sheet lists what the app collects, and some of it has nothing to do with wellness, like precise location and full contact lists. The app also shares data with a third-party analytics firm, and the proposed access list is far too broad. Work from the materials below: decide which data items are actually necessary, identify the privacy risks, and recommend minimization and access conditions for launch. Be ready to defend a launch, conditional launch, or no-launch recommendation against the evidence.
objectives: Decide which collected data items are necessary for the stated purpose
Identify the privacy risks, the access list, and the third-party sharing
Recommend minimization and access conditions, then a launch decision

dossier.title: Data collected by the app
dossier.intro: From the vendor data sheet, each item and whether it serves the wellness purpose.

rows: No: coarse or on-demand location is enough
No: one chosen contact is enough
Student ID and date of birth
Partial: ID yes, full DOB not required

dossier.title: Access list and sharing
dossier.intro: Who could see student wellness data under the current proposal.

rows: Too broad: no clinical need to know
Too broad: support does not need clinical content

dossier.title: Consent and governance notes
bullets: There is no clear, plain-language consent flow. Students would tap Agree on a long terms screen.
The vendor contract does not specify whether the analytics firm can resell or retain data.
No single law cleanly governs this data, but it is highly sensitive mental-health information and demands the tightest controls regardless.
There is no defined retention period for inactive accounts.

deliverables: In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Privacy impact assessment (Word document)
One row per data item: the purpose, whether it is necessary, and the recommended action. Add the access list, the third-party sharing risks, and the mitigations.
Minimization and access plan (Word document)
In your own words, which data to stop collecting, how to narrow the access list, and the contract terms the analytics firm must meet, justified against the materials.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: whether to launch, and the conditions that must be met first.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the privacy risks and your launch recommendation to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What does a privacy impact assessment start with?
options: Cataloging what personal data is collected and the justified purpose for each item.
The app's color scheme and icon.
The launch party date.
steps.rationale: A PIA begins by cataloging the data and the justified purpose for each item, which the data sheet provides. Design and launch logistics are not privacy scoping.

steps.prompt: The app collects continuous precise GPS to find nearby resources. How should you treat this?
options: Keep it, more data is safer.
Over-collection: coarse or on-demand location meets the purpose, so drop continuous GPS.
Require it for all users.
steps.rationale: Data minimization means collecting only what the purpose needs. Coarse or on-demand location finds nearby resources without continuous tracking. Collecting more is the privacy risk, not the safe choice.

steps.prompt: From the data table, which item is justified as necessary for the wellness purpose?
options: Full phone contact list.
Continuous precise GPS location.
Mood and journal entries.
steps.rationale: Mood and journal entries are core to wellness tracking. The contact list and continuous GPS are flagged as not necessary, since one chosen contact and coarse location suffice.

steps.prompt: Who on the proposed access list has a clear, appropriate need to see the full clinical record?
options: Licensed counselors.
The IT help desk.
All student affairs staff.
steps.rationale: Sensitive mental-health data needs tight, need-to-know access. Counselors have a clear clinical need. All-staff and help-desk full access are too broad and must be narrowed.

steps.prompt: Which set of mitigations most directly reduces the privacy risk here?
options: Collect more data to be safe and share it widely.
Minimize collection, restrict access to need-to-know, fix consent, and bind the analytics contract.
Launch first and review privacy later, after complaints arrive.
steps.rationale: Minimization, need-to-know access, plain-language consent, and a clear data-use contract directly cut the risks the materials raise. Collecting more or deferring review increases risk.

steps.prompt: Given the materials, what is the most defensible recommendation to leadership?
options: Do not launch under any circumstances.
Launch as proposed, the benefits outweigh the risks.
Conditional launch: proceed only after minimization, narrowed access, real consent, and a fixed analytics contract.
steps.rationale: The app has clear value but real, fixable privacy problems. A conditional launch tied to specific mitigations is the defensible call. Launching as proposed ignores the risks, and a flat no-launch discards a beneficial service that can be made safe.

questions.prompt: A privacy impact assessment begins by documenting:
options: The marketing budget.
What data is collected and the purpose for each item.
The app icon.
questions.explain: Cataloging the data and its justified purpose is the first step of a PIA, which the vendor data sheet supports.

questions.prompt: Per the data table, select the two items flagged as not necessary for the stated purpose.
options: Continuous precise GPS location.
Mood and journal entries.
Full phone contact list.
Counseling appointment scheduling.
questions.explain: Continuous GPS and the full contact list are over-collection. Coarse location and one chosen emergency contact meet the purpose. Mood entries and scheduling are necessary.

questions.prompt: On the access list, who has a clear appropriate need for the full clinical record?
options: Licensed counselors.
All student affairs staff.
The IT help desk.
questions.explain: Counselors have a clinical need to know. All-staff and help-desk full access are too broad and must be narrowed.

questions.prompt: What is the main risk in the third-party analytics arrangement as written?
options: It would slow the app down.
It uses too much storage.
It would raise the licensing cost.
The contract does not limit resale or retention, raising re-identification and misuse risk.
questions.explain: Unclear data-use terms mean the de-identified data could be retained, resold, or re-identified. The contract must bind the firm before launch.

hints: List every data item and the justified purpose. If the purpose is met with less, collect less.
Mental-health data demands the tightest, need-to-know access. Counselors yes, all-staff no.
An unclear third-party contract is its own risk. Bind data use before any launch.

answerKey.rootCause: The wellness app would collect sensitive student data beyond its purpose, including continuous GPS and full contact lists, grant full clinical access to all student affairs staff and the help desk, and share data with an analytics firm under an unclear contract, all with no real consent flow. The PIA recommends a conditional launch only after data minimization, need-to-know access, plain-language consent, and a binding analytics data-use contract.
containment: Minimize collection to the wellness purpose, dropping continuous GPS and the full contact list
Restrict full clinical access to licensed counselors and narrow help-desk and staff access
Require plain-language consent and a binding analytics contract limiting retention and resale before launch

scenarios.debrief: A privacy impact assessment catches privacy problems before launch, not after a complaint. The materials show clear over-collection, an overly broad access list, and an unclear third-party contract. The defensible call is a conditional launch: keep the valuable service, but only after minimizing data, locking access to need-to-know, and binding the vendor on data use.

scenarios.title: Tabletop Exercise: Ransomware on Registration Day

briefing.subject: Facilitate a ransomware tabletop exercise
briefing.body: On the first day of fall registration, when about 9,000 students try to enroll in a single morning, ransomware encrypts file servers and the Banner registration front end. You will design and facilitate a tabletop exercise around this exact day, following NIST SP 800-84. The materials below give you a timed inject schedule, the roles who must be in the room, and the known gaps in our current incident response plan. Drive the discussion inject by inject, make each role own a decision, and capture every gap in an after-action report. The hardest tension you must surface is the pressure to restore registration fast for 38,000 students against the need to preserve forensic evidence and avoid reencrypting clean systems. Work only from the materials, and defend your facilitation choices and your gap findings against them.
objectives: Design a realistic scenario with escalating injects tied to the real plan
Facilitate decisions across the right roles under time pressure
Capture every gap and owned action item in an after-action report

dossier.title: Inject schedule
dossier.intro: The timed injects you will deliver during the exercise. Each inject must force a decision from a named role.

rows: Help desk reports staff cannot open shared drives; ransom note appears on three servers
Declare an incident or keep triaging? Who has authority to declare?
Banner registration page returns errors; 9,000 students are mid-enrollment
Take registration offline to contain, or keep it up to serve students?
EDR shows the same encryptor process on 14 more hosts spreading by SMB
Isolate the network segment now, or wait to map the full blast radius?
Attacker portal claims 60 GB of student records were copied before encryption
Is this a reportable breach? Who decides notification and on what clock?
Backups exist but were last test-restored 11 months ago; restore would overwrite forensic images
Restore fast for students, or hold to preserve evidence and confirm eradication?

dossier.title: Roles in the room
bullets: Incident commander (CISO): declares the incident and owns the response decisions.
SOC lead: runs containment, isolation, and EDR actions.
Registrar and IT operations: own Banner and the registration service availability.
GRC and legal counsel: own breach determination and the notification clock.
Communications and the President's chief of staff: own messaging to students, faculty, and the cabinet.
Backup and recovery engineer: owns restore decisions and evidence preservation.

dossier.title: Known plan gaps
bullets: The current IR plan names no single person with authority to take Banner offline. It defaults to a committee, which stalls under time pressure.
Breach notification roles are undefined. The plan says report promptly but names no decision owner and no legal clock.
Backups are not test-restored on a schedule. The last successful test restore was 11 months ago.
There is no written rule on preserving forensic images before a restore, so a fast restore could destroy the only evidence.
No pre-drafted student or cabinet communications exist, so messaging would be written live during the crisis.

deliverables: Tabletop plan and inject deck (Word document)
The scenario narrative, the timed injects from the schedule, and the specific decision each role must make at each inject.
After-action report (Word document)
Following NIST SP 800-84: what was decided at each inject, every gap that surfaced, and the corrective action items with named owners and due dates.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: the top three gaps the exercise exposed and the single fix to fund first.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present your after-action findings to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Per NIST SP 800-84, what makes this tabletop worth running?
options: It tests the real IR plan and the real decision-makers against a believable registration-day scenario.
It scores each participant so HR can rank performance.
It uses a scenario so extreme no plan could ever cover it, to humble the team.
steps.rationale: 800-84 exercises validate the actual plan and the people who run it. A no-fault, realistic scenario surfaces gaps. Scoring individuals or using an impossible scenario teaches nothing actionable.

steps.prompt: The plan names no owner for taking Banner offline. How should you use that in the exercise?
options: Assign yourself the authority so the exercise keeps moving.
Skip the 08:25 inject so the team is not embarrassed.
Deliver the 08:25 inject and let the missing authority surface as a gap to record.
steps.rationale: The point of an inject is to force the decision the plan should cover. Letting the missing owner surface is exactly the gap the after-action report must capture. Skipping it or papering over it hides the real weakness.

steps.prompt: At 09:10 EDR shows the encryptor spreading by SMB to 14 more hosts. What is the best facilitation move?
options: End the exercise because containment is now obvious.
Press the SOC lead to choose: isolate the segment now or keep mapping, and record why.
Tell the team the answer so you stay on schedule.
steps.rationale: A good inject forces a named role to make and justify a decision under uncertainty. Handing them the answer or stopping the exercise defeats the purpose.

steps.prompt: The 10:30 inject says 60 GB of student records were copied. Who should own the notification decision in the room?
options: GRC and legal counsel, working the breach determination and the legal clock.
The help desk, since they saw it first.
Nobody decides during a tabletop; it is just talk.
steps.rationale: Breach determination and notification timing are governance and legal calls with a statutory clock. The roles table assigns them to GRC and legal, which is exactly the gap the plan left undefined.

steps.prompt: The 12:00 inject sets up the central tension. What should the exercise force the team to confront?
options: How to phrase the apology email.
Which vendor to blame in the press release.
Whether to restore fast for 38,000 students versus preserve forensic images and confirm eradication first.
steps.rationale: Registration-day pressure to restore fights the need to preserve evidence and avoid reencrypting clean systems. Surfacing this tension, and the missing rule on preserving images, is the core learning objective.

steps.prompt: What is the real output of an 800-84 tabletop?
options: A pass or fail grade for the team.
An after-action report listing gaps with corrective actions, owners, and due dates.
A verbal agreement that things went fine.
steps.rationale: The value of the exercise is the documented gaps turned into owned, dated corrective actions. Grades or undocumented agreements produce no improvement.

questions.prompt: From the plan gaps, which gap does the 08:25 registration inject most directly expose?
options: No single person has authority to take Banner offline.
Backups are not tested on a schedule.
No pre-drafted student communications exist.
Forensic image preservation is undefined.
questions.explain: The 08:25 inject demands a fast availability decision, and the plan defaults that to a stalling committee with no named owner. That is the gap it surfaces.

questions.prompt: The 10:30 data-theft inject is designed to surface which two gaps? Select two.
options: Undefined breach notification roles and clock.
No pre-drafted cabinet and student messaging.
Untested backups.
Missing network segmentation.
questions.explain: A claimed 60 GB exfiltration triggers the breach determination and the public messaging, and the plan defines neither the notification owner and clock nor any pre-drafted communications.

questions.prompt: Why is the 12:00 backup inject the hardest one to facilitate?
options: Because backups never matter in ransomware.
Because the registrar is not in the room.
Because it forces the restore-fast versus preserve-evidence tension with no written rule to settle it.
Because the encryptor has already been removed.
questions.explain: An 11-month-old untested backup plus no image-preservation rule means restoring fast could destroy evidence and reintroduce the threat. That unresolved tension is the point.

questions.prompt: Using the roles table, who should make the call at 08:05 to formally declare the incident?
options: The backup and recovery engineer.
The communications lead.
The registrar.
The incident commander, the CISO.
questions.explain: The roles table assigns declaring the incident and owning response decisions to the incident commander, the CISO. The other roles own narrower tasks like restore, messaging, or service availability.

hints: Each inject should map to a known plan gap and force a named role to decide.
The data-theft inject is where breach notification roles and the legal clock have to come up.
The backup inject is the real test: restoring fast for students fights preserving evidence and confirming eradication.

answerKey.rootCause: A registration-day ransomware tabletop, run under NIST SP 800-84, tests both the IR plan and the people. The injects surface the plan's real gaps: no owner to take Banner offline, undefined breach notification roles and clock, untested backups, and no rule on preserving forensic images before a restore. The central tension is restoring registration fast for 38,000 students versus preserving evidence and confirming eradication.
containment: Deliver escalating injects tied to each real plan gap and force named roles to decide
Surface the breach notification and forensic-preservation gaps explicitly
Capture every gap as an owned, dated corrective action in the after-action report

scenarios.debrief: A tabletop is cheap insurance. You learn where the plan breaks, and where people hesitate, in a conference room instead of during a real crisis on registration day. The injects here are built to expose specific gaps, and the after-action report with owned, dated actions is what turns the exercise into real improvement.

scenarios.title: Cyber Insurance Readiness Assessment

briefing.subject: Cyber insurance renewal readiness
briefing.body: Our cyber liability policy renews on October 1, and the carrier's renewal application asks pointed yes-or-no questions about our security controls. This matters more than it looks: if we attest yes to a control we do not actually have and later file a claim, the carrier can rescind the policy and deny payment when we need it most. The materials below are the questionnaire, with each control the insurer asks about, our honest current state, and the answer that current state supports. Several honest answers are no. Work through every row, map the gaps, and prioritize the fixes that most improve coverage and reduce premium risk before the renewal date. Use the materials and be ready to defend why an answer is yes or no.
objectives: Map each insurer control question to our honest current state
Identify exactly where the truthful answer is no
Prioritize the fixes that most improve coverage before October 1

dossier.title: Renewal application questionnaire
dossier.intro: Each control the carrier asks about, our honest current state, and the answer that state supports. Renewal date is October 1.

rows: MFA on all email and remote access
Enforced for IT staff and remote VPN, but not on faculty or staff email
MFA on all privileged and admin accounts
Enforced on all domain admin and Banner admin accounts
EDR deployed on all endpoints and servers
EDR on 94% of endpoints; 140 servers and lab machines uncovered
Backups are tested by restore at least annually
Backups run nightly to immutable storage but have never been test-restored
Proofpoint gateway in place, blocking about 1,200 phishing emails per week
Written incident response plan tested in the last 12 months
A plan exists from three years ago and has never been exercised
Privileged access management and least privilege reviewed
No PAM tool; access recertification last ran 19 months ago

dossier.title: How carriers weight these controls
bullets: MFA coverage is the single strongest factor carriers use to price and even to offer a policy. A no here can drive a surcharge or a declination.
Tested, immutable backups are weighted heavily because they directly reduce ransomware loss. Untested backups do not count as tested.
EDR coverage gaps matter most on servers, which is where the uncovered 140 machines sit.
A current, exercised IR plan signals the org can limit a loss. A three-year-old untested plan does not meet the question.
Material misrepresentation on the application is grounds for the carrier to rescind coverage after a claim.

dossier.title: Constraints before renewal
paras: MFA on faculty and staff email can be enabled inside the existing license at no new cost; the work is rollout and support, about three weeks.
A first full backup test restore can be scheduled within two weeks and is the cheapest control to flip from no to yes.
Full EDR coverage on the 140 servers needs procurement and is a longer effort. A PAM deployment is the longest and most expensive of all the gaps.

deliverables: Readiness gap analysis (Word or Excel)
One row per questionnaire item: the honest current state, the supported yes-or-no answer, the gap, and the fix with an owner and effort.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for the CFO: where coverage is at risk, and the two or three fixes to prioritize before October 1 ranked by coverage benefit per effort.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Attestation worksheet (Word or Excel)
The exact answers you would submit on the application, with a defensible note for every no.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the readiness gaps and your prioritized fixes to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Why does it matter that every application answer is accurate?
options: Carriers never verify the answers, so it is a formality.
Accurate answers always lower the premium.
A material misrepresentation lets the carrier rescind the policy and deny a future claim.
steps.rationale: If you attest to a control you lack, the carrier can void coverage when you file a claim. That is the whole risk. Carriers do verify after a loss, and accuracy does not by itself lower a premium.

steps.prompt: From the questionnaire, the MFA-on-email row should be answered:
options: No, because faculty and staff email is not covered.
Yes, since privileged accounts already have MFA.
Skip it, because email is low risk.
steps.rationale: The question asks about MFA on all email and remote access. Faculty and staff email is uncovered, so the honest answer is no, regardless of privileged-account MFA being a separate yes.

steps.prompt: Backups run nightly to immutable storage but have never been restore-tested. The truthful answer to the tested-backups question is:
options: Yes, the backups exist and are immutable.
No, until a restore is actually tested.
Not applicable.
steps.rationale: The question asks about tested backups. Untested backups are not tested backups, so the honest answer is no even though backups run nightly.

steps.prompt: Given the constraints, which fix flips a no to a strong yes fastest and cheapest?
options: Run the first full backup test restore, schedulable within two weeks at no procurement cost.
Buy EDR for the 140 uncovered servers.
Deploy a PAM tool across all privileged accounts.
steps.rationale: The constraints say a first test restore is the cheapest control to flip and can happen in two weeks. EDR needs procurement and PAM is the longest, most expensive effort, so neither is the fastest win.

steps.prompt: What does the CFO most need from your brief?
options: The IT organization chart.
Where coverage is at risk and the prioritized fixes to make before October 1.
A list of every vendor the college uses.
steps.rationale: The CFO owns the coverage decision and the renewal. The brief must show the coverage risk from the honest no answers and the prioritized, dated fixes before renewal.

questions.prompt: From the questionnaire, how many controls honestly answer no?
options: Two.
Seven.
Five.
None.
questions.explain: MFA on email, EDR coverage, tested backups, the IR plan, and PAM and access review all answer no. The privileged-account MFA and email gateway rows are the only honest yes answers, leaving five no's.

questions.prompt: Which two fixes give the most coverage benefit for the least effort before renewal? Select two.
options: Enable MFA on faculty and staff email within the existing license.
Run the first backup test restore.
Deploy a PAM tool.
Replace the email gateway.
questions.explain: MFA is the highest-weighted control and is license-covered, and the test restore is the cheapest no to flip. PAM is the longest, costliest effort, and the gateway is already a yes.

questions.prompt: Why is reporting backups as tested when they were never restored dangerous?
options: It raises the premium.
It has no effect on coverage.
It violates HIPAA directly.
It is a material misrepresentation that can let the carrier deny a ransomware claim.
questions.explain: Attesting to tested backups you never tested is exactly the kind of misstatement that lets a carrier rescind coverage at claim time, when ransomware recovery is most needed.

questions.prompt: Which questionnaire row is an honest yes despite the MFA-on-email row being a no?
options: EDR deployed on all endpoints and servers.
Written incident response plan tested in the last 12 months.
MFA on all privileged and admin accounts.
Privileged access management reviewed.
questions.explain: Privileged-account MFA is fully enforced on all domain admin and Banner admin accounts, so it is an honest yes even though MFA on faculty and staff email is a no. EDR, the IR plan, and PAM all answer no.

hints: Answer every row from the honest current state column, not from what sounds good.
MFA coverage is the control carriers weight most heavily, so a no there is the costliest gap.
The cheapest no to flip is the untested backup. A first test restore is fast and free.

answerKey.rootCause: The renewal application would be tempting to answer optimistically, but five controls honestly answer no: MFA on email, full EDR coverage, tested backups, a current exercised IR plan, and PAM with access review. Overstating any of them risks rescission at claim time. The highest-value, lowest-effort fixes are enabling MFA on faculty and staff email within the existing license and running the first backup test restore, both achievable before the October 1 renewal.
containment: Answer every questionnaire row truthfully from the current-state evidence and flag each no
Flip MFA on email and a first backup test restore first as the cheapest high-weight wins
Brief the CFO on coverage risk and the prioritized, dated fixes before October 1

scenarios.debrief: A cyber insurance application is really a controls checklist with legal teeth. Answer it honestly and it both protects your future claim and hands you a prioritized list of exactly which controls to improve. Here the cheapest, highest-impact moves, MFA on email and a first test restore, can be done before renewal, while EDR and PAM are owned as longer-term work.

scenarios.title: Asset Inventory and Shadow-IT Review

briefing.subject: Asset inventory and shadow IT across departments
briefing.body: Departments across the college keep buying their own apps and devices on purchasing cards without telling IT, and you cannot protect what you do not know exists. The materials below give you our inventory-completeness numbers and a discovered shadow-IT list, including tools that hold student data outside any security review. Work the numbers, judge how trustworthy the inventory is, and rank the shadow IT by the sensitivity of the data each tool holds. Then recommend a lightweight intake process so new assets and apps get registered and assessed before they go live, anchored in NIST SP 800-53 CM controls. Use only the materials and defend your risk ranking and your process design against them.
objectives: Assess how complete and current the asset inventory really is
Rank the discovered shadow IT by data sensitivity and risk
Recommend a lightweight intake process to register and assess new assets

dossier.title: Inventory completeness
dossier.intro: How much of the environment the official CMDB actually accounts for, by category.

rows: Network and IoT devices (cameras, printers, lab gear)

dossier.title: Discovered shadow IT
dossier.intro: Apps found through expense-report and DNS review that IT never approved or assessed.

rows: Student names, IDs, and disability accommodations
Aid applications with SSNs and tax data
Student essays and graded work pasted in

dossier.title: Why this is the blind spot
bullets: The departmental SaaS and IoT categories are under half covered, so the riskiest, fastest-growing parts of the environment are the least known.
Three shadow-IT tools hold regulated data: accommodation records and aid data are FERPA and GLBA covered, and the SSNs in the file share fall under state breach law.
None of the four unsanctioned tools went through a security review, a data processing agreement, or a vendor risk assessment.
Free-tier AI tools often train on submitted content, so pasting student work in can leak FERPA-protected records.
There is no intake process today, so buying on a purchasing card is faster and easier than asking IT.

deliverables: Inventory gap and shadow-IT findings (Word or Excel)
The coverage gaps by category, the discovered shadow IT ranked by data sensitivity, the risk each poses, and a recommended owner per item.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: the riskiest unmanaged assets and the proposed intake process, with the single tool to address first.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Intake process design (Word document)
A simple register-and-assess workflow for new apps and devices, mapped to NIST SP 800-53 CM controls.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the inventory gaps and your intake process to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Why is asset inventory the foundation for the rest of security?
options: It is only needed for accounting and depreciation.
You cannot patch, monitor, or defend an asset you do not know exists.
It matters only for physical devices, not software.
steps.rationale: Every other control assumes you know what you have. Unknown assets cannot be patched, monitored, or included in incident response. It is not just an accounting exercise and it covers software too.

steps.prompt: From the completeness table, which gap should worry you most?
options: Managed workstations at 96% coverage.
Servers at 90% coverage.
Departmental SaaS at 41% coverage, where data-holding apps hide.
steps.rationale: SaaS coverage at 41% is the worst and is exactly where unsanctioned, data-holding apps live. Workstations and servers are well covered by comparison.

steps.prompt: Which shadow-IT tool is the highest risk to address first?
options: The personal cloud file share in Financial Aid holding SSNs and tax data.
The approved LMS plugin in Online Learning.
The consumer scheduling app holding class schedules.
steps.rationale: The file share holds SSNs and tax data, the most regulated and damaging data set, under GLBA and state breach law. The LMS plugin is already sanctioned, and schedules are less sensitive than SSNs.

steps.prompt: What most reduces shadow IT going forward, per the CM control family?
options: Ban all departmental purchasing of software forever.
Ignore it, since departments know their own needs.
A simple, fast intake process so new apps and devices get registered and assessed before going live.
steps.rationale: A bare ban gets bypassed and ignoring the problem leaves data unprotected. An easy official path to register and vet tools is what actually changes behavior, which is the configuration management baseline goal.

steps.prompt: What belongs in the leadership brief?
options: The inventory gaps, the shadow-IT risks ranked by data sensitivity, and the proposed intake process.
A raw device count and nothing else.
An apology with no recommendations.
steps.rationale: Leadership needs the risk picture and the fix. A bare count gives no risk context, and an apology without a process changes nothing.

questions.prompt: Which asset category has the weakest inventory coverage?
options: Managed workstations.
Servers and VMs.
Departmental SaaS applications.
None; all are above 90%.
questions.explain: Departmental SaaS sits at 41% coverage, the lowest of all categories and the place data-holding shadow IT hides.

questions.prompt: Which two shadow-IT tools hold regulated data that pulls in FERPA or GLBA? Select two.
options: The survey tool holding disability accommodation records.
The personal cloud file share holding aid applications with SSNs.
The approved LMS plugin.
The consumer scheduling app holding class schedules.
questions.explain: Accommodation records are FERPA education records and the aid applications with SSNs are GLBA and state-breach covered. The LMS plugin is sanctioned and reviewed, and schedules are far less sensitive.

questions.prompt: Why is the free-tier AI tutoring chatbot a FERPA concern specifically?
options: It costs money the department did not budget.
Free AI tools may train on submitted content, leaking pasted student work and grades.
It is slower than the approved tools.
It only works on personal devices.
questions.explain: Students' essays and graded work pasted into a free AI tool can be retained or used for training, exposing FERPA-protected records with no agreement in place.

questions.prompt: Combining the two estimated categories, roughly how many SaaS and IoT assets are missing from the CMDB?
options: About 50.
About 100.
About 250.
About 920.
questions.explain: SaaS is short about 106 (180 minus 74) and network and IoT is short about 810 (1,500 minus 690), totaling roughly 920 unaccounted assets in the two estimated categories.

hints: Read the coverage column. The lowest number is where the real risk hides.
Rank shadow IT by the sensitivity of the data each tool holds, not by department size.
Make the right way the easy way. A simple intake process beats both a ban and a blind eye.

answerKey.rootCause: The asset inventory is strong on managed endpoints but weak where it matters, with departmental SaaS at 41% and network and IoT at 46% coverage. Four unsanctioned shadow-IT tools run with no review, three holding regulated data, including a personal cloud file share with SSNs and tax data and a free AI tool holding student work. The fix completes the inventory, addresses the regulated-data tools first, and adds a lightweight intake process mapped to NIST SP 800-53 CM so new assets are registered and assessed before going live.
containment: Complete and continuously update the inventory, prioritizing the SaaS and IoT gaps
Remediate the regulated-data shadow IT first, starting with the SSN-holding file share
Stand up a simple intake process under the CM control family to register and vet new assets

scenarios.debrief: Asset management is the unglamorous base under every other control. Patching, monitoring, and incident response all assume you know what you have, and the completeness numbers here show the blind spot is exactly where data-holding apps hide. Rank the shadow IT by data sensitivity, fix the SSN-holding tool first, and make registration easy enough that people use it instead of going around IT.

scenarios.title: Controls Assessment and Audit: SIS

briefing.subject: Controls assessment of the Banner SIS
briefing.body: Internal Audit needs an independent assessment of the security controls protecting Banner, the Student Information System for 38,000 students. Using NIST SP 800-53A as the method, you will test each control two ways: is it implemented, and is it actually operating effectively. The materials below are our control-test results, with the evidence gathered for each. Several controls exist on paper but are not followed in practice, and your job is to call those out as findings. Document each result, attach the evidence, and write a recommendation with an owner and a due date for every gap. Work from the materials and defend each finding against the evidence, not against opinion.
objectives: Confirm the controls in scope and the 800-53A assessment method
Test each control for both implementation and effectiveness
Document findings with evidence and owned, dated recommendations

dossier.title: Control test results
dossier.intro: Each control assessed for implementation and effectiveness, with the objective evidence gathered.

rows: Policy requires quarterly review; last review was 19 months ago; 230 separated-employee accounts still active
Enforced for IT staff only; 0% of student and 59% of faculty accounts lack MFA
Logs are collected but no one reviews them; no documented review since the SIEM was installed
Nightly immutable backups confirmed; last successful test restore was 8 months ago, outside the annual requirement
Hardened baseline applied and verified on all Banner servers in the last scan
27 standard users hold registrar admin rights with no documented business need

dossier.title: The 800-53A method
bullets: Each control objective is assessed by three methods: examine the artifacts, interview the staff, and test the control in operation.
Implemented means the control exists and is in place. Effective means it is operating as intended and producing the result it should.
A control can be implemented but not effective. A documented policy that nobody follows is the classic example, and it is still a finding.
Findings are rated by the risk of the gap, and each needs a recommendation, a named owner, and a due date to drive remediation.
Independence is what gives the assessment credibility with leadership and the audit committee. The assessor must not own the controls being tested.

dossier.title: Audit committee context
paras: The committee meets quarterly and expects findings ranked by risk, not a flat list. The two account-related findings, AC-2 and AC-6, both leave excessive standing access into a 38,000-student system and rate highest.
One control, CM-6, passed both implementation and effectiveness and should be reported as a strength, not buried among the gaps. Reporting what works is part of a credible assessment.

deliverables: Controls assessment report (Word document)
For each control: the test method, the evidence, the implemented and effective results, the finding if any, and a recommendation with owner and due date.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for the audit committee on overall control effectiveness, the highest-risk findings, and the one strength to acknowledge.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Findings and remediation tracker (Word or Excel)
Every finding ranked by risk, with owner, due date, and status, ready for the next committee meeting.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the control findings and recommendations to leadership as if briefing the audit committee. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What does an 800-53A assessment actually determine?
options: How many users the system has.
Whether each control is implemented and operating effectively.
The dollar value of the server hardware.
steps.rationale: 800-53A assesses whether controls work, not just whether they exist. User counts and hardware value are irrelevant to control effectiveness.

steps.prompt: Per the method, how is a control objectively tested?
options: By examining artifacts, interviewing staff, and testing the control in operation.
By assuming it works because a policy exists.
By asking the control owner once and moving on.
steps.rationale: Examine, interview, and test is the 800-53A method for gathering objective evidence. Assuming or a single question gives no evidence.

steps.prompt: AC-2 has a quarterly-review policy but the last review was 19 months ago and 230 separated accounts are active. The result is:
options: Fully effective, since the policy exists.
Out of scope for the assessment.
Implemented but not effective, a finding.
steps.rationale: The control exists on paper but is not operating. A policy nobody follows is implemented but not effective, which is a finding under the method.

steps.prompt: Which finding should rate highest for the committee?
options: CP-9 backups, where the test restore is 8 months old.
AC-6 least privilege, with 27 users holding registrar admin rights with no business need.
CM-6 configuration baseline, which passed.
steps.rationale: Excessive standing admin access into a 38,000-student system is the highest-risk gap. The backup test is overdue but lower risk, and CM-6 is a passing strength, not a finding.

steps.prompt: What must every finding carry to drive remediation?
options: A recommendation, a named owner, and a due date.
Just a description of what is wrong.
A note that it will be looked at eventually.
steps.rationale: Findings only lead to fixes when they carry an action, an owner, and a date. A bare description or a vague someday note changes nothing.

steps.prompt: Why does the assessor's independence matter to this audit?
options: It does not; only the findings matter.
It exists only to slow the process down.
An independent assessor gives leadership and the committee a credible, unbiased result.
steps.rationale: Independence, the assessor not owning the controls tested, is what makes the result trustworthy to leadership and the audit committee.

questions.prompt: From the results table, how many controls are findings (implemented but not fully effective)?
options: One.
Three.
Five.
All six.
questions.explain: AC-2, IA-2, AU-6, CP-9, and AC-6 all fall short on effectiveness. Only CM-6 passed both, so five controls are findings.

questions.prompt: Which two findings leave excessive standing access into the SIS and rate highest? Select two.
options: AC-2 with 230 active separated-employee accounts.
AC-6 with 27 users holding unneeded registrar admin rights.
CM-6 configuration baseline.
CP-9 backup test restore timing.
questions.explain: Both account controls leave too much standing access into a 38,000-student system, the highest-risk gaps. CM-6 passed and CP-9 is a lower-risk timing issue.

questions.prompt: Why should CM-6 appear in the report even though it is not a finding?
options: To pad the report length.
Because reporting a control that works is part of a credible, balanced assessment.
Because it is the highest risk.
It should not appear at all.
questions.explain: A credible assessment reports strengths as well as gaps. CM-6 passed implementation and effectiveness, so it belongs in the report as a strength.

questions.prompt: The IA-2 evidence shows MFA enforced for IT staff only. What is the most defensible result for that control?
options: Not implemented at all.
Implemented and fully effective.
Out of scope.
Partially implemented and not effective, a finding.
questions.explain: MFA exists for IT staff but 0% of students and 59% of faculty lack it, so the control is only partially implemented and is not effective at protecting Banner access. That is a finding.

hints: 800-53A asks whether a control works, not just whether it is written down.
Gather objective evidence three ways: examine, interview, test.
Rank findings by risk. Excessive standing access into a 38,000-student system outranks an overdue backup test.

answerKey.rootCause: The Banner controls assessment under NIST SP 800-53A finds five of six controls implemented on paper but not operating effectively: account recertification (AC-2) 19 months overdue with 230 active separated accounts, partial MFA (IA-2), unreviewed audit logs (AU-6), an overdue backup test restore (CP-9), and excessive least privilege (AC-6) with 27 unneeded admins. The two account controls leave the most standing access and rate highest. CM-6 passed and should be reported as a strength. Each finding carries an owned, dated recommendation, and the assessor's independence gives the result credibility.
containment: Test each control for implementation and effectiveness using examine, interview, and test
Rank findings by risk, with the AC-2 and AC-6 access gaps highest, and report CM-6 as a strength
Attach a recommendation, a named owner, and a due date to every finding

scenarios.debrief: An audit asks a sharper question than a checklist: not is the control written down, but does it actually work. Here five controls exist on paper yet fail in practice, and the two account controls that leave excessive standing access into a 38,000-student system rate highest. 800-53A gives you the examine-interview-test method, independence gives the result its credibility, and owned, dated recommendations turn findings into fixes.

scenarios.title: Acceptable Use Policy Development

briefing.subject: Refresh the acceptable use policy
briefing.body: Our acceptable use policy was last updated in 2014 and predates the cloud, the smartphone era at work, and generative AI entirely. Two recent incidents traced straight back to the gaps, and the policy gives us no clear rule to point to. The materials below are a gap analysis of the current AUP against the topics a modern policy must cover, plus the two incidents that exposed the holes. Work the gap table, judge which gaps matter most, and draft a refreshed policy that is plain-language, specific, and actually enforceable. Then plan the review, approval, and acknowledgment so it can be adopted. Use the materials and defend your priorities and your wording choices against them.
objectives: Identify what a modern AUP must cover from the gap analysis
Write clear, specific, enforceable rules in plain language
Plan review, approval, and a tracked acknowledgment for adoption

dossier.title: AUP gap analysis
dossier.intro: Each topic a modern AUP should cover, whether the current 2014 policy covers it, and the issue.

rows: No rule on storing college data in personal Dropbox, Drive, or similar
Mentions personal laptops once but sets no security or data rules for phones
Silent on pasting student data or college data into AI tools
Covered, but references a retired email system and an old reporting address
Still references peer-to-peer file sharing and instant messenger, not modern apps
States rules exist but names no consequences and no acknowledgment requirement

dossier.title: Incidents that exposed the gaps
bullets: A staff member uploaded a spreadsheet of 1,400 student records to a personal cloud drive to work from home. No AUP rule prohibited it, so there was nothing to enforce.
A faculty member pasted a roster of student names and grades into a free AI chatbot to draft feedback. The policy said nothing about AI, and the data may now be retained by the vendor.
In both cases, HR asked which policy was violated and the answer was none clearly enough to act on.

dossier.title: What makes an AUP enforceable
paras: Rules people cannot understand do not get followed, and rules that are vague cannot be enforced. Plain language and specific prohibitions are what make a policy usable.
An AUP touches many groups, so it needs review by legal, HR, IT, and student representatives before approval. Skipping a group invites a rule that cannot be applied to them.
Enforcement rests on two things: formal leadership approval and a recorded acknowledgment from each user. Without the acknowledgment, you cannot show the user agreed to the rule you are enforcing.

deliverables: Draft acceptable use policy (Word document)
The full refreshed policy: scope, plain-language rules covering cloud, BYOD, and AI, consequences, and an acknowledgment section.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership on what changed since 2014, which gaps the recent incidents exposed, and how the policy will be adopted.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Adoption plan (Word document)
The review, approval, and tracked-acknowledgment steps, with the groups to involve and the rollout communication.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the new policy and adoption plan to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: From the gap table, which topics is the 2014 AUP completely silent on?
options: Email and acceptable personal use.
Enforcement and consequences only.
Cloud and personal file-sharing and generative AI tools.
steps.rationale: The table marks cloud and AI as not covered at all. Email and personal use are covered but outdated, and enforcement is partial, not absent.

steps.prompt: How should the new rules be written so people follow and you can enforce them?
options: In dense legalese that covers every edge case.
In plain language with specific, enforceable prohibitions.
As vague suggestions to allow flexibility.
steps.rationale: People only follow rules they understand, and only specific rules can be enforced. Legalese goes unread and vague suggestions cannot be applied.

steps.prompt: The faculty AI incident shows the AUP needs which specific rule?
options: A clear rule against pasting student or college data into AI tools without approval.
A ban on all email attachments.
A rule about peer-to-peer file sharing.
steps.rationale: A roster pasted into a free AI tool exposed FERPA data with no rule to cite. The fix is a specific prohibition on putting protected data into unapproved AI tools.

steps.prompt: Who should review the draft before approval?
options: Only the policy author.
No one; speed matters more.
Legal, HR, IT, and student representatives.
steps.rationale: An AUP applies to many groups. Legal, HR, IT, and students each catch a rule that cannot be applied to them, which is why the materials require all four.

steps.prompt: What two things make the policy enforceable?
options: Formal leadership approval and a recorded acknowledgment from each user.
Posting it on a website and hoping people read it.
Word of mouth from supervisors.
steps.rationale: The HR question in both incidents was which policy was violated. Approval plus a recorded acknowledgment is what lets you show the user agreed to the rule.

steps.prompt: How should the refreshed AUP be rolled out?
options: Posted once with no announcement.
Kept internal to IT.
Communicated to students and staff with a tracked acknowledgment.
