steps.rationale: Adoption needs active communication and a tracked acknowledgment so there is a record each person agreed. A silent posting or an IT-only file does not reach the community.

questions.prompt: From the gap table, which two topics is the current AUP completely missing? Select two.
options: Cloud and personal file-sharing services.
Generative AI tools.
Email and phishing responsibilities.
Acceptable personal use.
questions.explain: Cloud and AI are both marked not covered. Email and personal use are covered but reference retired systems, so they need updating, not adding.

questions.prompt: What did both recent incidents have in common?
options: Malware was installed on a server.
There was no clear AUP rule for HR to enforce against.
An outside attacker breached the network.
The email gateway failed.
questions.explain: In both the cloud upload and the AI paste, HR asked which policy was violated and found none clear enough to act on. The missing rules are the common thread.

questions.prompt: What two things make the refreshed policy enforceable?
options: A long appendix and a glossary.
Broad, general language that tries to cover everything.
Posting it on the intranet only.
Formal leadership approval and a recorded user acknowledgment.
questions.explain: Enforcement rests on formal approval and a recorded acknowledgment so you can show the user agreed to the rule being applied.

questions.prompt: The staff cloud-upload incident most directly argues for adding which rule to the AUP?
options: A prohibition on storing college data in personal cloud or file-sharing services.
A rule requiring quarterly password changes.
A ban on personal laptops entirely.
A rule about acceptable break-room use.
questions.explain: A staff member moved 1,400 student records to a personal cloud drive with no rule against it. The gap the incident exposes is exactly the missing cloud and personal file-sharing prohibition.

hints: Start with the topics marked not covered: cloud and AI are the new gaps.
Both incidents failed for the same reason, no clear rule to enforce. Write that rule plainly.
Enforceability needs two things: approval and a recorded acknowledgment from each user.

answerKey.rootCause: The 2014 AUP predates cloud, modern BYOD, and generative AI, leaving cloud file-sharing and AI tools completely uncovered and email, personal use, and enforcement outdated or incomplete. Two incidents, a 1,400-record cloud upload and a student roster pasted into a free AI tool, exposed the gaps when HR had no clear rule to enforce. The refresh adds plain, specific, enforceable rules for cloud, BYOD, and AI, is reviewed by legal, HR, IT, and students, is formally approved, and is adopted through a tracked acknowledgment.
containment: Add plain, specific, enforceable rules for cloud, BYOD, and AI tools
Review the draft with legal, HR, IT, and student representatives
Approve the policy and roll it out with a tracked user acknowledgment

scenarios.debrief: An acceptable use policy sets the ground rules for the whole community, and a decade-old one leaves real gaps that incidents will find. The two incidents here both failed the same way: no clear rule for HR to enforce. Keep the policy current with the tools people actually use, write it so people can follow it, and make sure there is a record they agreed.

scenarios.title: Vendor Breach Response: Billing SaaS

briefing.subject: Breach notice from the BVHN billing vendor
briefing.body: MedLedger, the SaaS billing vendor for our BVHN health partner, sent a breach notice at 6:40 this morning. Their integration uses one OAuth token granted into our cloud tenant, and that token can read the BVHN billing dataset, about 41,200 patient billing records. MedLedger says an attacker stole a set of customer API tokens, but they will not confirm whether ours was among them. Do not take their word for it. Confirm in the SIEM feed whether the token was actually used to pull data from our side and where it went, then use the materials below to reassess the vendor with HECVAT and check the business associate agreement. The data is patient billing, so HIPAA and the BAA are both in scope, and the notification clock may already be running.
objectives: Confirm whether the vendor token accessed our data and what was taken
Scope the exposure and the regulated data involved
Reassess the vendor's risk and verify the business associate agreement

iocs.note: Destination the vendor token data was pushed to

dossier.title: MedLedger vendor profile and prior rating
dossier.intro: What we knew about this vendor before the breach.

rows: Cloud billing and claims for BVHN
Yes, signed 2 years ago, never reviewed since
Single OAuth token, read scope on billing dataset

dossier.title: What the token could reach
dossier.intro: Scope of the OAuth grant and the data at stake.

dossier.title: HECVAT and BAA context
bullets: The signed BAA requires MedLedger to report a security incident to us within 5 business days. Their notice arrived 11 days after their stated detection date.
The prior HECVAT did not require token rotation, encryption-at-rest attestation, or proof of a SOC 2 report. Those gaps were accepted as conditions to revisit at renewal.
HIPAA gives a covered entity up to 60 days to notify affected individuals once a breach is known. The breach is known to us as of today.
MedLedger holds PHI for BVHN, which makes them a business associate. A current BAA with breach-notification terms is mandatory under HIPAA.

deliverables: In-app investigation and assessment
Confirm the exposure in the logs, work the assessment steps, and answer the graded questions. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Vendor risk reassessment (HECVAT) (Word document)

Re-rate MedLedger's risk in light of the breach, using the HECVAT questionnaire as your framework. Cite the late notice, the never-rotated token, and the missing attestations, then recommend approve with conditions, suspend, or terminate.
Business associate agreement review (Word document)
Confirm the BAA is in place and check its breach-notification and data-handling terms against what actually happened, including the 5-day reporting clause the vendor appears to have missed.
Executive brief and notification memo (Word document)
One page for leadership and a draft notification decision: who must be told, by when, under HIPAA and state breach law.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes

Required. On camera, present what the token accessed, the regulated data involved, and your vendor recommendation to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What in the feed confirms the vendor token actually pulled data, not just that a breach was reported?
options: A help-desk ticket about slow email
A single failed login
The OAuth grant tied to the vendor, followed by a mass download from the tenant and an outbound upload
steps.rationale: The OAuth grant plus a mass download and an outbound upload is the chain that proves the token was used to take data. A ticket or one failed login proves nothing about exfiltration.

steps.prompt: The materials list 41,200 records the token could reach. Which classification drives the response?
options: Patient billing data, which is PHI under HIPAA
Public payment FAQ pages
Aggregated, de-identified billing totals
steps.rationale: Billing and claims tied to patients are protected health information. The 41,200 PHI records set the legal and notification requirements. The FAQ pages and an address are not regulated.

steps.prompt: First containment action for a compromised vendor integration?
options: Email the vendor and ask them to stop
Revoke the vendor's OAuth token and rotate the integration credentials
Wait for their next breach update
steps.rationale: Revoking the token cuts the access immediately on your side. You control the grant; do not wait on a vendor who already reported 11 days late.

steps.prompt: Given the prior Moderate rating, the late notice, and a never-rotated token, how should the vendor's risk change?
options: Lower it, because they self-reported honestly
Leave it unchanged, since a breach can happen to anyone
Re-rate it higher using the HECVAT, and require token rotation, SOC 2 evidence, and faster reporting as conditions
steps.rationale: A confirmed breach plus a missed reporting clause raises residual risk above Moderate. HECVAT is the higher-ed framework for re-rating and setting binding conditions. Honesty does not lower a demonstrated risk.

steps.prompt: The contract review and notification analysis should confirm what for this PHI?
options: A business associate agreement (BAA) is in place, its 5-day reporting term was breached, and the HIPAA 60-day notification clock now applies
Nothing is needed because the vendor is a SaaS provider
A marketing co-branding agreement governs the data
steps.rationale: Any vendor handling PHI needs a BAA, and ours sets a 5-day reporting term the vendor missed. The HIPAA clock is up to 60 days from when we know, which is now. A SaaS label does not remove HIPAA, and a co-branding deal is irrelevant.

questions.prompt: What single action stops the vendor token from being used further?
options: Reset every student password
Revoke the OAuth token and rotate the credentials
Disable the campus wifi
questions.explain: The exposure is the vendor's token. Revoke it and rotate the credentials to cut the access. Student passwords and wifi are unrelated.

questions.prompt: Which two indicators belong on the blocklist from this case?
options: The exfiltration domain
The exfiltration IP
The college's own cloud tenant URL
The CISO's mailbox
questions.explain: Block the adversary's exfiltration domain and IP. Never block your own legitimate infrastructure like the tenant URL or a mailbox.

questions.prompt: Because the data is PHI, the vendor relationship requires:
options: A press release
A new firewall only
A business associate agreement (BAA)
questions.explain: HIPAA requires a BAA with any business associate that handles PHI. A firewall or a press release does not satisfy that requirement.

questions.prompt: MedLedger reported the breach 11 days after their stated detection date. Against the BAA's 5-day reporting term, this is:
options: A breach of the BAA's notification clause and a finding for the HECVAT reassessment
Acceptable, since HIPAA gives the college up to 60 days
Irrelevant, because they reported eventually
questions.explain: The 60-day HIPAA window is the college's clock to notify individuals, not the vendor's reporting deadline. The BAA sets 5 business days, which they missed, so it is a contract breach and a HECVAT finding.

hints: Follow the OAuth grant to a mass download and then an outbound upload. That chain proves data left.
Classify the data first. The 41,200 PHI records set the legal requirements.
Revoke the token on your side before chasing the vendor, and check the BAA's reporting clock against their 11-day delay.

answerKey.rootCause: A compromised OAuth token belonging to the MedLedger billing vendor was used to mass-download about 41,200 patient billing records (PHI) from the college cloud tenant and exfiltrate them. The vendor reported 11 days late, breaching the BAA's 5-day term. The response is to revoke the token, scope the PHI exposure, reassess the vendor with HECVAT and binding conditions, and verify the BAA and HIPAA notification duties.
containment: Revoke the vendor OAuth token and rotate integration credentials
Block the exfiltration domain and IP
Reassess the vendor with HECVAT and verify the BAA and breach-notification terms

scenarios.debrief: Third-party breaches are still your incident. Confirm what the vendor's access actually touched in the feed, contain on your own side first, then turn the event into a HECVAT reassessment and a BAA and notification review. In healthcare, the BAA terms and the HIPAA notification clock are not optional, and a late vendor notice is itself a finding.

scenarios.title: FERPA Data Exposure: Misconfigured Cloud Share

briefing.subject: Public cloud share holding student records
briefing.body: The Advising department set a cloud folder of student records to public so an off-site staffer could read it, and search engines began indexing it. We found it when a student googled their own name and saw a transcript link. The folder was open for about 9 days before we caught it. Confirm in the SIEM feed what was exposed and whether anyone actually pulled the data, then use the records inventory below to scope the FERPA exposure and determine the notification duty. Contain the exposure first, before you write a single word of analysis.
objectives: Confirm the exposure and whether records were downloaded
Scope the FERPA records involved
Determine the breach-notification duty and recommend notification

iocs.note: Destination records were copied to after public exposure
Address that pulled the exposed records

dossier.title: Exposed records inventory
dossier.intro: What sat in the public folder, by type and sensitivity.

dossier.title: Exposure window and indexing evidence
bullets: The folder permission changed from private to anyone-with-the-link, then to fully public, on a date 9 days before discovery.
Search-engine cache shows at least 3 transcript files were indexed and appeared in public results.
Cloud access logs show downloads from one external address that is not on any known college or partner network.
There is no DLP rule today that alerts on a folder of student records being made public.

dossier.title: Notification context
paras: FERPA does not impose a fixed breach-notification deadline, but it requires the institution to address unauthorized disclosure of education records and may require notice as a matter of policy and best practice. The 210 SSNs pull in Texas breach-notification law, which does set deadlines and may require notice to the state attorney general above a threshold of affected residents. Scope first, because the count of affected students and the data types decide which notices are mandatory versus discretionary.

deliverables: In-app investigation and assessment
Confirm the exposure in the logs, work the assessment steps, and answer the graded questions. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
FERPA breach assessment and notification memo (Word document)
Scope the records exposed from the inventory, determine the FERPA and state-law notification duty, and draft who must be told, by when, including the SSN-driven state obligations.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership on the exposure, the FERPA impact, and the notification decision.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes

Required. On camera, present what was exposed, the FERPA impact, and your notification recommendation to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What in the feed confirms records were actually taken, not just exposed?
options: A single failed login
A public-access event followed by a mass download and an outbound copy
A help-desk ticket
steps.rationale: Exposure plus a mass download and an outbound copy shows the data was actually pulled, not just visible. A failed login or a ticket does not.

steps.prompt: First containment action?
options: Email the department to fix it later
Wait and watch the access logs
Remove public access on the folder and revoke the share links
steps.rationale: Stop the exposure immediately by removing public access and killing the links. Watching or deferring lets more records walk out.

steps.prompt: Using the inventory, which records most expand the legal exposure beyond FERPA alone?
options: The 210 SSNs in advising notes, which trigger state breach law
The 12 public flyers
The appointment schedules only
steps.rationale: The SSNs add Texas breach-notification obligations on top of FERPA, including possible attorney-general notice. Flyers are public and schedules alone do not add the state PII trigger.

steps.prompt: How is the notification decision made?
options: By guessing a number
By skipping it since FERPA has no fixed deadline
By scoping whose records were exposed and applying both FERPA and state breach law
steps.rationale: Notification depends on whose regulated data was exposed and every applicable law. FERPA having no fixed clock does not remove the state-law duty created by the SSNs.

steps.prompt: What prevents a repeat of this exposure?
options: Default-private sharing, a DLP rule on public student-record links, and periodic public-link audits
Hoping it does not happen again
Deleting the cloud platform
steps.rationale: Secure-by-default sharing plus DLP and link audits stop the next exposure. The materials note there is no such DLP rule today, which is the gap to close.

questions.prompt: What confirms the records were actually taken?
options: A single 4625 event
A mass download and outbound copy after the public exposure
A slow network
questions.explain: The download plus exfiltration after exposure shows the data was pulled, not just visible. One failed-login event proves nothing.

questions.prompt: Select the two indicators to block.
options: The exfiltration domain
The pulling IP
The college's own SharePoint URL
The registrar's mailbox
questions.explain: Block the adversary's destination domain and the IP that pulled the data, never your own infrastructure.

questions.prompt: What most expands the notification duty beyond FERPA here?
options: The folder's color
The 210 exposed SSNs, which trigger state breach law
The number of files
questions.explain: The SSNs pull in Texas breach-notification law with real deadlines, on top of the FERPA education-record exposure.

questions.prompt: The folder was public for about 9 days and search-engine cache shows transcripts were indexed. What does the indexing evidence establish for the assessment?
options: That the data is misclassified
That no real exposure occurred since it was internal
That the records were publicly reachable and cached, so exposure is confirmed and the cached copies must be addressed in remediation
questions.explain: Indexed, cached transcripts prove the records were reachable by anyone, not just visible internally, and the cached copies are part of containment. The window and cache do not bear on classification.

hints: Look for the public-access event, then the mass download, then the outbound copy.
Contain by removing public access and revoking links before anything else.
Scope the inventory. The 210 SSNs are what turn a FERPA matter into a state-law deadline.

answerKey.rootCause: A misconfigured cloud folder exposed FERPA-protected student records publicly for about 9 days, search engines indexed transcripts, and an external address mass-downloaded and exfiltrated the data. The 210 SSNs in the folder add state breach-notification duties. The response removes public access, scopes the exposed records, and triggers FERPA and state breach notification.
containment: Remove public access and revoke the share links
Block the exfiltration domain and IP
Scope the exposed records and execute FERPA and state breach notification

scenarios.debrief: A misconfiguration is still a breach when data walks out the door. Contain the exposure first, prove what was actually taken in the feed, then let the scope of the regulated records, especially the SSNs, drive an honest notification decision rather than wishful thinking.

scenarios.title: Financial-Aid Phishing to GLBA Incident

briefing.subject: Compromised financial aid mailbox
briefing.body: A financial aid officer clicked a phishing link and entered credentials on a fake login page yesterday afternoon. The account now has a hidden inbox rule that auto-deletes any message containing the words wire, payment, or invoice, and a fraudulent wire request for 48,500 dollars went out to a new vendor account this morning. Aid data is GLBA-covered, so this is both a security incident and a compliance event. Confirm the compromise in the SIEM feed, use the materials to scope the GLBA data the mailbox touched, then handle containment and the GLBA incident report and notification analysis.
objectives: Confirm the account compromise and the mail rule
Scope the GLBA-covered data exposed
Handle containment and the GLBA incident and notification

iocs.note: Sender domain of the aid-office phish
Credential-harvest page that captured the login

dossier.title: Compromised account and rule details
dossier.intro: What the takeover looks like in identity and mail.

rows: Phishing link, credentials entered on fake page
Not enforced at time of compromise
Impossible travel, foreign IP same hour as local login
Auto-delete messages containing wire, payment, invoice
48,500 dollars to a new vendor account
Requested this morning, not yet cleared

dossier.title: GLBA data the mailbox could reach
dossier.intro: Customer information in scope under the GLBA Safeguards Rule.

dossier.title: GLBA incident and notification context
bullets: Under the updated GLBA Safeguards Rule, the institution must investigate and document security incidents and may have notification obligations for events affecting customer information at scale.

The hidden inbox rule is a defense-evasion technique meant to hide the fraudulent wire thread from the real owner. Removing it is part of containment, but the rule's existence is evidence for the report.
The bank and routing numbers and SSNs also trigger state breach-notification law, which has its own deadlines and thresholds.
A documented incident report is required regardless of whether the wire is recovered.

deliverables: In-app investigation and assessment
Confirm the compromise in the logs, work the assessment steps, and answer the graded questions. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
GLBA incident report (Word document)
Document the compromise, the inbox rule, the wire, the aid data exposed from the inventory, the containment, and the notification analysis under GLBA and state law.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership on the incident, the GLBA impact, and the notification decision.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the compromise, the GLBA impact, and your recommendation to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What confirms the account is compromised?
options: One spam email in the inbox
A login from an impossible location plus a hidden inbox rule the user did not set
A routine password change request
steps.rationale: Impossible-travel sign-in and an attacker-created inbox rule together confirm account takeover. A single spam message or a normal password change does not.

steps.prompt: First containment action?
options: Reset the credentials, revoke active sessions, and remove the malicious inbox rule
Email the attacker to negotiate
Wait for the wire to clear before acting
steps.rationale: Cut the attacker's access first: reset, revoke sessions, and delete the rule they planted. Waiting lets the 48,500 dollar wire clear.

steps.prompt: Using the inventory, which data drives the compliance side?
options: The public aid policy emails
Student financial aid data, including bank numbers and SSNs, which is GLBA-covered customer information
The office floor plan
steps.rationale: Aid applications, bank and routing numbers, and SSNs are GLBA-covered customer information and also trigger state PII law. Policy emails are not regulated.

steps.prompt: What determines the notification path?
options: The time of day the wire was sent
Nothing, since money was not lost yet
The GLBA-covered and state-regulated data exposed and the applicable breach laws
steps.rationale: The exposed GLBA data plus the bank numbers and SSNs under state law determine who is notified and when. The notification duty does not depend on whether the wire cleared.

steps.prompt: What reduces the chance of a repeat?
options: Removing email from the aid office entirely
Ignoring it as a one-off
Enforcing MFA, alerting on or blocking auto-delete inbox rules, and targeted training for the aid office
steps.rationale: MFA and inbox-rule controls plus targeted training address the root cause. The materials note MFA was not enforced, which is the gap to close.

questions.prompt: Account takeover here is confirmed by:
options: A single spam message
Impossible-travel login plus an attacker-created inbox rule
A slow mailbox
questions.explain: The anomalous sign-in and the hidden rule together confirm compromise. Neither alone is conclusive.

questions.prompt: Select the two indicators to block.
options: The phishing domain
The phishing URL
outlook.office365.com
The officer's mailbox
questions.explain: Block the adversary domain and URL, not legitimate infrastructure like the mail service or the victim's own mailbox.

questions.prompt: The compliance dimension here is set by:
options: HIPAA only
No regulation, since it is just email
GLBA-covered aid data, with bank numbers and SSNs adding state law
questions.explain: Financial aid data is customer information under GLBA, and the bank numbers and SSNs add state breach-notification duties. HIPAA does not apply to aid data.

questions.prompt: The hidden rule auto-deletes messages containing wire, payment, or invoice. What is its purpose, and how should it be handled?
options: To hide the fraudulent wire thread from the real owner; remove it in containment but document it as evidence
To organize the officer's inbox; leave it in place
To improve mail performance; ignore it
questions.explain: The rule is defense evasion meant to conceal the wire fraud from the victim. It is both something to delete during containment and evidence to preserve in the GLBA incident report.

hints: Pair the impossible-travel sign-in with the hidden inbox rule to confirm takeover.
Contain by resetting credentials, revoking sessions, and deleting the rule before the wire clears.
Aid data is GLBA-covered, and the bank numbers and SSNs pull in state law too.

answerKey.rootCause: A phishing email compromised a financial aid mailbox with no MFA, the attacker planted a hidden auto-delete inbox rule and pushed a fraudulent 48,500 dollar wire, and GLBA-covered aid data including bank numbers and SSNs was exposed. Containment resets access and removes the rule; the compliance side handles GLBA incident reporting and state breach notification.
containment: Reset credentials, revoke sessions, and remove the malicious inbox rule
Block the phishing domain and URL and stop the fraudulent wire
Handle GLBA incident reporting and breach notification, and enforce MFA

scenarios.debrief: A phished mailbox in the right office is both a security incident and a compliance event. Contain the takeover fast and recall the wire, then let the GLBA-covered data and the bank numbers and SSNs drive the reporting and notification work. The hidden inbox rule is both something to remove and evidence to document.

scenarios.title: Stolen Laptop with PII: Risk Determination

briefing.subject: Stolen staff laptop with student PII
briefing.body: An advisor's laptop was stolen from a parked car two nights ago. It held a local spreadsheet with about 120 student records, including names, IDs, and some grades. The asset record shows BitLocker full-disk encryption was enabled and the device was powered off at the time. Since then we see a handful of failed login attempts from an unfamiliar address against the advisor's account, but no confirmed successful logon. The core task is a breach risk determination, not a panic. Confirm the post-theft attempts in the SIEM feed, then use the device and encryption materials below to decide whether the PII was actually at risk and whether that triggers a notification. Do not default to mandatory notice without the analysis.
objectives: Confirm post-theft access attempts in the logs
Determine whether the PII was actually at risk, given encryption
Make a documented breach risk determination and notification decision

iocs.note: Unfamiliar address attempting access after the theft

dossier.title: Device and data record
dossier.intro: The asset record for the stolen laptop and what it held.

rows: BitLocker enabled and confirmed in MDM
Issued, device offline, not yet confirmed

dossier.title: Post-theft access-attempt summary
dossier.intro: What the logs show against the advisor's account after the theft.

rows: Failed login attempts from unfamiliar IP

dossier.title: Encryption safe-harbor context
paras: Most state breach-notification laws, including Texas, provide a safe harbor when the lost data was encrypted and the decryption key was not also compromised. The device was powered off, so the BitLocker keys were not resident in memory and the disk is at rest and unreadable without the recovery key. The failed logins are against the cloud account, not the disk, and none succeeded. The determination must weigh encryption status, evidence of access, and data sensitivity together, and it must be documented even when the outcome is low risk. A documented low-risk determination is defensible. Concealing a loss is not.

deliverables: In-app investigation and assessment
Confirm the access attempts in the logs, work the assessment steps, and answer the graded questions. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Breach risk determination (Word document)
Document the device, the data, the BitLocker encryption status, the failed-only access attempts, and your reasoned determination with an encryption safe-harbor analysis.
Executive brief and notification decision (Word document)
One page for leadership with your notification recommendation and the reasoning, including why the encryption safe harbor does or does not apply.
Asset and encryption attestation (Word document)
Capture the MDM attestation that BitLocker was enabled and the device was powered off, as the evidence the safe-harbor analysis relies on.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present your risk determination and notification decision to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What do the post-theft logs actually show?
options: A confirmed data download from the device
Failed access attempts from an unfamiliar location, but no confirmed success
Nothing at all
steps.rationale: The logs show attempts against the account without a confirmed successful logon and no data access. That distinction is central to the determination.

steps.prompt: Per the device record, what most reduces the risk that the PII was exposed?
options: A sticker on the lid
BitLocker full-disk encryption with the device powered off, making the data unreadable
A strong opinion that it is fine
steps.rationale: Encryption with the device off means the keys were not in memory and the disk is unreadable without the recovery key. That is the pivotal fact, not cosmetics or assertion.

steps.prompt: How is the breach risk determination made?
options: By assuming the worst with no analysis
By ignoring it since it was just a laptop
By weighing encryption, access evidence, and data sensitivity together
steps.rationale: A risk determination weighs the facts: was the data readable, was there confirmed access, how sensitive is it. Assuming the worst or ignoring it are both wrong.

steps.prompt: Given encryption, no SSNs, and no evidence of successful access, the likely outcome is:
options: A documented low-risk determination, likely qualifying for the encryption safe harbor from notification
Mandatory public notification regardless of the facts
Quietly hiding the incident
steps.rationale: Encryption with an uncompromised key provides a safe harbor under most state laws, but only with a documented determination. Mandatory notice regardless ignores the law, and hiding it is never acceptable.

steps.prompt: What prevents the next lost device from being a breach?
options: Requiring staff to never take laptops off campus
Enforced full-disk encryption everywhere and keeping PII off local spreadsheets
Relying on device tracking software to recover the laptop
steps.rationale: Encryption on every device plus keeping PII in governed systems instead of local files removes the risk next time. The local spreadsheet was the real exposure.

questions.prompt: The post-theft logs show:
options: A confirmed exfiltration
Failed attempts, no confirmed successful access
A ransom note
questions.explain: Attempts without confirmed success, and no data access, is the key fact for the determination.

questions.prompt: What most reduces breach risk for this stolen device?
options: A warranty
A loud alarm
BitLocker full-disk encryption with the device powered off
questions.explain: Encryption with the keys not in memory makes the stored data unreadable to a thief.

questions.prompt: Encryption with no evidence of access usually supports:
options: A documented low-risk determination or safe harbor
Automatic public notice
Concealing the loss
questions.explain: Encryption with an uncompromised key often provides a safe harbor, but the determination must be documented, never hidden.

questions.prompt: Why does the device being powered off at the time of theft strengthen the safe-harbor argument?
options: Because a powered-off laptop cannot be sold
Because it proves the thief was not technical
Because the BitLocker keys were not resident in memory, so the encryption key was not exposed along with the data
questions.explain: Safe harbor requires that the decryption key was not compromised with the data. Powered off means the keys were not in memory, so the disk is at rest and unreadable without the recovery key. Salability and the thief's skill are irrelevant.

hints: Separate access attempts from confirmed access. They are not the same thing.
Encryption with the device powered off is the pivotal fact in this determination.
Even a low-risk outcome must be documented with an attestation, never assumed or hidden.

answerKey.rootCause: A stolen laptop held a local spreadsheet of about 120 student records, with failed post-theft login attempts against the account but no confirmed success and no data access. The device had BitLocker full-disk encryption enabled and was powered off, so the data is unreadable. With encryption intact, no SSNs, and no confirmed access, the facts support a documented low-risk determination and likely encryption safe harbor, with prevention through enforced encryption and keeping PII off local files.
containment: Confirm encryption status and remotely wipe or lock the device
Document the risk determination weighing encryption, access evidence, and sensitivity
Enforce full-disk encryption and keep PII off local devices

scenarios.debrief: Not every lost device is a reportable breach, but every one needs a documented risk determination. Here BitLocker encryption with the device powered off, plus failed-only access attempts, supports a low-risk finding and likely safe harbor. The analysis and the attestation, not a guess and not a panic, are what protect the institution.

scenarios.title: Ransomware on the Registrar: BIA-Driven Recovery

briefing.subject: Ransomware in the registrar's office
briefing.body: Ransomware hit the registrar's office overnight, mid-semester, with grade submission deadlines this week. It moved in from a compromised host, disabled endpoint defenses, encrypted file shares and the registration database, and dropped ransom notes demanding 1.2 million dollars in cryptocurrency. Confirm the scope and entry path in the SIEM feed, then drive the recovery from the governance side. Use the business impact analysis below to decide what comes back first, confirm the backups are clean and offline, and brief leadership on the recovery plan and a documented decision on the ransom. We have immutable backups, so favor recovery over payment, but make it a reasoned call.
objectives: Confirm the ransomware scope and entry in the logs
Use the BIA to prioritize recovery order
Brief leadership on the recovery plan and the ransom decision

iocs.note: Source of lateral movement into the registrar VLAN

dossier.title: BIA recovery-priority table
dossier.intro: The registrar systems affected, with recovery objectives from the BIA.

dossier.title: Backup status
dossier.intro: The state of recovery sources, verified before any restore.

dossier.title: Ransom and decision context
bullets: Ransom demand: 1.2 million dollars in cryptocurrency, with a 72-hour deadline before a threatened leak.
Clean immutable and offline backups exist with a recovery point of about 6 hours, so most data is recoverable without paying.
Paying does not guarantee a working decryptor, may violate sanctions rules depending on the actor, and funds future attacks.
The ransom decision is a governance and legal matter, made with leadership and counsel and documented, not decided by one administrator.

deliverables: In-app investigation and assessment
Confirm the scope in the logs, work the assessment steps, and answer the graded questions. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
BIA-driven recovery plan (Word document)
Use the BIA priority table to set the recovery order, confirm clean offline backups, state the recovery point, and document the restore steps.
Executive brief and ransom decision memo (Word document)
One page for leadership on the recovery plan, the timeline against the RTOs, and the documented decision not to pay given clean backups.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the recovery plan and the ransom decision to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What confirms the scope of the ransomware?
options: A single failed login
Lateral movement, disabled defenses, mass file encryption, and ransom notes
A slow printer
steps.rationale: The chain of lateral movement, defense evasion, encryption, and ransom notes scopes the incident from entry to impact. One failed login does not.

steps.prompt: First containment action?
options: Pay the ransom immediately to stop the timer
Reboot every affected machine
Isolate affected hosts on the network without powering them off
steps.rationale: Isolate to stop spread while preserving memory and evidence. Do not rush to pay, and powering off or rebooting can destroy volatile evidence.

steps.prompt: Using the BIA table, which system is restored first?
options: The registration and records database, priority 1 with a 4-hour RTO
Departmental print and scan, the lowest priority
Whatever IT feels like restoring first
steps.rationale: The BIA sets recovery priority, and the registration database is priority 1 with the tightest RTO. Low-priority print services and personal preference do not drive order.

steps.prompt: Per the backup status, what must be true of the backups used to recover?
options: The same online snapshots, for speed
Any backup will do under time pressure
The clean immutable or offline copies, verified before restore
steps.rationale: The online snapshots were encrypted and are unusable. Restore from the clean immutable and offline copies, verified first, to avoid reinfection.

steps.prompt: Given clean backups, how is the ransom decision handled?
options: A documented decision with leadership and legal, favoring recovery from backups over payment
Pay quietly and move on before the deadline
Let the on-call admin decide alone
steps.rationale: With clean immutable backups and a 6-hour recovery point, recovery is preferred. The ransom decision is a governance and legal matter, documented, never a quiet solo call.

questions.prompt: The ransomware scope is shown by:
options: One spam email
Lateral movement, disabled defenses, encryption, and ransom notes
A reboot
questions.explain: That chain of events scopes the incident from entry to impact.

questions.prompt: Which system does the BIA say to restore first?
options: Departmental print and scan
The shared document file server
The registration and records database (priority 1, 4-hour RTO)
questions.explain: The BIA marks the registration database as priority 1 with the tightest RTO, so it comes back first.

questions.prompt: Which indicators should be blocked, and what should never be blocked?
options: Block the attacker source IP, the C2 domain, and the malware hash; never block the registrar's own servers
Block the registrar's own database server
Block nothing and just restore
questions.explain: Block the malicious source IP, C2 domain, and payload hash. Never block your own infrastructure, and blocking is still needed alongside recovery.

questions.prompt: The last clean backup ran about 6 hours before encryption. What does that 6-hour recovery point most directly support?
options: A decision to recover from backups rather than pay, since at most about 6 hours of data is lost
A decision to pay the ransom to avoid any data loss
That the backups are unusable
questions.explain: A 6-hour recovery point means only the changes since the last backup are at risk, which is recoverable and strengthens the case to restore rather than pay 1.2 million. The immutable and offline copies are clean and usable.

hints: Follow the chain: lateral movement, defense evasion, encryption, ransom note.
Isolate, do not power off, and let the BIA priority table set the recovery order.
Recover only from the clean, offline, immutable backups, never the encrypted online snapshots.

answerKey.rootCause: Ransomware moved laterally into the registrar VLAN, disabled defenses, used C2, and encrypted systems mid-semester, demanding 1.2 million dollars. Clean immutable and offline backups exist with a 6-hour recovery point. Recovery is driven by the BIA's priority order, restoring the registration database first from clean offline backups, with a documented leadership and legal decision to recover rather than pay.
containment: Isolate affected hosts without powering off, and block the C2 domain, source IP, and payload hash
Restore from clean, offline, immutable, verified backups in BIA priority order
Document the ransom decision with leadership and legal, favoring recovery

scenarios.debrief: Ransomware recovery is where the BIA earns its keep: it tells you the registration database comes back before the print server when everything is on fire. Contain without destroying evidence, recover from the clean offline backups rather than the encrypted snapshots, and make the ransom decision a documented governance call with legal, not a panicked one.
